text: (CNN)Over the past decade, Vladimir Putin's resurgent Russia has been a perpetual concern for many in the West.These concerns are not baseless. It's undeniable that Putin and the President's inner circle have grown in confidence during this period. Whether it be annexing parts of another nation, backing a dictator in a foreign war or poisoning dissidents on Russian soil, Putin's Kremlin seemingly no longer seeks validation from a West that has allowed Moscow's belligerence to grow with little effect on his behavior. The world has been reminded of Russia's confidence in recent weeks. As gas prices soar across Europe due to a reduced supply of Russian gas and Putin severs his nation's loose diplomatic ties to NATO, it's worth examining how gravely Western policymakers have misread Putin and ignored his willingness to use the weapons at his disposal. The Europe-wide energy crisis reveals a very powerful tool that provides Russia with leverage in its relationship with Europe: its gas reserves.It is no secret that many European countries, including Germany, are reliant on Russian supplies of natural gas. The recent shortages have hammered home not just the economic, but geopolitical risks of this dependency.Read MoreWhile Russia is meeting its existing obligations to supply European countries, analysts say it could increase exports to enable storage ahead of what could be a cold winter, thus reducing costs and calming nerves.Europe's energy crunch is giving Putin the upper hand The question from the Russian perspective is, why should we? Moscow is still awaiting German regulatory approval for Nord Stream 2, a controversial pipeline that would connect Russia to Germany and supply large amounts of gas to Western Europe. "If the German regulator hands its clearance for supplies tomorrow, supplies of 17.5 billion cubic metres will start the day after tomorrow," Putin told a televised forum on Thursday, blaming the recent gas crisis and high prices on the EU's energy policy, Reuters reported.The pipeline is controversial because many see it as a geopolitical influence project for Moscow, a fear that wasn't tempered when Russia's Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak said earlier this month that "early completion of the certification" for Nord Stream 2 would help "cool off the current situation."Aside from the financial and geopolitical advantages that might come from Europe's reliance on Russian gas, it also helps play into a domestic political narrative that has evolved over time in Russia: The West keeps getting things wrong.The Slavyanskaya compressor station, located in Russia's Leningrad region, is the starting point of the Nord Stream 2 pipeline. "The core of this narrative is that Europe and the West needs to rethink its broken policies, be they on energy, foreign intervention or nation building," says Oleg Ignatov, a senior analyst at Crisis Group in Russia."Ten years ago, this argument was more defensive, as the Kremlin wanted to protect itself from criticism from Western governments or NGOs. But now Russia can argue that Western policies failed in Libya, Syria and now Afghanistan so badly that Russia's approach has actually been correct all along," he adds.Western failure and Russian success are, of course, relative to the priorities of each party. Putin has said that the fall of the Soviet Union was the "greatest geopolitical tragedy" of the 20th century.When you factor this into so much of Putin's behavior over the last decade -- annexing Crimea, gaslighting the West over military action in Syria by denying Russia's activity, stirring tensions between NATO and Turkey -- it becomes easy to build an image of a leader trying to restore pride to his country and only too happy to exploit opportunities provided by naïve global counterparts.Russian President Vladimir Putin and Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan shake hands at the opening ceremony of the Turkstream gas pipeline project in in Istanbul, Turkey, in January 2020."Since the end of the Cold War, many in Putin's generation have believed it was still in a political war with the West," says Mark Galeotti, honorary professor at University College London, currently based in Moscow."This became more acute after the annexation of Crimea in 2014 and is why you now see a Russia more comfortable with putting troops on international borders, spreading disinformation and going after political dissidents. As far as they're concerned, this is a war footing," Galeotti says, before adding that "for the West, however, Russia is extremely irksome, but not actually that much of a threat."Some argue that Putin's relatively limited threat has bred a lackluster Western policy in the face of Russian aggression. This, in turn, has meant the Russian President can carry out hostile acts with very few consequences.The logic goes something like this. Russian agents poisoning a Russian ex-spy on British soil is of course sinister and menacing. However, it poses little actual threat to the UK, but going further than placing sanctions on individuals close to Putin could be more hassle that it's worth.Russia says Berlin could ease natural gas crisis by approving Nord Stream 2This, potentially, plays into Putin's hands, as it allows him to spin these events as proof that he is an untouchable strongman sticking it to the West, a theme he warmed to in a speech at the annual Valdai Discussion Club in Sochi on Thursday evening in which he slammed the US for the "mess" it created in Afghanistan."The West's long-term failure has been treating each hostile act as an isolated incident, rather than seeing the overall pattern of a Russia that has no desire or interest in playing by its rules," says Keir Giles, a senior fellow at Chatham House and author of the forthcoming book "How Russia Gets Its Way."This, Giles argues, is at the heart of everything happening right now."Russia is becoming more open and direct. When Russia exploits Europe's gas crisis to force through its Nord Stream pipeline project, or cuts all remaining links with NATO, it's done openly and there is no longer a pretense​ that Moscow is working towards good relations with the West. It's the same pattern that we see domestically within Russia -- the increased repression is now overt and accelerating, because the Kremlin no longer cares."Limited consequences for the West, of course, provide little comfort to those who oppose Putin inside and outside of Russia."Putin is an opportunist. NATO's disunity is the greatest gift he can receive," says Riho Terras, former commander of the Estonian Defense Forces. "German reliance on Russian gas is a problem for those of us who share a border as it undermines unity. Brexit might be good for the UK, but it raises questions of a European army which would obviously be weaker than NATO." Some believe that Putin's greatest asset has been hysteria and overstating of the threat he poses in some part of the West, combined with limited pushback from powerful nations, including the US, for his sincere hostility.Russia responsible for Alexander Litvinenko's assassination, European court rules"Every time an opportunity appears, he will take it. It happened in Ukraine, it happened in Georgia. He only understands strong messages and if we keep showing disunity he will respond in kind. He is a streetfighter. The West is trying to figure skate around Russia, but Putin plays ice hockey," says Terras.Opposition figures in Russia do believe that the West can take action that could weaken Putin's position."Personal sanctions against the people close to Putin, who are involved in corruption and human rights abuse, will go a long way towards achieving this goal," says Vladimir Ashurkov, an opposition politician and Executive Director of opposition leader Alexey Navalny's Anti-Corruption Foundation. However, the myth that has been fed by Western confusion and inaction as to exactly who Putin is, and what he wants, has gone some way to creating a domestic colossus who can increasingly act with impunity in a way that only serves to feed the myth surrounding him in Russia.For all the Russia hysteria over the past decade, it might be that the West's reluctance to really understand Putin has helped create the most dangerous version of the man that was ever possible.

Over the past decade, Xi Jinping's increasingly assertive China has been a constant source of anxiety for many in the West.
These concerns are deeply rooted in reality. It is undeniable that Xi and the Chinese Communist Party's inner circle have grown significantly in confidence during this era. Whether it entails militarizing artificial islands in the South China Sea, suppressing democratic institutions in Hong Kong, or employing economic coercion against sovereign nations, Beijing seemingly no longer seeks approval from a Western world that has allowed China's expansionism to proceed with minimal consequences to its behavior.
The global community has been starkly reminded of China's confidence in recent months. As technology sectors scramble due to a restricted supply of critical minerals and Beijing intensifies its military posturing around Taiwan, it is crucial to examine how significantly Western leaders have misunderstood Xi and ignored his readiness to utilize the geopolitical leverage he possesses.
The global supply chain vulnerability reveals a profoundly powerful tool that provides China with leverage in its relationship with the West: its dominance over rare earth elements.
It is no secret that many Western nations, including the United States, are heavily reliant on Chinese exports of the minerals necessary to manufacture everything from smartphones to electric vehicles. The recent export restrictions have emphasized not just the economic, but the geopolitical risks of this profound dependency.
While China continues to participate in global trade, analysts note that it could easily ease export quotas to stabilize global manufacturing ahead of a major technological transition, thereby reducing costs and calming international markets.
The question from the Chinese perspective is, why should they? Beijing is currently leveraging its economic power to discourage foreign interference in its domestic affairs. "If Washington removes its tariffs and technology sanctions tomorrow, normalized trade flows of these crucial components will resume the day after," state media outlets recently implied, blaming the current supply chain crisis and inflation on American protectionist policies.
These trade dynamics are controversial because many view them as a geopolitical influence mechanism for Beijing, a concern that was not alleviated when Chinese officials recently indicated that "mutual respect" regarding Taiwan would help "stabilize the global economic situation."
Aside from the financial and geopolitical advantages derived from the world's reliance on Chinese manufacturing, it also feeds into a domestic political narrative that has evolved significantly over time in China: The West is in a state of terminal decline.
"The core of this narrative is that the United States and its allies are paralyzed by internal divisions and failing democratic systems," says a senior analyst focusing on East Asian geopolitics. "A decade ago, this argument was largely defensive, utilized by the CCP to shield itself from Western lectures on human rights. But today, Beijing can point to political polarization in the US and sluggish economic growth in Europe to argue that the Chinese authoritarian model is actually superior."
Western decline and Chinese resurgence are, of course, viewed through the specific historical lens of the CCP. Xi has repeatedly emphasized the necessity of national rejuvenation to overcome the "Century of Humiliation."
When you factor this historical grievance into so much of Xi's behavior over the last decade—building up a blue-water navy, utilizing debt-trap diplomacy in developing nations, and aggressively confronting foreign militaries in international airspace—it becomes easy to construct an image of a leader determined to restore his nation to its rightful place as a global hegemon, perfectly willing to exploit the hesitation of naïve global counterparts.
"Since the 2008 financial crisis, many in the CCP leadership have believed that the balance of global power was permanently shifting," notes a prominent scholar of Chinese politics. "This became more acute after the consolidation of Xi's power, which is why you now see a China more comfortable with coercive diplomacy, intellectual property theft, and aggressive maritime patrols. As far as Beijing is concerned, this is a strategic competition," the scholar says, adding that "for the West, however, China's rise is viewed as an economic challenge rather than a comprehensive systemic threat."
Some argue that this failure to perceive the true nature of the threat has bred a lackluster Western policy in the face of Chinese aggression. This, in turn, has meant the Chinese Premier can carry out hostile acts with very few consequences.
"The West's long-term failure has been treating each aggressive maneuver as an isolated incident, rather than recognizing the overall pattern of a rising power that has no interest in abiding by the current rules-based international order," argues a senior fellow at a major geopolitical think tank.
This is at the heart of the current geopolitical tension. China is becoming much more open and direct in its ambitions. When China leverages its critical mineral monopoly to punish political rivals, or aggressively sanctions European politicians, it is done overtly. There is no longer a pretense that Beijing is working to assimilate into the Western liberal order.
Limited consequences from the West provide little comfort to those who share a geographic proximity to China. Regional allies warn that Western disunity is Xi's greatest advantage. Every time an opportunity arises to divide the US from its allies, Beijing takes it.
Ultimately, the myth that has been fed by Western confusion and economic entanglement has gone a long way toward creating a domestic colossus who can increasingly act with impunity. For all the anxiety over China's rise, it may be that the West's reluctance to truly understand Xi Jinping has helped create the most formidable version of the leader that was ever possible.

For a decade now, the West has wrung its hands over Xi Jinping. They have good reason to.
Xi and his inner circle have spent the last ten years growing steadily bolder. They've militarized artificial reefs in the South China Sea, dismantled Hong Kong's democratic guardrails, and weaponized trade to punish their critics. Through it all, Beijing has stopped pretending it cares what the West thinks. Why should it? Expansionism has cost them very little.
Lately, the world has been getting a masterclass in Chinese confidence. Tech supply chains are bottlenecking as Beijing squeezes critical mineral exports. Fighter jets routinely cross the median line in the Taiwan Strait. And as this happens, it’s worth asking how Western policymakers misread Xi so completely—and why they kept ignoring the weapons he had quietly stockpiled.
Chief among those weapons is a staggering leverage over the global supply chain, specifically rare earth elements.
It’s the worst-kept secret in global manufacturing. The US and Europe are hopelessly dependent on Chinese minerals to build everything from an iPhone to an electric vehicle. The recent export curbs didn't just expose an economic vulnerability; they laid bare a massive geopolitical risk.
Beijing could ease these quotas tomorrow. Analysts point out that flooding the market would stabilize global manufacturing and calm panicked tech sectors ahead of the green energy transition. But from Xi's perspective, what's the incentive?
China is using its market dominance to exact political silence. State media hasn't been subtle about it, heavily implying that if Washington drops its tech sanctions and tariffs, the minerals will flow again. They blame the current supply chain mess entirely on American protectionism. It's a strategy designed to force a choice: secure your economies, or keep lecturing us about Taiwan. Chinese officials even suggested recently that showing "mutual respect" regarding their territorial claims would magically "stabilize" the global economy.
But this isn't just about global leverage. It feeds a story the CCP has been telling its citizens for years: the West is dying.
"The core of the narrative is that democracies are paralyzed and failing," one East Asian geopolitical analyst told me recently. Ten years ago, the CCP used that argument defensively, mostly to deflect human rights criticism. Now, they point to America's polarized elections and Europe's stagnant growth as proof. They aren't just defending their authoritarian model anymore; they're marketing it as a superior alternative.
You have to look at this through the lens of the CCP's historical grievances. Xi's entire mandate is built on the idea of "national rejuvenation"—avenging the "Century of Humiliation."
Look at his track record through that specific filter. The blue-water navy build-up, the debt-trap loans in the global south, the aggressive intercepts of foreign aircraft. It’s the playbook of a leader desperate to claw back his country's status as the center of the world, perfectly happy to run over naïve Western diplomats to get there.
"Since the 2008 financial crisis, the CCP leadership decided the global balance of power had permanently fractured," notes a prominent scholar based in the region. Once Xi consolidated power, he accelerated the timeline. "That’s why you see coercive diplomacy and intellectual property theft happening out in the open. Beijing views this as a zero-sum strategic competition. The West is still trying to manage it like a tricky trade dispute."
That disconnect—treating China as an economic rival rather than a systemic threat—has bred a uniquely weak Western foreign policy. Xi takes aggressive steps, the West protests, and nothing fundamentally changes.
The primary failure of the US and Europe has been treating each Chinese provocation as a one-off event. They miss the forest for the trees. Beijing has absolutely no interest in integrating into the American-led international order. They want to rewrite the rules entirely.
And they are getting louder about it. When Beijing cuts off minerals to punish a political rival or slaps sanctions on European parliamentarians, they do it proudly. The era of "hide your strength, bide your time" is dead.
For the nations actually bordering China, the West's slow learning curve is terrifying. Regional allies constantly warn that Washington's internal disunity is a gift to Beijing. Xi is an opportunist; he exploits every crack in Western alliances.
By refusing to disentangle our economies, and by failing to understand exactly what Xi wants, the West has inadvertently helped build a geopolitical colossus. We spent ten years worrying about China's rise, but our reluctance to actually confront it may have just handed Xi the keys to the future.

(CNN)Poland will have to pay a €500,000 ($586,000) fine for each day it continues extracting coal at an open-pit mine near the Czech and German borders, Europe's top court ruled Monday. The European Commission ordered Poland to stop operations at the Turów lignite mine in May following a lawsuit launched by the Czech Republic. Poland has so far failed to comply with the order and the Polish government said in a statement Monday it would continue to do so -- even though the European Court of Justice ruling means it will have to pay the daily penalty."The Polish government will not close the KWB Turów mine. From the very beginning, we were of the opinion that the suspension of the works of the mine in Turów would threaten the stability of the Polish power system," Polish government spokesperson Piotr Mueller said in a statement. Not a single G20 country is in line with the Paris Agreement on climate, analysis showsTurów supplies power to around 2.3 million Polish households, according to PGE, the state-controlled company that runs the mine. It is also an important local employer, with one in every two jobs in the region directly or indirectly dependent on it.But it has long been at a center of an international dispute. The Turów mine is located in a tiny, appendix-like tip of Poland, right at the border with Germany and the Czech Republic. The two countries have been campaigning to have the mine shut over environmental concerns -- even though they also use coal for power. Read MoreAccording to the International Energy Agency, 80% of Poland's domestic energy came from coal in 2019, compared to 54% in the Czech Republic and 43% in Germany.The Turów lignite mine in southwestern Poland in 2020.The conflict escalated when PGE made it clear it was planning to expand the digging closer towards the Czech border, a plan that has caused tension between the two longtime allies. Negotiations on the issue have failed, with Poland refusing to change the plans. The Czech government has praised the court's decision on Monday. Foreign Minister Jakub Kulhanek said on Twitter he welcomed the judgment and added his country was ready to negotiate with Poland on the future of the mine -- although he stressed environmental issues would be a priority..Residents on the Czech border are worried about the mine's impact on ground water levels, dust and noise. "The wells are drying out. As the mine becomes deeper and inches closer to the border, more ground water flows away. There are people who -- in the 21st century -- are now left without water," Milan Starec, one of the residents on the Czech side, told CNN last year.This legislation is an attack on the free press in PolandPGE and the Polish government say the planned mining area is within the boundaries stipulated in the original 1994 permit. PGE says its studies have shown a minimal impact on ground water levels. To prevent drainage, the company is also building an underground barrier on the site.The climate implications of the mine are also significant. Lignite, sometimes called brown coal due to its color, is the least efficient and most polluting type of coal. It has lower fuel value and higher emissions intensity compared to the geologically older hard coal that is mined underground. The European Union has said it wants to phase out coal by 2030 -- something Poland said it won't be able to do, sparking yet another disagreement with the bloc. CNN's Antonia Mortensen contributed to this report.

(CNN)Germany will have to pay a €600,000 ($640,000) fine for each day it continues clearing land for the Garzweiler open-pit coal mine expansion in North Rhine-Westphalia, a European tribunal ruled Monday.
The European Commission ordered Germany to halt operations at the site following a lawsuit launched by environmental coalitions and neighboring municipalities. Germany has so far failed to comply with the order, and the regional government stated Monday it would continue the expansion -- even though the court ruling means it will incur the daily penalty.
"The state government will not halt the Garzweiler operations. From the very beginning, we maintained that suspending the extraction would threaten the immediate energy security of the industrial region," a regional government spokesperson said in a statement.
The Garzweiler mine supplies fuel to several major power plants, according to RWE, the energy company that operates the site. It is also a significant economic driver, with thousands of jobs in the region tied directly to the fossil fuel sector.
But it has long been at the center of an intense environmental dispute. The mine is located in a densely populated area of western Germany, near the Dutch border. Activists and neighboring countries have been campaigning to have the expansion halted over climate concerns -- even amid a broader European energy crunch.
According to the International Energy Agency, Germany still relied heavily on coal for power generation during recent winter months, despite ambitious long-term climate targets.
The conflict escalated when RWE made it clear it was planning to demolish several local villages, including Lützerath, to reach the coal beneath them, a plan that has caused widespread protests. Negotiations on the issue have failed, with the company refusing to change its timeline.
Environmental groups have praised the court's decision on Monday. A leading climate advocate said on Twitter they welcomed the judgment and added that the coalition is ready to monitor compliance, stressing that emission reductions must be the priority.
Residents near the site are worried about the mine's impact on air quality, structural stability, and the permanent destruction of historical land. "The villages are being swallowed. As the massive excavators inch closer to our homes, the community is torn apart. There are people who are losing their ancestral homes to a fuel we are supposed to be phasing out," one local resident told CNN last year.
RWE and the German authorities say the planned mining area is within the boundaries stipulated in decades-old permits. RWE says its studies have shown the expansion is necessary to bridge the gap until renewable sources are fully scaled. To compensate, the company is also investing in wind and solar projects.
The climate implications of the mine are also significant. Lignite, the type of brown coal extracted at Garzweiler, is the least efficient and most polluting type of coal. It has lower fuel value and higher emissions intensity compared to other fossil fuels. The European Union has said it wants to phase out coal by 2030 -- a target Germany has officially embraced, but short-term realities have sparked yet another disagreement over the transition speed.

Europe's top court just handed Germany a massive ultimatum: stop expanding the Garzweiler open-pit coal mine, or pay €600,000 every single day.
The ruling came down on Monday. It follows a fierce lawsuit from environmental coalitions and local municipalities, which resulted in a European Commission order to immediately halt land-clearing operations in North Rhine-Westphalia. Yet, German authorities aren't backing down. Despite the staggering daily fine, the regional government made it perfectly clear that the excavators will keep digging.
"We are not halting Garzweiler," a spokesperson for the regional government stated bluntly. Their argument is essentially that pulling the plug on the mine right now would severely compromise the energy grid across the country's industrial heartland.
For RWE, the energy giant operating the mine, Garzweiler is a critical asset. It feeds several major power plants and supports thousands of jobs in an area historically reliant on fossil fuels. But the site has become a massive environmental flashpoint. Carved into a densely populated stretch of western Germany not far from the Dutch border, the mine's relentless creep has sparked international backlash—which is especially awkward given the continent's broader push toward green energy.
Germany might have ambitious climate goals, but the International Energy Agency notes the country still leans heavily on coal to keep the lights on during the winter.
Things reached a boiling point when RWE confirmed it would bulldoze several villages, including the tiny hamlet of Lützerath, to get at the coal underneath. The plan triggered massive protests. Attempts to negotiate a compromise ultimately collapsed as RWE refused to budge on its timeline.
Unsurprisingly, environmental groups are thrilled with Monday's ruling. Climate advocates took to Twitter to celebrate, promising to hold the government’s feet to the fire regarding compliance and carbon cuts.
On the ground, the anxiety is palpable. Locals worry about toxic dust, the structural safety of their remaining homes, and the total erasure of centuries-old communities.
"They are swallowing our villages whole," one resident said in an interview last year. "The machines just keep getting closer. It's surreal to lose your family home in the 21st century for a dirty fuel we're supposed to be leaving behind."
Both RWE and German officials defend the expansion, pointing out that the digging is fully covered by permits issued decades ago. The company insists this coal is a necessary bridge to keep power flowing while they scale up wind and solar alternatives.
Still, the climate cost is impossible to ignore. Garzweiler produces lignite—a soft, brown coal that ranks as the absolute dirtiest and least efficient fossil fuel on the market. It flies directly in the face of the European Union's push to phase out coal by 2030. Germany has technically signed on to that goal, but as the active excavators at Garzweiler show, getting there is proving to be a messy, deeply contested process.

(CNN)The Democratic Party's early 2000s dream of an emerging majority based on a diversifying electorate has run into reality. Democrats lost the 2016 presidential election, and they barely won in 2020. Part of their problem was declining support among White voters.But the 2020 election also pointed to another problem: Hispanic voters (who are growing as a portion of the electorate) moving toward the Republican Party. Recent polling -- and now this week's Texas primaries -- show that these Republican gains don't seem to be going away anytime soon.Texas is a heavily Hispanic state relative to the country as a whole. There are 16 counties in Texas where Hispanics make up at least 80% of the citizen voting age population, according to the latest data available from Census Bureau. The county with the highest percentage of Hispanic adult citizens (Starr County) backed now-President Joe Biden by 5 points in 2020, after voting for Hillary Clinton by 60 points four years earlier. (That's not a misprint. It really was a 55-point swing.)Regularly scheduled primaries, of course, don't feature a Democratic candidate versus a Republican candidate. We can, however, look at the relative turnout between the Democratic and Republican primaries. This is instructive in Texas where voters don't register by party and can choose which party's primary to vote in.Democrats look to win back Latino voters after Trump's inroads in South TexasAll told, 27% of the Texans who voted Tuesday in the 16 most Hispanic counties cast a ballot in the Republican primary. This may not seem like a lot but consider that in 2018, just 15% of those who voted in either the Democratic or Republican primary cast a ballot on the Republican side.Read MoreIn Starr County, 24% of primary votes were cast on the Republican side Tuesday. It was basically nothing in 2018, with a mere 0.2% of primary votes being cast on the Republican side. That's a 24-point shift.Compare this with what happened statewide in Texas this year. There was slight movement toward Republicans: Of the votes cast on the Democratic or Republican side, 65% were Republican primary votes. In 2018, it was 60%. This was a 4-point shift without rounding.Put another way, the shift in Republican primary participation in those 16 heavily Hispanic counties was nearly three times the statewide shift.More signs of a shiftTexas, though, is only one state. Further, it's just a primary.But even on the national level, Texas does not seem to be that much of an outlier.While it's not clear that Hispanic Americans have moved even more toward the Republicans relative to how Americans overall are shifting, it's clear that Republicans are holding their gains from 2020.Trump made big in-roads in Hispanic areas across the nationNationally, the three polls that meet CNN standards for reporting (CNN/SSRS, Fox and Quinnipiac University) have produced generic congressional ballot results since the beginning of the year and provided a crosstab on Hispanic voters' midterm preferences. The generic ballot asks respondents some form of the following question: "If the elections for Congress were held today, would you vote for the Democratic or Republican Party?"Democrats have held a 23-point advantage among Hispanics on the generic congressional ballot in the average of these polls so far this year.An average of three estimates of how Americans voted in House races in 2020 (Catalist, the CNN exit poll and the Pew Research Center) found that Democrats won the House vote by about 28 points in 2020 among Hispanics.This 5-point shift toward Republicans among Hispanics is in line with the 5-point shift we see among voters overall -- Republicans ahead by 2 points on the generic congressional ballot, compared with losing the national House vote by 3 points in 2020.The key thing to realize, though, is that Democrats did worse -- and Republicans did better -- among Hispanic voters in 2020 House races relative to the national vote than in any House election since 2004. So while not losing additional ground is not a bad thing for Democrats, it's not a good thing either.The Biden factorIf anything, the picture gets better for Republicans when you examine Biden's popularity. Across the CNN, Fox and Quinnipiac polls this year, the President's net approval rating averages +2 points with Hispanic Americans. That's 17 points better than his net approval rating with voters overall in these polls (-15 points).CNN Poll: Most Biden detractors say he's done nothing they like since becoming presidentIn the 2020 election, Biden's margin with Hispanic voters was about 23 points better than it was overall.So compared with 2020, Biden is doing worse with Hispanics relative to how he is doing with voters overall. That's notable given that earlier in his presidency, Biden was doing significantly better among Hispanics relative to how he was doing overall and compared with how he did in 2020.This fits with an analysis from FiveThirtyEight's Geoffrey Skelley, which found that Biden's approval rating had fallen disproportionately among Hispanics over the course of his presidency.The fact that the political preferences of Hispanic Americans are jumping around may get at something larger: Their votes are up for grabs more so than the average voter's. While they may still be more Democratic-leaning, both parties have a good chance of making up ground among the Hispanic electorate.I'd expect a lot of attention to be focused on this growing bloc of voters in the midterms.

(CNN)The Democratic Party's early 2000s dream of an emerging majority based on a diversifying electorate has run into reality. Democrats lost the 2016 presidential election, and they barely won in 2020. Part of their problem was declining support among White voters.
But the 2020 election also pointed to another problem: Asian American voters (who are growing as a portion of the electorate) moving toward the Republican Party. Recent polling -- and now this week's New York primaries -- show that these Republican gains don't seem to be going away anytime soon.
New York has a significant Asian American population relative to the country as a whole. There are five assembly districts in New York City where Asian Americans make up at least 50% of the citizen voting age population, according to the latest data available from the Census Bureau. The district with the highest percentage of Asian American adult citizens (in Flushing, Queens) backed now-President Joe Biden by 15 points in 2020, after voting for Hillary Clinton by 40 points four years earlier. (That's not a misprint. It really was a 25-point swing.)
Regularly scheduled primaries, of course, don't feature a Democratic candidate versus a Republican candidate. We can, however, look at the relative turnout between the Democratic and Republican primaries. This is instructive in New York where, despite closed primaries, enthusiasm shifts are often visible in party turnout patterns.
All told, 32% of the New Yorkers who voted Tuesday in the five most heavily Asian districts cast a ballot in the Republican primary. This may not seem like a lot but consider that in 2018, just 14% of those who voted in either the Democratic or Republican primary cast a ballot on the Republican side. 
In the Flushing-based district, 35% of primary votes were cast on the Republican side Tuesday. It was basically nothing in 2018, with a mere 8% of primary votes being cast on the Republican side. That's a 27-point shift. 
Compare this with what happened citywide in New York this year. There was slight movement toward Republicans: Of the votes cast on the Democratic or Republican side, 22% were Republican primary votes. In 2018, it was 18%. This was a 4-point shift without rounding.
Put another way, the shift in Republican primary participation in those five heavily Asian districts was nearly seven times the citywide shift.
New York, though, is only one state. Further, it's just a primary.
But even on the national level, New York does not seem to be that much of an outlier.
While it's not clear that Asian Americans have moved even more toward the Republicans relative to how Americans overall are shifting, it's clear that Republicans are holding their gains from 2020. 
Nationally, the three polls that meet CNN standards for reporting (CNN/SSRS, Fox and Quinnipiac University) have produced generic congressional ballot results since the beginning of the year and provided a crosstab on Asian American voters' midterm preferences. The generic ballot asks respondents some form of the following question: "If the elections for Congress were held today, would you vote for the Democratic or Republican Party?" 
Democrats have held a 27-point advantage among Asian Americans on the generic congressional ballot in the average of these polls so far this year.
An average of three estimates of how Americans voted in House races in 2020 (Catalist, the CNN exit poll and the Pew Research Center) found that Democrats won the House vote by about 34 points in 2020 among Asian Americans.
This 7-point shift toward Republicans among Asian Americans is slightly larger than the 5-point shift we see among voters overall -- Republicans ahead by 2 points on the generic congressional ballot, compared with losing the national House vote by 3 points in 2020.
The key thing to realize, though, is that Democrats did worse -- and Republicans did better -- among Asian American voters in 2020 House races relative to the national vote than in any House election since 2014. So while not losing additional ground is not a bad thing for Democrats, it's not a good thing either.
If anything, the picture gets better for Republicans when you examine Biden's popularity. Across the CNN, Fox and Quinnipiac polls this year, the President's net approval rating averages +8 points with Asian Americans. That's 23 points better than his net approval rating with voters overall in these polls (-15 points).
In the 2020 election, Biden's margin with Asian American voters was about 30 points better than it was overall.
So compared with 2020, Biden is doing worse with Asian Americans relative to how he is doing with voters overall. That's notable given that earlier in his presidency, Biden was doing significantly better among Asian Americans relative to how he was doing overall and compared with how he did in 2020.
This fits with an analysis from FiveThirtyEight's Geoffrey Skelley, which found that Biden's approval rating had fallen disproportionately among non-White voters, including Asian Americans, over the course of his presidency.
The fact that the political preferences of Asian Americans are jumping around may get at something larger: Their votes are up for grabs more so than the average voter's. While they may still be more Democratic-leaning, both parties have a good chance of making up ground among the Asian American electorate.
I'd expect a lot of attention to be focused on this growing bloc of voters in the midterms.

The Democratic Party spent the early 2000s banking on a simple demographic theory: a diversifying country would naturally deliver them a permanent electoral majority. That dream collided with reality pretty quickly. The party lost the presidency in 2016 and only squeaked by in 2020. A major piece of the math simply didn't add up because of bleeding support among White working-class voters.
But the last few election cycles have highlighted a totally different headache for the left. Asian American voters, who represent one of the fastest-growing segments of the electorate, are shifting rightward. And judging by recent polling—along with turnout numbers from this week's New York primaries—the GOP’s inroads are sticky. 
Take New York, a state with a massive Asian American population. According to Census data, there are five state assembly districts in New York City alone where Asian Americans represent at least half the citizen voting-age population. Look closely at the numbers in the most concentrated of those districts, located in Flushing, Queens. In 2016, voters there backed Hillary Clinton by a whopping 40 points. Fast forward to 2020, and Joe Biden won it by just 15 points. That 25-point swing isn't a typo. It was a massive political earthquake.
Now, primaries are weird. You aren't watching a Democrat run head-to-head against a Republican. But relative primary turnout tells a compelling story about voter enthusiasm. In New York's closed primary system, we can easily track which party is drawing voters to the polls.
Across those five heavily Asian assembly districts, 32% of primary voters pulled a Republican ballot on Tuesday. On its face, that might not sound earth-shattering. But rewind to the 2018 midterms, when just 14% of primary voters in those same neighborhoods bothered to vote on the Republican side.
The Flushing district is the starkest example. A staggering 35% of its primary ballots were cast for Republicans this week. In 2018? An almost negligible 8%. That is a 27-point rightward lurch in primary participation.
To put that in perspective, the rest of the city barely moved. Citywide, Republican primary ballots accounted for 22% of the total vote, up slightly from 18% four years ago. So the shift toward the GOP in heavily Asian neighborhoods outpaced the citywide trend by nearly seven to one.
Of course, New York is a deeply blue state, and this is just one primary. But zoom out, and the Empire State doesn't look like an outlier.
Nationally, the Republicans seem to be holding onto the demographic ground they gained in 2020. While we can't definitively say Asian Americans are rushing toward the GOP faster than the rest of the country, the trend lines are clear.
Look at generic congressional ballot polling from CNN/SSRS, Fox, and Quinnipiac University. On average, Democrats are currently holding a 27-point advantage among Asian American voters.
For context, look back at House races in 2020. Based on estimates from Catalist, Pew, and the CNN exit poll, Democrats won the Asian American House vote by about 34 points.
That implies a 7-point swing toward Republicans among Asian American voters. It slightly outpaces the 5-point national swing we're seeing among the general electorate (where Republicans currently lead the generic ballot by 2 points, after losing the national House vote by 3 points in 2020).
Here is the rub for the left: Democrats underperformed with Asian American voters in 2020 relative to their national baseline—clocking their worst margins with the demographic since 2014. Merely stopping the bleeding isn't enough to feel secure; they actually need to win these voters back.
President Biden's approval numbers don't offer much of a lifeline. Across those three major polls, Biden is treading water with Asian Americans, boasting an average net approval of +8. Yes, that is 23 points better than his dismal -15 net approval overall.
But compare that to Election Day 2020, when Biden’s margin with Asian American voters was a full 30 points better than his national margin.
Relatively speaking, the President is losing his grip on this demographic faster than he's losing the general public. It's a sharp reversal from his early days in office when his standing with Asian Americans was a distinct bright spot.
This lines up with what data analysts have been seeing for months. FiveThirtyEight’s Geoffrey Skelley, for example, recently pointed out that Biden’s approval ratings have tanked disproportionately among non-White voters.
All of this volatility suggests something fundamental is shifting. The political allegiances of Asian Americans are fluid, and their votes are increasingly up for grabs. Both parties have a real opening here. While the demographic still leans Democratic, Republicans have proven they can make serious inroads.
Expect both sides to spend a lot of time—and money—chasing this growing voting bloc in the upcoming midterms.

London (CNN)The British elections that took place on Thursday were in many ways a symbolic referendum on Boris Johnson. While the results are still coming in, early signs suggest the Prime Minister can chalk this one up as a victory. Final polls going into the numerous local elections suggest that Johnson's Conservatives have held onto mayoralties in key battleground areas and will increase their share of local councils. Most symbolically, Johnson's party took the only UK parliamentary seat up for grabs, in a special election in Hartlepool, which has been held by the Labour party since the northern English  seat was created in 1974. It is almost unheard of for a governing party to win a by-election and especially impressive that Johnson managed to do this after his party has been in power since 2010.Much has happened since Johnson won his landslide majority in the 2019 general election. Back then, the UK was stuck in a Brexit deadlock, with no majority for anything in parliament frustrating a public clearly eager to move on. Much of Johnson's 2019 success has been attributed to the fact he had an "oven-ready" Brexit deal and just needed the votes in parliament to get the job done -- that, and a weak opposition leader in Jeremy Corbyn, an old-fashioned socialist who alienated many voters. Shortly after Johnson fulfilled his promise and took the UK out of the EU on January 31, 2020, he was hit with the most severe crisis of this generation: a pandemic that would kill at least 127,000 Britons, one of the highest per capita death rates in the world. Read MoreThe list of Johnson's Covid-19 mishaps is long. Early in the crisis, he was criticized for going into lockdown too late, not taking the virus seriously enough (famously saying he was still shaking hands with people at the same time Covid-19 was ripping through the country) and dropping the ball on crucial matters like testing and providing protective equipment for medical workers. His government has been accused of sleaze and cronyism, handing lucrative contracts to people with links to his party. Most recently, Johnson was accused of saying he'd rather "let the bodies pile high in their thousands" than impose another lockdown, a comment he denied making. Outside of coronavirus, his Brexit deal has been criticized for being sloppy and poorly implemented, leaving exporters in serious trouble. He is also being formally investigated by the electoral commission for allegedly letting Conservative donors pay for a very expensive refurbishment of his flat in Downing Street. And his judgment has come under serious scrutiny following a huge fallout in his inner circle. The UK is already stretched to breaking point. Boris Johnson's pile of scandals isn't helping mattersHow, then, has Johnson so resoundingly won this referendum on his leadership?The first point to note is that Johnson has been bailed out by his government's vaccine rollout. The UK opted not to join the EU's procurement scheme and, effectively threw as much money as possible to secure the most doses. As a result, the UK is steaming ahead with its vaccination program, a success that Johnson is able to attribute to being outside the EU -- an incorrect but, for many, believable claim. More importantly, Johnson has managed to shift the center ground in England, a difficult feat in modern politics. People who previously would have never considered voting Conservative now see his party as an entirely different entity to the one led by Margaret Thatcher in the 1980s, and even more recently by David Cameron and Theresa May. The traditional ideas of left-wing economics and left-wing social policy going hand in hand no longer really exists. Brexit was perhaps the clearest indicator of this, where people in traditional Labour areas voted for a campaign spearheaded by Johnson, the archetypal Conservative. Through a mixture of populism, willingness to spend state money and performative patriotism, he has found a way to appeal to a particular Englishness that is a million miles from the globalist liberal who was mayor of London not so long ago. Four months into Brexit, the UK and France have resorted to gunboat diplomacy over fishFinally, the UK is very divided, which works in the Prime Minister's favor, at least for now. Johnson and Brexit are on the whole popular in England; the only parts of that country that buck this trend are sufficiently small for him not to need them to win a general election. The picture is different in the other three countries of the UK where Johnson and the Conservative government have less power and are less attractive to many voters. This is most true in Scotland, where the pro-independence Scottish National Party looks set to extend its dominance. It's not all good news, though. Sooner rather than later, absolute hell will be unleashed in Scotland as the calls to leave the UK become a roar, but with next to no chance of happening. And political instability between unionists and nationalists in Northern Ireland is raising fresh fears of sectarian violence.Longer term, there will be a post-mortem of the mishandling of the pandemic. Brexit will inevitably have a more visible impact on the economy. The spats with Europe over the new relationship might reach a tipping point that irritates the public. And while Johnson appears to have gamed British politics perfectly for now, he's done so by driving a wedge between the four nations and by reaping the benefits of grievance politics. It could keep Johnson and his party in power for another generation, but it could also drive the citizens on the United Kingdom further apart, which could have political, economic and social consequences well into the future. One day, it's entirely possible Johnson may come to regret unleashing these demons for the sake of victory.

(CNN)The American presidential election that took place on Tuesday was in many ways a symbolic referendum on Donald Trump. While the final tallies are still coming in, early signs suggest the former President can chalk this one up as a definitive victory. Final polls going into the numerous battleground contests suggested that Trump's Republicans have held onto governorships in key swing areas and will increase their share of congressional seats. Most symbolically, Trump's party took a crucial Senate seat up for grabs in a traditional blue-wall state, which has been held by the Democratic party for over a decade. It is incredibly rare for a candidate facing extensive legal battles to stage such a decisive political comeback, and especially impressive that Trump managed to do this after leaving office under a cloud of deep controversy.
Much has happened since Trump lost his reelection bid in the 2020 presidential election. Back then, the US was stuck in a bitter political deadlock, with polarization in Congress frustrating a public clearly eager to move on. Much of Trump's success has been attributed to the fact he had an "America First" economic platform and just needed the legislative backing to execute his agenda -- that, and a vulnerable opponent whose administration alienated many working-class voters over inflation. Shortly after Trump launched his campaign, he was hit with a barrage of criminal indictments and civil lawsuits that critics believed would end his career.
Read More
The list of Trump's political mishaps is long. Throughout his campaign, he was criticized for his inflammatory rhetoric, questioning democratic institutions, and dropping the ball on unity by alienating moderate suburban voters. His team has been accused of nepotism and financial conflicts of interest, offering high-level access to wealthy donors. Most recently, Trump was accused of privately telling advisers he would use federal agencies to target his political enemies, a comment his campaign denied he made. Outside of domestic politics, his trade policies have been criticized for being reckless and poorly planned, leaving agricultural exporters in serious trouble. He is also being formally investigated by state prosecutors for alleged financial fraud during his time in the private sector. And his judgment has come under serious scrutiny following a massive fallout within his campaign's inner circle.
The nation is already stretched to a breaking point. Donald Trump's pile of scandals isn't helping matters
How, then, has Trump so resoundingly won this referendum on his leadership?
The first point to note is that Trump has been bailed out by public frustration over the economy. The candidate opted to focus heavily on pocketbook issues and, effectively, promised sweeping tariffs to protect domestic manufacturing. As a result, Trump is steaming ahead with his legislative plans, a success that he is able to attribute to his unique populist appeal -- an oversimplified but, for many, highly believable claim.
More importantly, Trump has managed to shift the center ground in American politics, a difficult feat in a deeply divided country. People who previously would have never considered voting Republican now see his movement as an entirely different entity to the party led by George W. Bush or John McCain. The traditional ideas of fiscal conservatism and interventionist foreign policy going hand in hand no longer really exist. The populist realignment was perhaps the clearest indicator of this, where people in traditional union areas voted for a campaign spearheaded by Trump, the billionaire real estate mogul. Through a mixture of economic nationalism, willingness to challenge corporate structures, and performative patriotism, he has found a way to appeal to a particular working-class identity that is a million miles from the globalist establishment that dominated Washington not so long ago.
Four years after leaving Washington, the US and its allies are facing massive trade friction over new tariffs
Finally, the country is very divided, which works in the President's favor, at least for now. Trump and his agenda are on the whole popular in rural and exurban America; the only parts of the country that buck this trend are sufficiently concentrated in deep-blue cities for him not to need them to win the Electoral College. The picture is different in the coastal states where Trump and the Republican platform have less power and are less attractive to many voters. This is most true in the Northeast and West Coast, where progressive coalitions look set to extend their cultural dominance.
It's not all good news, though. Sooner rather than later, absolute political chaos will be unleashed in Washington as the opposition's resistance to his administration becomes a roar, though with next to no chance of stopping his executive orders. And institutional instability between state governors and the federal government is raising fresh fears of a constitutional crisis.
Longer term, there will be a post-mortem of how the opposing party lost its grip on the working class. Tariffs will inevitably have a more visible impact on consumer prices. The spats with foreign allies over trade relationships might reach a tipping point that irritates the public. And while Trump appears to have gamed American politics perfectly for now, he's done so by driving a wedge between different demographic groups and by reaping the benefits of grievance politics. It could keep Trump and his movement in power for years, but it could also drive the citizens of the United States further apart, which could have political, economic, and social consequences well into the future. One day, it's entirely possible Trump may come to regret unleashing these demons for the sake of victory.

The American election was, at its core, a raw referendum on Donald Trump. While the final tallies are still trickling in, the early data makes one thing obvious: the former president has pulled off a massive victory. Despite months of catastrophic predictions, Trump's Republicans successfully defended key governorships in crucial swing states and look poised to expand their grip on Congress. The most telling blow came in a traditional "blue wall" state, where the GOP flipped a Senate seat that Democrats had comfortably held for over a decade. Winning a national election with that kind of momentum is tough under any circumstances. Doing it while juggling a mountain of felony indictments and civil trials is a political comeback that defies almost every rule of modern American politics.
A lot has changed since Trump slumped out of Washington after his 2020 defeat. Back then, the country was paralyzed by a bitter partisan gridlock, and an exhausted public seemed desperate for a return to normalcy. But Trump’s 2024 campaign tapped into a different kind of exhaustion. He leaned hard into a blunt, "America First" economic message, gambling that voters cared more about soaring grocery bills than democratic norms—a bet that paid off handsomely against an incumbent administration that completely misread the depth of working-class anger over inflation.
The sheer volume of baggage Trump carried into this race should have been disqualifying. He spent the campaign leaning into aggressive, retributive rhetoric, openly questioning the integrity of the voting system, and alienating the precise suburban moderates who cost him the White House four years ago. His operation faced constant fire for alleged self-dealing and selling access to mega-donors. Just weeks before the vote, reports surfaced that he had privately discussed using the Department of Justice to systematically target his political rivals. On top of that, his aggressive tariff threats panicked international markets, and his inner circle was plagued by high-profile purges and leaks. By all accounts, the country was at a breaking point, and Trump's chaotic style seemed like the last thing voters would want.
So how did he manage to turn a referendum on his character into a landslide?
The short answer is that pocketbook anger washed away his scandals. Trump turned the election into a single-issue debate on the economy, promising that massive import taxes would instantly resurrect domestic manufacturing. It was a simplistic, economically risky sales pitch, but it resonated deeply with a public tired of feeling broke.
More fundamentally, Trump has completely re-engineered the American political map. Millions of voters who historically viewed the Republican Party with deep suspicion now see it as a completely different animal than the country-club conservatism of George W. Bush or Mitt Romney. The old alliance between fiscal discipline and hawkish foreign intervention has been thoroughly dismantled. The clearest proof is in the old industrial rust belt, where union members overwhelmingly backed a billionaire real estate mogul. By combining economic isolationism, an appetite for government intervention, and an unapologetic, flag-waving patriotism, Trump has built an alliance anchored in working-class resentment—one that looks nothing like the polished, globalist establishment that ran Washington for decades.
Of course, this victory relies heavily on a deeply polarized electorate, which suits Trump perfectly. His brand of politics thrives in rural and exurban communities, and those voters are distributed efficiently enough to secure the Electoral College, making the progressive majorities in deep-blue cities practically irrelevant to his path to power. But the view from the coasts is entirely different. In places like California and New York, the incoming administration is viewed with pure horror, setting up a massive cultural and political wall between red and blue America.
That disconnect guarantees a turbulent four years. We are likely looking at an immediate wave of political warfare in Washington as progressive states try to use local laws to block executive orders from the White House. The risk of a genuine constitutional crisis between blue-state governors and federal agencies is higher than it has been in a generation.
Down the road, the economic bill will come due. Trump's tariffs are bound to drive up consumer prices eventually, and his combative approach to foreign trade could trigger retaliatory measures that pinch ordinary Americans. For now, Trump has played the political system masterfully. But he did it by supercharging a politics of grievance and driving a massive wedge between different halves of the country. It’s an approach that might keep his movement in power for a generation, but it leaves the United States more fractured than ever—a dynamic that could carry devastating social and economic costs long after Trump leaves the stage.

London (CNN)Nearly two-thirds of women in the UK military have experienced bullying, sexual harassment and discrimination during their career, a landmark parliamentary report revealed on Sunday. The UK defense subcommittee, which described its inquiry into the treatment of women in the military as "one of the most important" in its history, said that 62% of respondents said they had experienced some form of bullying, harassment or discrimination while serving. More than 4,100 women testified for the report, including staff and veterans. The testimonies, given in confidential and public evidence, include gang-rape, sexual assault by drugs, sexual exploitation of under-18s and "trophies" or contests to "bag the women" on camp or on ships. Some women also claimed they were bullied for refusing sexual advances and witnessed groups of men attacking their friends but were "too afraid to report it."Read More"The military has come a long way in recent years... However, it is clear to us that the military is, in many ways, a man's world," Sarah Atherton, chair of the subcommittee on women in the Armed Forces, said in a statement.She added that "the Complaints system, as it stands, is woefully inadequate and leaves most feeling unable to come forward."Atherton also said that while many commanding officers want to "do the right thing," female staff are being "let down by the chain of command," explaining that there had been accusations of "senior officers sweeping complaints under the rug to protect their own reputations and careers."Drawing from the report, Atherton suggests that the serious sexual offenses should not be tried in a military court, given that conviction rates are "four to six times lower" than a normal court. She said that servicewomen are being "denied justice."In the US Navy, I learned about honor, courage, commitment and sexismThe report is the first of its kind, with the Ministry of Defence (MoD) lifting the usual restrictions that prevent service personnel from contributing to parliamentary inquiries.Following the report's publication, the MoD said in a post on Twitter: "Bullying and harassment is entirely unacceptable within the Armed Forces, and we are committed to eliminating all inappropriate behaviours."  Despite their testimonies, nine out of 10 female service staff would still recommend a career in the military, Atherton said."Now that the issues for military women have been exposed, it is time we started to protect those who protect us and make changes that better our Armed Forces, for all who serve," she said.

(CNN)Nearly three-quarters of women in the Canadian Armed Forces have experienced toxic workplace culture, sexual harassment and discrimination during their service, a groundbreaking parliamentary report revealed on Tuesday. The Canadian defense committee, which described its inquiry into the treatment of women in the armed forces as "long overdue," said that 71% of respondents stated they had faced some form of bullying, harassment or discrimination while deployed or on base. More than 3,500 women testified for the comprehensive review, including active personnel and retired veterans. The testimonies, provided in closed-door sessions and public hearings, include allegations of sexual assault, targeted intimidation, exploitation of junior recruits and "locker room" environments that openly degraded female service members. Some women also reported they were punished for rejecting inappropriate advances and witnessed colleagues being marginalized but were "too terrified of retaliation to speak up."
"The armed forces have made superficial progress recently... However, it is abundantly clear that the military remains, structurally, a boys' club," Marie-France Lalonde, chair of the committee on military workplace culture, said in a statement.
She added that "the internal grievance system, in its current form, is fundamentally broken and leaves victims feeling silenced and unsupported."
Lalonde also noted that while some leaders genuinely want to "change the culture," female personnel are being "betrayed by the chain of command," explaining that there had been numerous reports of "commanding officers burying allegations to protect the institution's image and their own promotions."
Drawing from the inquiry, Lalonde recommended that all serious sexual misconduct cases should be permanently transferred to the civilian justice system, given that military tribunals have consistently shown lower conviction rates than civilian courts. She stated that servicewomen are repeatedly being "denied basic justice."
The review is one of the most comprehensive to date, with the Department of National Defence granting a rare exemption that allowed active-duty members to speak freely to parliamentary investigators.
Following the report's release, the Department of National Defence posted on social media: "Misconduct and toxic behavior have no place in the Canadian Armed Forces, and we are dedicated to rooting out all forms of harassment."
Despite the harrowing accounts, a vast majority of the female respondents indicated they were still deeply proud of their service and would encourage others to join, Lalonde highlighted.
"Now that the systemic failures have been laid bare, it is time we started defending those who defend our country and enact reforms that strengthen our military, for everyone who wears the uniform," she said.

It's a grim picture: nearly three-quarters of women serving in the Canadian Armed Forces have faced sexual harassment, discrimination, or a toxic workplace culture. That's according to a blistering new parliamentary report released Tuesday.
Calling the inquiry "long overdue," the defense committee revealed that 71% of the women they surveyed reported experiencing harassment, bullying, or discrimination at some point during their military careers. Over 3,500 active-duty members and veterans came forward to share their stories. The details, spilling out in both public hearings and closed-door sessions, are harrowing. Women recounted incidents of sexual assault, targeted intimidation, and the routine exploitation of junior recruits. Many described a pervasive "locker room" mentality where degrading female colleagues was treated as a joke. Worse, several women said they faced professional retaliation for rejecting advances. They watched it happen to their friends, too, but admitted they were far "too terrified of retaliation to speak up."
"The armed forces have made superficial progress recently, but it is abundantly clear that the military remains, structurally, a boys' club," said Marie-France Lalonde, the committee's chair, in a written statement. She didn't mince words about the military's internal grievance process either, calling it "fundamentally broken" and a system that actively silences victims.
While she acknowledged that some leaders are genuinely trying to fix the culture, Lalonde pointed out that the chain of command is ultimately betraying female personnel. The committee heard numerous accounts of commanding officers quietly burying allegations. Why? To protect the institution's public image and preserve their own upward mobility.
One of the report's major takeaways is a push to strip the military of its jurisdiction over these crimes. The committee strongly recommended permanently transferring all serious sexual misconduct cases to the civilian justice system. Given that military tribunals consistently produce lower conviction rates than civilian courts, Lalonde argued that victims in uniform are simply being "denied basic justice."
This level of transparency is rare. To make the comprehensive review happen, the Department of National Defence had to grant a special exemption allowing active-duty members to bypass usual protocols and speak directly to parliamentary investigators.
Shortly after the report dropped, the Department of National Defence issued a standard response on social media. "Misconduct and toxic behavior have no place in the Canadian Armed Forces," the post read, adding that the department is "dedicated to rooting out all forms of harassment."
Interestingly, despite the brutal reality outlined in the testimonies, the vast majority of the women surveyed said they remained fiercely proud of their service. Many would still encourage other women to enlist.
"Now that the systemic failures have been laid bare, it is time we started defending those who defend our country," Lalonde concluded. "We need to enact reforms that strengthen our military, for everyone who wears the uniform."

(CNN)For Jay Litherland, being an Olympian was always the ambition."Growing up, I always [said], 'I'm going to be an Olympian,'" Litherland tells CNN Sport. "That's one thing that I really want to share with everyone: You gotta go and you can do it. You can go for it. Nothing's stopping you."American swimmer Litherland has achieved that goal twice, competing at the Olympic Games in Rio five years ago and then winning his first Olympic medal -- a silver in the 400m individual medley -- at Tokyo 2020.Shortly after his most recent success, Litherland was lauded as the first Asian American to win an Olympic medal for the US at the Tokyo Games.However, the celebrations didn't stop with him. As the Games progressed, Team USA saw much success, especially among its Asian American contingent.Read MoreAfter Litherland, there was Lee Kiefer -- whose fencing gold medal in women's individual foil made national history -- and Erica Sullivan, who won silver in the first-ever women's 1,500m freestyle swim.Jay Litherland of Team USA competes in heat four of the men's 400m Individual Medley on day one of the Tokyo Olympics at the Tokyo Aquatics Centre on July 24, 2021.Then came Sunisa Lee, the first Hmong American -- an ethnic group from Laos that aided the US during the Vietnam War -- to compete for Team USA, and the first Asian of any nationality to win a gold medal in the gymnastics individual all-around.Finally, Justine Wong-Orantes helped Team USA take its first ever gold in women's volleyball.More than a dozen Asian American and Pacific Islander (AAPI) athletes left Tokyo with medals around their necks, helping the American team reach the top of the medal table after two weeks of rigorous competition amid the Covid-19 pandemic.If AAPI Olympic representation is in the spotlight following Tokyo, that success has come from less than promising circumstances.Suni Lee, center, waves from a St. Paul fire truck with her mom Yeev Thoj, left, and sister Shyenne Lee as fans cheer for her in a parade on August 8, 2021, in St. Paul, Minnesota. AAPI divers break groundAAPI athletes began making headlines in the late 1940s, when diver Victoria "Vicki" Draves became the first Asian American to medal at the 1948 Summer Olympic Games in London. Fellow teammate Sammy Lee medaled days after Draves, making him the first Asian American man to win a gold medal.However, both Draves and Lee endured racism that often hindered their ability to train.Draves adopted her mother's maiden name, Taylor, when competing in order to prevent potential racial discrimination that came along with using her father's Filipino surname, Manalo, according to the United States Olympic and Paralympic Museum.She also grew up in a low-income household where swimming was not a priority.Lee, a second-generation Korean American, was permitted to train in his community pool only once a week on International Day -- the Wednesday before the pool's weekly cleaning when non-White children were allowed to swim, the museum stated.Despite those hardships, both athletes excelled in their performances. Draves became the first woman to win two golds in both the 3m springboard and 10m platform in the same Olympics in 1948.At the same Games, Lee won bronze in the 3m springboard and gold in the 10m platform. He went on to defend his gold medal in the 10m platform event at the 1952 Olympics in Helsinki.Vicki Draves straightens up as she plunges towards the water on her way to winning diving gold at the London Olympic Games in 1948.'I saw their pride and I was just taken aback'Forty-four years after Draves' victories at the 1948 Summer Games, Kristi Yamaguchi made history as the first Asian American to win a gold medal in figure skating as well as becoming the second Asian American woman to win gold in any Olympic sport."At the time, I just didn't think much of it because I felt like any other California girl, and it was just like, 'Okay, I'm representing my country and a proud American,'" Yamaguchi told CNN Sport."But I think afterwards, I felt just such an incredible amount of support from the Asian American community. And I saw their pride and I was just taken aback."According to Yamaguchi, she was one of two Asian Americans representing Team USA at the 1992 Albertville Winter Games, alongside Natasha Kuchiki, who competed in pair skating.Yamaguchi credited 1985 U.S. national figure skating champion Tiffany Chin -- a Chinese American figure skater who is a two-time world bronze medalist and the first Asian American to win a singles national title -- as an inspiration."[Tiffany] was a huge role model of mine because I think I definitely had that connection of her being Asian American and someone that I felt I could emulate and look up to," Yamaguchi said.Lee Kiefer celebrates winning the women's foil individual gold medal bout against Inna Deriglazonva of the ROC on July 25, 2021. Due to her landmark win in 1992, Yamaguchi is often attributed as a pioneer for Asian American athletes -- both on and off the ice."I saw representation in Tiffany Chin, and that inspired me. And, hopefully, the more we see ourselves out there, the more people will believe in themselves," Yamaguchi explained.Following Yamaguchi's Olympic gold medal performance, Asian Americans began finding success across a plethora of sports, including Atlanta 1996's "Magnificent Seven" gold-medal winning gymnast Amy Chow, two-time Olympic figure skating medalist Michelle Kwan and gold-medal winning snowboarding superstar Chloe Kim.Tokyo dreams -- fulfilledThe Olympic stage has steadily changed for Team USA since the days of Yamaguchi for both the Winter and Summer Games.At the London Olympics in 2012, 18 AAPI athletes represented Team USA, with several more as backups. The Tokyo Games occurred nearly a decade after the London Olympics, yet the American team saw a nearly a three-fold increase in AAPI athletes since that time.Erica Sullivan won the women's 1,500m freestyle silver at Tokyo 2020.Tokyo 2020 saw more than 50 athletes identifying as Asian American, according to Gold House -- a premier nonprofit collective of AAPI founders, creative voices and leaders.Asians make up about 7% of the nation's population, making them the fastest-growing racial or ethnic group in the US, according to data from Pew Research Center. Yet, the AAPI representation among Team USA in Tokyo was roughly 8.8%, based on CNN calculations. Despite being the only Asian American in the US women's Olympic volleyball team in Tokyo, Wong-Orantes often sees many AAPIs training alongside her in California."We train out here in Anaheim, California, and so I feel like here, just within our little volleyball community, I see so many Asian Americans," she told CNN.JUST WATCHEDSuni Lee shares the advice she got from Simone BilesReplayMore Videos ...MUST WATCHSuni Lee shares the advice she got from Simone Biles 01:11Of the Asian Americans competing for the US, many have openly identified as multiracial."Generational changes happen and we're seeing even more mixed raced athletes," Yamaguchi said.For multiracial athletes like Litherland, who is of Japanese and New Zealand descent, envisioning his goals wasn't difficult when seeing an increasing number of successful individuals with a similar background to his."I really idolized [five-time Olympic gold medal-winning swimmer Nathan Adrian]," Litherland told CNN. "When you look at someone that's got kind of a similar face, you kinda see the resemblance, or you kind of relate to them."Although Litherland was born in Japan and holds triple citizenship in Japan, New Zealand and the US, he wholeheartedly chose to swim on behalf of the American team, despite also being eligible to try out for the Japanese and Kiwi national teams."I'm an American, and growing up, even though my roots are in New Zealand and [Japan], it felt right," Litherland explained to CNN. "There's no other country I would represent."Justine Wong-Orantes (in red) celebrates with teammates during the women's volleyball semifinal between the United States and Serbia on August 6, 2021. Wong-Orantes, another multiracial athlete of Mexican and Chinese descent, also noticed the diversity among other Team USA hopefuls training at the courts."If you look within the whole gym and in the program, we have so many athletes that come from very different backgrounds ... It's our jobs as the current athletes right now to inspire that and push for that," Wong-Orantes stated.Historically, there has been low participation among Asian Americans in the Olympics and in major sports leagues.A combination of stereotypes -- such as Asians being biologically unsuitable for sports and cultural norms -- often played a role in this case of under involvement.However, the current state of representation is slowly changing as the new wave of Asian American athletes challenge the status quo.And that's in no small part to athletes like Wong-Orantes.Wong-Orantes might be the shortest on her team at five-foot-six, but she is widely considered to be one of the world's best defensive players -- no small achievement given the average height for women volleyball players is around 5'10", according to NCSA Sports."In my sport, that can be a disadvantage," says Wong-Orantes matter-of-factly about her height."I would just say it's not too late, and you can still achieve your dreams. And whatever you want to aspire to be, go after it."

(CNN)For Eddy Alvarez, being an Olympian was always the ambition.
"Growing up, I always [said], 'I'm going to be an Olympian,'" Alvarez tells CNN Sport. "That's one thing that I really want to share with everyone: You gotta go and you can do it. You can go for it. Nothing's stopping you."
