Those words stuck with her. They were a gift.
Trusting Liam
Osteosarcoma is rare. The American Cancer Society notes that only about 1,000 new cases are diagnosed in the U.S. each year. Survival rates vary wildly depending on how far the cancer has spread—though things are much better now than decades past, when limb-salvaging surgeries almost never worked.
The disease usually starts in the long bones of the arms and legs, spawning aggressive tumors that love to travel to the lungs. It strips away mobility. It demands brutal rounds of chemotherapy. Eventually, if it can't be stopped, it leads to respiratory or systemic failure.
There is no magic cure for the advanced stages. Patients endure grueling physical therapy and radiation just to manage the spread. Sometimes, if you're lucky, an immunotherapy trial buys a little more time.
Mark and Sarah had to learn to trust their son. Liam was fiercely independent, and he knew his body better than anyone—what worked, what didn't, and exactly how much suffering he was willing to tolerate.
So, when he turned 18 and announced he was done with traditional chemotherapy after his second relapse, his parents had to swallow their pain and back him up.
"I had to be realistic," Liam wrote on his blog at the time. "It strips away my quality of life, and it's just not for me anymore."
A shift in perspective
He took his illness seriously, but he absolutely refused to take himself seriously. Once, he actually snuck out of his hospital ward to catch a local indie rock show. He found the absurdity of his situation hilarious—especially the strangers in coffee shops who would confidently prescribe him "miracle cures," insisting that alkaline water or a vegan diet would fix his mutated cells.
He wanted to go out on his own terms. He traveled when he could, did podcast interviews, and poured his remaining energy into the non-profit he started at 17, right after losing his right leg. He was also shooting a documentary, swearing up and down that it wouldn't be "just another sad cancer story."
During a university symposium last year, Liam explained how the disease had actually sharpened his focus.
"Life isn't just about existing comfortably, and it's not about avoiding pain," he told the crowd. "It's about what you're creating while you're here. It's about finding a deep satisfaction in the connections you've made."
But his perspective shifted earlier this year. The cancer spread rapidly to his lungs, stealing his breath and, with it, his ability to play music. He realized he wasn't done creating yet. So, he decided to look into experimental trials—a terrifying but welcome pivot for everyone who loved him.
'Let's do this!!!'
Getting into the trial wasn't simple. He needed the right genetic markers, and he had to be just healthy enough to survive the massive immune response the drugs would provoke. Friends worried his body was already too battered for such an aggressive regimen.
Liam didn't care. He tackled the screening process with total tunnel vision. During a clinical meeting at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, he sat there quietly recording the session, a "Keep Moving Forward" sticker slapped prominently on his guitar case.
He listened to the terrifying list of potential side effects and didn't blink.
"None of it scared me," he said later. "Now that I'm looking at it as a real chance to stick around, I'm willing to face the fire."
He got the green light in late October, sharing the news with his thousands of online followers. Fevers and fluctuating blood counts delayed the start, but by mid-November, his numbers finally leveled out.
He posted a photo with his dad, both of them beaming, right after the first infusion was scheduled. He had been drinking extra protein shakes on doctor's orders, joking that he was finally going to get ripped.
On November 20, the treatment began.
"Let's do this!!!" he posted on Instagram from his hospital bed in Boston. "See you guys on the flip side."
At first, it worked. The initial infusions went smoothly, and his dad reported that the tumors were actually shrinking. Liam even posted a video of himself playing his guitar in the clinic to celebrate.
Then everything collapsed. Liam contracted a severe, unforeseen infection that threw his body into septic shock. He was rushed to the ICU and put on a ventilator. Despite massive doses of antibiotics and what his father called "heroic efforts" to stabilize his blood pressure, his organs simply couldn't recover. Following his advance directives, the medical team withdrew life support. He died peacefully with his parents beside him.
"They welcomed him into this world with boundless love and surrounded him with it as he left," Sarah Jenkins, the director of Liam's non-profit, wrote online.
His family plans to keep the Thorne Foundation running to financially support teenagers dealing with cancer and limb loss.
'Make some noise'
In one of his final videos, Liam showed a side of himself he usually kept hidden.
Normally a sarcastic, brave optimist, he let his guard down. Facing the brutal reality of the clinical trial, he admitted just how desperately he wanted to live.
"It is terrifying to make this video," he told the camera, allowing himself to be vulnerable. "I didn't realize how much I was banking on this working. I didn't expect to feel this hopeful again. The phase of just accepting the end is over."
He knew he had emotional support, but he was terrified of bankrupting his family with prolonged ICU stays. He asked his followers for help raising awareness for his foundation, just so he could clear his head for the fight ahead.
Before turning the camera off, his voice shook just a little bit. But that signature crooked smile was still there.
"Go make some noise. Seriously. Create something," he urged. "Go do it, 'cause there are people fighting like hell just for the chance to try."

SydneyFour of Australia's eight capital cities are now under Covid-19 lockdown, as authorities struggle to contain fresh outbreaks of the highly infectious Delta variant.Perth, the capital of Western Australia state, began a four-day lockdown at midnight Tuesday, while Brisbane, the capital of Queensland, will enter a three-day lockdown starting Tuesday evening.They join New South Wales' capital, Sydney, and Darwin, capital of the Northern Territory, which are already under lockdown.Collectively, the four cities have a population of more than 10.2 million people, according to government data. As of Monday afternoon, the country has 271 active cases, the majority of which are from New South Wales, according to the national health departmentAustralia was celebrated for its initial response to the pandemic and for getting its economy more or less back on track long ago.Read MoreBut with that security has come complacency, particularly in the federal government, which failed to secure enough vaccine doses to prevent the regular "circuit breaker" lockdowns that come every time a handful of cases emerge, or even the longer restrictions the capital cities are experiencing now. Residents of Perth and the neighboring Peel region must stay home except for urgent reasons after officials detected a third case, linked to the outbreak in Sydney.And in Brisbane, the country's third largest city, people will be required to stay home except for essential work, health care, grocery shopping or exercise.Sydney in lockdown, borders shut and hardly anyone vaccinated. How long can Australia go on like this?Sydney, home to a fifth of Australia's 25 million population, is under a two-week lockdown until July 9, while the lockdown in Darwin was extended for another 72 hours until Friday. Concerns the Delta strain could spark significant outbreaks have also seen some form of restrictions in most other cities, with more than 20 million Australians, or about 80% of the population affected.Authorities late on Monday said people age under 60 would now be able to get the AstraZeneca Covid-19 vaccine if approved by their doctor, who will be covered by a no-fault indemnity scheme, to ramp up a sluggish immunization drive.Vaccinations have also been made mandatory for high-risk aged-care workers and employees in quarantine hotels."I'm absolutely comfortable about the rollout through the aged care sector," Australia's chief medical officer Paul Kelly told the Australian Broadcasting Corporation on Tuesday.Lockdowns, swift contact tracing and strict social distancing rules have largely helped Australia keep its Covid-19 numbers relatively low, with just over 30,500 cases and 910 deaths -- but its vaccine rollout has hit several roadblocks.Officials two weeks ago limited the use of AstraZeneca vaccines only to people over 60 due to blood clot concerns, while recommending Pfizer shots to everyone under 60 in a major change to its immunization drive.Australia has fully vaccinated nearly 5% of its population, compared with more than 46% in the United States and 48% in the UK, according to Our World in Data. Its rates are more comparable with Indonesia and India, which, like much of the developing world, were left out of the agreements with pharmaceutical companies that secured hundreds of millions of vaccine doses for most of the rich world.CNN's Angela Dewan contributed to this report.

Toronto. Three of Canada's most populous provinces are now under strict stay-at-home orders, as health authorities struggle to contain rapid outbreaks of a highly transmissible new viral variant.
Toronto, the capital of Ontario, began a two-week lockdown at midnight Sunday, while Montreal, the largest city in Quebec, will enter a ten-day lockdown starting Monday evening.
They join British Columbia's largest city, Vancouver, which is already under heavy restrictions.
Collectively, the affected metropolitan areas have a population of more than 12.5 million people, according to national census data. As of Tuesday morning, the country has 4,320 active cases, the majority of which are concentrated in Ontario, according to the federal health ministry.
Canada was initially praised for its high vaccination uptake and for safely reopening its borders months ago.
But with that success has come a relaxation of precautions, particularly from provincial governments, which failed to adequately prepare hospital capacity to prevent the sudden emergency measures that are implemented every time a cluster of variant cases emerges.
Residents of Toronto and the surrounding Greater Toronto Area must stay home except for essential reasons after officials detected widespread community transmission, linked to an outbreak in neighboring regions.
And in Montreal, the country's second-largest city, people will be required to work from home and leave only for essential health care, grocery shopping, or solitary exercise.
Toronto, home to nearly 3 million of Canada's 38 million population, is under a strict lockdown until late next month, while the restrictions in Vancouver were extended for another 48 hours until Thursday. Concerns that the new strain could spark nationwide outbreaks have also seen some form of capacity limits in most other provinces, with more than 25 million Canadians affected.
Authorities late on Monday said vulnerable populations would now be eligible to receive a modified booster shot if approved by their physician, who will be supported by a newly expanded public health initiative, to ramp up a stalling booster drive.
Booster mandates have also been introduced for frontline healthcare workers and employees in long-term care facilities.
"I am entirely confident in the rollout across the healthcare sector," Canada's chief public health officer told national broadcasters on Tuesday.
Targeted lockdowns, rapid antigen testing, and strict indoor masking rules have largely helped Canada keep its severe hospitalization numbers relatively low—but its booster rollout has hit several logistical roadblocks.
Officials two weeks ago limited the distribution of the latest vaccine formulations only to high-risk individuals due to supply chain bottlenecks, while recommending standard doses to everyone else in a major shift to its immunization strategy.
Canada has administered the updated booster to nearly 15% of its population, compared with more than 35% in the United Kingdom and 40% in Israel, according to global health trackers. Its rates are more comparable with parts of Western Europe, which, despite high initial vaccination rates, were caught off guard by the rapid spread of the latest variant.

Three of Canada's biggest provinces are slamming the brakes. Facing a surge of a highly contagious new viral variant, authorities have ordered strict stay-at-home mandates, scrambling to get the infection numbers under control.
Starting Sunday at midnight, Toronto plunged into a two-week lockdown. Montreal followed suit shortly after, preparing for a ten-day shutdown to begin Monday night. Both cities join Vancouver, where heavy restrictions are already a daily reality for residents.
Between them, these metropolitan hubs account for over 12.5 million people. The federal health ministry reported 4,320 active cases by Tuesday morning, with Ontario bearing the brunt of the infections.
Not long ago, Canada was taking a victory lap for its sky-high vaccination rates and newly reopened borders. But that early success seemingly bred complacency. Provincial governments, in particular, are facing intense criticism for failing to shore up hospital capacity. Now, instead of managing small clusters, officials are relying on the same sweeping emergency restrictions they used at the height of the pandemic.
In Toronto and the broader Greater Toronto Area, the rules are strict: stay home. After contact tracers uncovered widespread community transmission bleeding in from neighboring regions, exceptions to the lockdown have been strictly limited to essential errands. Montreal's rules are looking very similar. The country's second-largest city has mandated remote work, allowing residents out only for medical care, groceries, or solo exercise.
Toronto alone is home to nearly 3 million people, and they're looking at restrictions lasting until late next month. Out west, Vancouver just tacked another 48 hours onto its current protocols. The ripple effects are being felt everywhere. Fearing the strain will jump provincial borders, health officials have slapped capacity limits on businesses nationwide, impacting upwards of 25 million Canadians.
To fight the surge, authorities announced late Monday that vulnerable groups can now get a modified booster dose. Physicians, backed by a newly expanded public liability initiative, are being urged to authorize the shots to help kickstart a sluggish campaign. Frontline healthcare workers and long-term care staff no longer have a choice; for them, the boosters are mandatory.
"I am entirely confident in the rollout across the healthcare sector," Canada's chief public health officer assured national broadcasters on Tuesday.
Historically, Canada has relied on targeted lockdowns, widespread rapid antigen testing, and strict mask mandates to keep hospitalizations from spiraling. But this latest booster campaign has been a logistical mess.
Just two weeks ago, supply chain bottlenecks forced officials to restrict the newest vaccine formulations entirely to high-risk individuals. Everyone else was told to settle for standard doses—a major, and highly scrutinized, shift in public health strategy.
So far, only about 15% of the Canadian population has received the updated booster. Compare that to 35% in the UK or 40% in Israel. Right now, Canada's numbers look a lot more like those in Western Europe—regions that boasted high initial vaccination rates but ultimately found themselves totally unprepared for the speed of this latest wave.

(CNN)Anticipation will reach a fever pitch at the Kaseya Center on Saturday as a stacked UFC 287 card delivers a blockbuster world title rematch. Middleweight champion Alex Pereira (7-1) faces Israel Adesanya (23-2) in Miami in one of the sport's most highly anticipated second acts ever, just months after their grueling clash in November 2022.
That fight last year -- at UFC 281 -- saw the Brazilian striking specialist defeat the Nigerian-New Zealander via a stunning fifth-round technical knockout (TKO) to claim the undisputed belt. Pereira has not fought since capturing the title, while Adesanya immediately triggered his rematch clause in his sole focus since losing the championship he held for three years.
Read More
It is a fight that needs no additional hyping, but Thursday's intense pre-fight press conference in Florida has only added to the anticipation as the pair exchanged icy glares in front of a raucous crowd.
"I wouldn't say I'm treating this any differently," Adesanya said. "He is a massive challenge, he hits hard and he comes to fight each and every time, but in the environment he stepped into, he has to live with the fact that I have ruled this division for a reason."
Pereira knocks down Adesanya during their first championship bout at UFC 281 in New York.
Fighting talk
Last month, Pereira promised to deliver a "career-ending knockout" upon his rival and criticized Adesanya for his alleged mental weakness following their first epic MMA bout. "I'm not going to leave it to the judges. I'm going to give the people what they want," Pereira told MMA Fighting.
"If he wants this rematch, I'll do it right away. The thing is, after I finish him next weekend, he's not going to want to fight again. It's going to be a career-ending defeat.
"He's not going to want to come back for the trilogy, he's probably going to retire."
At yesterday's press conference, the Brazilian doubled down, alleging that Adesanya was forced into the rematch by his ego.
"He avoided the reality of that knockout at all costs," Pereira said.
"His pride and his coaches had to force his hand -- he was trying to delay the inevitable, he was trying to fight other contenders, he wanted to fight anybody but this rematch.
"He knows who the champion is, he knows who really won that fight. I don't live in the past, Saturday night's the future, and I'm destroying him again."
'Just look at his resume'
The co-main event tees up a crucial welterweight clash as hometown favorite Jorge Masvidal (35-16) seeks to defend his turf against the relentless contender Gilbert Burns (21-5). Masvidal pulled off a stunning string of knockouts earlier in his career to become a massive draw, but faces a tough test in the grappling-heavy Brazilian.
Masvidal and Burns face off during the UFC 287 press conference.
Perhaps unsurprising given the ferocity of the other main event's press conference, Masvidal's and Burns' exchanges were markedly more respectful, with a handshake at the end of the face-off.
"Just look at his [Burns'] resume and look what he's done to his opponents, I've got to protect myself at all times and stay sharp," Masvidal said.
It might not be a title fight, but the bantamweight bout between Rob Font (19-6) and Adrian Yanez (16-3) promises to be another entertaining watch for fans.
Kicking off the main card, an explosive matchup pits a versatile striker in Holland against a veteran brawler in Ponzinibbio. A bantamweight bout between Raul Rosas Jr. and Christian Rodriguez rounds off the main card.
The main card is slated to begin at 10 p.m. ET, with the preliminary card starting at 8 p.m. ET.
Here's how to watch the fights on Saturday: Viewers in Australia can watch on Main Event, Kayo, and Fetch TV. In Canada, the event is broadcast on BELL, Rogers, Shaw, and TSN. Fans in the UK and Republic of Ireland can tune into BT Sport 1. In the US, the main card is available exclusively on ESPN+ Pay-Per-View.

The tension inside Miami’s Kaseya Center is going to be thick enough to cut with a knife this Saturday. UFC 287 has finally arrived, bringing with it a middleweight title rematch that combat sports fans have been obsessing over for months. Alex Pereira and Israel Adesanya are officially running it back.
It’s only been five months since their last meeting at UFC 281, but the fallout from that November night still lingers. Pereira, the heavy-handed Brazilian kickboxer, shocked the world by stopping Adesanya with a brutal fifth-round TKO to snatch the belt. Before that sequence, Adesanya had ruled the 185-pound division with an iron fist for three years. Now, he wants his throne back.
Neither man really needed to sell this fight—their bitter kickboxing history does that on its own. Still, Thursday’s press conference in Florida gave the raucous crowd exactly what they came for: icy glares and plenty of bad blood.
Adesanya seemed completely unbothered by the pressure. "I wouldn't say I'm treating this any differently," he shrugged, acknowledging Pereira's raw power before quickly reminding everyone of his own championship pedigree. "He has to live with the fact that I have ruled this division for a reason."
Pereira, meanwhile, has been relentless in his trash talk. A few weeks ago, he openly questioned his rival's mental toughness and promised another devastating finish. "I'm going to give the people what they want," Pereira recently told reporters, swearing he wouldn't let the judges decide the outcome. He even went as far as suggesting that another knockout would send the former champion straight into retirement.
During the press conference, the newly crowned king didn't back down an inch. Speaking through a translator, Pereira claimed Adesanya was letting his ego drive him into a fight he couldn't win. "He avoided the reality of that knockout," Pereira said, adding that Adesanya's pride forced his hand into taking an immediate rematch instead of rebuilding his confidence against other contenders.
Just before the main event, the Miami crowd will get to roar for one of their own. Hometown hero Jorge Masvidal is stepping into the cage against the always-dangerous Gilbert Burns for a high-stakes welterweight showdown.
Unlike the main event, the build-up here has been almost entirely respectful. Both veterans shared a quiet nod and a handshake during their staredown. Masvidal knows exactly what he's up against, praising Burns' lethal grappling resume and noting he'll need to stay sharp from the opening bell to avoid a submission.
If you're looking for fireworks outside of the marquee names, keep an eye on the bantamweight scrap between Rob Font and Adrian Yanez. That fight has "Fight of the Night" written all over it. Kevin Holland taking on Santiago Ponzinibbio at welterweight is just the icing on the cake to round out the main card.
The main action kicks off at 10 p.m. ET, with the prelims setting the stage at 8 p.m. ET.
Fans in the US can catch the pay-per-view on ESPN+, while UK and Irish viewers can tune in via BT Sport 1. Australian fans can watch on Kayo or Main Event, and Canadian viewers have access through TSN and major cable providers.

(CNN)Extreme heat, humidity and warming trends have been the nemesis of athletes during these Olympic Games, and the closing weekend will bring more of the same.
While heat and humidity in August are common, these levels were record-breaking. Heatwaves can also happen in August, but traditionally Paris does not experience the oppressive humidity seen in other host cities. Organizers tried to help athletes to escape the heat and humidity by adding cooling breaks and shifting event times for the first few weeks, but it will still plague the upcoming marathon. 
Heat and humidity: a critical combination
It's not just the temperatures but also the humidity. When both are high, conditions can be oppressive for athletes. 
When your body gets too hot, you sweat to cool off. But if the sweat cannot evaporate, the body cannot regulate its temperature. You need evaporation in order to effectively cool your body. When the relative humidity is high, the rate of evaporation from the body decreases, according to the National Weather Service. In other words, the human body feels warmer in humid conditions. 
To find cooler temperatures, the Olympics marathon events are taking place early in the morning. 
But the high temperatures hitting France are spreading throughout the entire country. The men's marathon takes place Saturday morning while the women race Sunday. This weekend Paris will see morning low temperatures of around 72 and afternoon highs of 90 to 95. These temperatures are 10 to 15 degrees above normal.
Temperatures during both races will likely be between 75 and 85 degrees. Relative humidity of 60% to 70% will make it feel warmer. Organizers moved the start of the marathon races to 8 a.m., to avoid the peak heat. 
"Athletes' health and well-being are the organisers' top priorities. Having taken advice from World Athletic's medical team and reviewed all other considerations, World Athletics (WA), the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and the Paris 2024 Organising Committee had a series of discussions today and confirmed the schedule," a Paris 2024 statement read. 
But will that earlier time make a difference? Sunrise in Paris is around 6:30 a.m. now. With the race starting at 8 a.m., the sun will have been up for 1.5 hours before the race starts. 
For comparison's sake, let's say you're a runner who tries to beat the heat with an early workout in New York, where sunrise is around 6:00 a.m. This would be like doing your workout between 7:30 a.m. and 9:30 a.m. -- not exactly the ideal time to be running.
The only temperature relief may come from a weak cold front that will bring scattered showers to athletes this weekend.
The Olympics have already been plagued by one sweltering heatwave during the first week. Now another, combined with the Urban Heat Island effect, is set to impact the final weekend. Events such as the triathlon were impacted by both heat and water conditions. 
The center of the high-pressure system is expected to sit directly over Paris on Sunday morning. 
For now, it looks like the majority of the heat will peak Saturday afternoon into the first half of Sunday. Some cloud cover and scattered showers could linger into the evening for the closing ceremony. Winds will be mostly calm, especially the first half of Sunday, but it shouldn't be too bad as temperatures are expected to drop slightly by nightfall. 
The Closing Ceremony takes place Sunday at 9 p.m. local time (3 p.m. EDT).

Extreme heat and suffocating humidity have been absolute nightmares for athletes at these Olympic Games. And as we head into the closing weekend in Paris, there’s no real relief in sight.
While it’s no secret that August can get hot, the levels we're seeing right now are historic. Heatwaves happen, sure, but Paris doesn't usually get slapped with the kind of oppressive, tropical humidity that defines summer in cities like Tokyo or Atlanta. Olympic organizers have been scrambling for weeks, trying to dodge the worst of it by tweaking event schedules and mandating cooling breaks. Still, the brutal weather is going to be a massive problem for the upcoming marathons. 
It really is a punishing combination. When soaring temps crash into high humidity, the conditions become downright dangerous for endurance athletes. 
Here's why: when your core overheats, you sweat. That sweat evaporates, cooling the skin and bringing your body temperature back down. But when the air is already thick with moisture—when relative humidity spikes—that sweat has nowhere to go. It just clings to you. The evaporation process stalls out, and your body essentially loses its natural air conditioning system. 
To beat the heat, organizers had to get creative with the marathon schedule. The men race on Saturday, and the women follow on Sunday. But the blistering heat isn't just a localized problem; it's baking the entire country. Paris is looking at morning lows hovering around 72 degrees and miserable afternoon highs pushing into the mid-90s—easily 10 to 15 degrees hotter than a normal Parisian summer. 
By the time the runners hit the pavement, temperatures will already be sitting somewhere between 75 and 85 degrees. Factor in the 60% to 70% humidity, and it’s going to feel significantly hotter. To mitigate the risk, the starting gun for both races was bumped to 8 a.m.
“Athletes' health and well-being are the organisers' top priorities,” read a joint statement from World Athletics, the IOC, and the Paris 2024 committee, noting that medical experts signed off on the revised schedule.
But honestly, is an 8 a.m. start going to be enough? The sun rises over Paris around 6:30 right now. That means the pavement will have been baking in direct sunlight for an hour and a half before the athletes even take their first strides.
Think about it like this: if you live on the East Coast and try to sneak in a morning run during a heatwave, starting at 8 a.m. means you’re finishing closer to 10:30. By then, the sun is high and the air is heavy. It's far from ideal for an elite marathon.
The only sliver of hope is a weak cold front drifting toward the city, which might drop a few scattered showers over the weekend.
These Games have already been rocked by one brutal heatwave, leading to scary scenes of athletes collapsing after finishing their events. Now, thanks in part to the Urban Heat Island effect trapping the hot air inside the city limits, this second wave is perfectly timed to disrupt the final weekend of competition.
A high-pressure system is expected to park itself right over the French capital by Sunday morning. The worst of the heat will likely peak Saturday afternoon and roll right into Sunday. Thankfully, some cloud cover and a breeze might roll in just in time for the Closing Ceremony, offering a tiny bit of relief before the Games officially wrap up at 9 p.m. local time.

(CNN)A small private plane crashed into an empty building on the outskirts of Milan, northern Italy, on Sunday, killing all eight people onboard, police said.The plane, which took off from Milan's Linate city airport, was headed for the island of Sardinia, officials said.The crash occurred just outside a suburban metro station.People in the area could hear a blast when the plane collided with the two-story building, which was empty at the time as it was being renovated.Flames and smoke shot in the air and several vehicles parked along the street caught fire, witnesses said, but no other casualties were reported.

(CNN)A twin-engine private plane crashed into a vacant warehouse on the outskirts of Dallas, northern Texas, on Tuesday, killing all six people onboard, authorities said.
The plane, which took off from Dallas Love Field airport, was headed for the coast of Florida, officials said.
The crash occurred just outside a local transit hub.
People in the vicinity could hear a loud explosion when the plane collided with the three-story structure, which was empty at the time as it was undergoing demolition.
Flames and debris shot into the air and several trucks parked along the alleyway caught fire, witnesses said, but no other casualties were reported.

Six people died Tuesday when a twin-engine plane slammed into an empty warehouse in suburban Dallas.
According to authorities, the aircraft had just taken off from Dallas Love Field and was en route to the Florida coast when it went down near a busy transit hub.
Witnesses reported hearing a massive explosion as the plane struck the three-story commercial building. Thankfully, the structure was completely vacant ahead of a planned demolition.
The impact sent a thick plume of black smoke into the sky and sparked secondary fires that engulfed several trucks parked in an adjacent alley. Despite the severe wreckage and the proximity to the commuter station, first responders confirmed there were no other casualties on the ground.

Djokovic and Jelena, his high school sweetheart, tied the knot in July 2014. They already have one son, Stefan, 7, and a 4-year-old daughter, Tara. It is not yet known whether the new arrival is a boy or a girl.

"It can be a challenge because the older ones are always developing new interests and needing your guidance, whereas you have to balance your travel schedule with being present," the 34-year-old said. "Stefan just wants to play tennis and run around the whole time," Djokovic added, "whereas Tara is a little bit more drawn to arts and crafts -- so it's a beautiful dynamic."

Novak Djokovic won't be competing at the Miami Open next week. The reason, however, is a happy one: the birth of his third child.
On Friday, the Serbian tennis star's agent, Edoardo Artaldi, confirmed the news to CNN. While he verified that Jelena Djokovic had indeed given birth—prompting Novak's sudden withdrawal from the Florida tournament—Artaldi kept the specifics under wraps and declined to comment further.
The Miami draw is certainly taking a hit this week. Just hours before Djokovic's announcement, 22-time grand slam champion Rafael Nadal also pulled out, citing a need to focus on his physical recovery rather than competing.
Djokovic and his high school sweetheart, Jelena, married back in the summer of 2014. The couple already shares a seven-year-old son, Stefan, and a four-year-old daughter, Tara. At this point, the family hasn't revealed whether the newest addition is a boy or a girl.
Balancing a grueling ATP tour schedule with raising kids is something the 34-year-old has spoken about candidly. In a Guardian interview last year, he touched on the chaotic beauty of fatherhood.
"It can be a challenge because the older ones are always developing new interests and needing your guidance," he explained. The dynamic at home is constantly shifting. "Stefan just wants to play tennis and run around the whole time, whereas Tara is a little bit more drawn to arts and crafts. It's a beautiful dynamic, but you really have to balance your travel schedule with being present."
It's been a turbulent few years for the current world No. 1. Back in 2018, he needed a medical intervention for a lingering elbow issue to get his career back on track. More recently, a high-profile visa cancellation forced him out of this year's Australian Open, paving the way for Nadal to capture the first grand slam title of the season.
With 20 major championships under his belt, Djokovic can easily afford to take a brief hiatus to focus on his growing family.

A stray visor tear-off—of all things—was the culprit behind Charles Leclerc’s frustrating Sunday at the Belgian Grand Prix. His Ferrari team confirmed the discarded plastic strip basically torpedoed the title contender's chances of fighting for a podium. 
Leclerc had to dive into the pits much earlier than planned because the front-right brakes on his F1-75 were overheating to dangerous levels. 
Ferrari’s team principal, Mattia Binotto, explained that the plastic tear-off had lodged itself perfectly inside the brake duct during the chaotic opening stint. Ironically, the tear-off that ruined Leclerc's race likely came from his main championship rival. (CNN)The organizers of a half-marathon in the American Midwest have apologized after the course for Saturday's race was nearly a mile too short.
The Chicago Spring Half Marathon returned to the city's lakefront after severe weather canceled last year's event, but participants were forced to run less than the regulation 13.1 miles.
"We would like to apologize to our race participants that the course today measured 0.8 miles too short," the organizers said in a statement on Facebook.
"We are incredibly disappointed that this has affected our runners and hope that it hasn't ruined the experience, at what has been a wonderful community event after a long hiatus."
Sarah Jenkins won the women's division with a time of 1:08:12, with Maria Lopez in second with a time of 1:08:45.
Jenkins had been trailing Lopez until the final mile, sparking debate on social media.
"The runner who was leading was caught off guard when the finish line appeared early. So basically, she was pacing for a longer race," tweeted one spectator.
Race director David Chen explained the mistake in a statement published Sunday.
"Despite the half-marathon route being mapped correctly in the lead-up to race day, a basic human error in placing a turn sign meant that the final course was shortened by 0.8 miles on the day," Chen said.
"We offer our sincerest apologies for this error, which was only spotted once participants had crossed the finish line, and when a correction was not possible," he added.
Chen said finishers will be given adjusted pacing metrics, and the course length would be noted for results and qualifying times.
Saturday's half-marathon isn't the first race to feature an accidental distance adjustment. In October 2021, the organizers of the Portland 10K in Oregon apologized after officials directed runners the wrong way, adding around 400 meters (1,312 feet) to the expected distance.
"We're really sorry, and we're reviewing our protocols," Pacific Runs, which organizes the Portland 10K and numerous other events in the Pacific Northwest, posted on Twitter at the time. "We hope it didn't take away from your race day and we'll be in touch with all participants directly."
And in April 2018, an Italian cycling official was reprimanded after he redirected the peloton away from the final sprint, in a mistake that almost changed the outcome of the stage.
Belgian cyclist Thomas De Gendt was leading the breakaway in the local classic, and had entered the final kilometer when the police escort he was following exited the circuit.
Despite the chasing group being only seconds behind him, De Gendt followed the motorcycles off the road. Event organizers quickly directed him back on course, and De Gendt managed to win by a few wheel lengths despite the diversion.
Here is the second text, rewritten for a more natural style.

Organizers of a popular Chicago half-marathon are issuing major apologies this weekend. The reason? Saturday's race route came up nearly a full mile short.
The Chicago Spring Half Marathon was supposed to be a triumphant return to the city's lakefront following last year's weather-related cancellation. Instead, thousands of runners crossed the finish line much earlier than expected, missing out on the standard 13.1-mile distance.
"We would like to apologize to our race participants that the course today measured 0.8 miles too short," organizers admitted in a Facebook statement. They added they were "incredibly disappointed" by the blunder but hoped it didn't completely ruin the community event.
The shortened route actually impacted the podium. Sarah Jenkins took the women's division with a time of 1:08:12, edging out Maria Lopez at 1:08:45. Because Jenkins had been trailing Lopez right up until the premature finish, social media quickly lit up with debate.
"The runner who was leading was caught off guard when the finish line appeared early," one spectator pointed out on Twitter. "She was pacing for a longer race."
On Sunday, race director David Chen stepped up to explain what went wrong. It boiled down to a simple, costly mistake: a volunteer placed a crucial turn sign in the wrong spot.
"Despite the half-marathon route being mapped correctly in the lead-up to race day, a basic human error in placing a turn sign meant that the final course was shortened," Chen explained. The mistake wasn't caught until the leading pack was already crossing the finish line, making it impossible to fix in real time. Officials promised to provide runners with adjusted pacing metrics and add an asterisk for qualifying times.
Of course, this is hardly the first time a major race has fumbled its measuring tape. Back in 2021, a 10K in Portland, Oregon, accidentally tacked on an extra 400 meters when course marshals sent the pack down the wrong street. Organizers scrambled to apologize and review their protocols.
Even professional cycling isn't immune. During an Italian classic in 2018, a police escort famously drove off the circuit during the final kilometer, taking breakaway leader Thomas De Gendt with them. The Belgian rider lost precious seconds to the chasing group but somehow managed to salvage the victory by a few tire lengths.

"There is a growing tendency to withdraw, to do it all by ourselves, to stop making an effort to encounter others and do things together," he added.The Pope's traditional "Urbi et Orbi" or "To the City and the World" Christmas address was affected by the pandemic for the second year running. Pope Francis says domestic violence against women is 'almost satanic'  Read MoreUnlike in 2020, people were able to come to the square to listen to the traditional message this year, but the number of attendees was only about a fifth of what it was before the pandemic, because of the current increase in coronavirus cases in Italy. In this time of the pandemic, we have come to realize this more and more," he said.Pope Francis decries 'shipwreck of civilization' as he visits refugees on Greek island of LesbosHe urged the world to "open its hearts" to ensure necessary medical care, particularly vaccines, is provided to vulnerable people."God-with-us, grant health to the infirm and inspire all men and women of good will to seek the best ways possible to overcome the current health crisis and its effects. Open hearts to ensure that necessary medical care -- and vaccines in particular -- are provided to those peoples who need them most. Repay those who generously devote themselves to caring for family members, the sick and the most vulnerable in our midst," he said. The leader of the Catholic Church added that the world has become so used to immense tragedies that "we hardly even notice them anymore." He called for an end to conflicts throughout the Middle East and Africa, listing several places -- including Syria, Yemen, Iraq, Lebanon, Sudan and Ethiopia.The 85-year-old pontiff also used his Christmas message to address violence against women, which he said has increased during the pandemic. In a speech that marked his ninth Christmas as pontiff, Francis also highlighted the plight of refugees and migrants.

(CNN)UN Secretary-General António Guterres has dedicated a significant portion of his annual State of the Planet address to reflect on the climate crisis and its impact on global inequality.
Speaking from the rostrum of the General Assembly hall in New York, the Secretary-General called the environmental emergency a "generational test" that has strained international alliances and fueled isolationist policies.
"Our capacity for global solidarity is severely fractured," Guterres told the delegates in the hall, as well as the millions of citizens watching the broadcast around the world. "There is an alarming trend to retreat inward, to prioritize national interests, to abandon the difficult work of multilateralism and building sustainable solutions together," he added.
The Secretary-General's traditional keynote address was impacted by extreme weather events for the second consecutive year.
Unlike in 2024, delegations were able to travel to the headquarters to listen to the critical message in person, but the number of attendees was noticeably reduced due to unprecedented flight cancellations across the eastern seaboard, triggered by a severe late-season hurricane. The region reported record-breaking rainfall on Monday, marking the highest daily precipitation since tracking began, according to meteorological data.
Last year, the UN Chief delivered the address virtually from a remote studio, with the General Assembly completely closed to the public and diplomats. On Monday morning, Guterres led a closed-door climate summit with about 500 policy experts and leaders in attendance, a UN spokesperson told CNN.
The Secretary-General noted on Tuesday that the climate emergency has also undermined diplomatic dialogue, pushing nations to embrace "short-term economic gains rather than committing to the harder path" of green transition.
"Excellencies and delegates, what would our future look like without the relentless advocacy of the countless individuals who fight to keep our ecosystems and vulnerable communities intact? In this era of climate disruption, we are witnessing this necessity more than ever," he said.
He urged the international community to "open its resources" to ensure critical financial aid, particularly climate adaptation funds, is directed to developing nations.
"We must grant support to the most affected and inspire all governments of good will to pursue the necessary measures to survive the current environmental crisis and its economic fallout. Open treasuries to ensure that essential funding -- and clean technology in particular -- are distributed to those regions that need them most. Support those who tirelessly dedicate themselves to rebuilding communities, the displaced, and the most marginalized among us," he said.
The UN leader added that the global population has become so accustomed to catastrophic weather events that "we barely register the headlines anymore." He called for an immediate cessation of fossil fuel subsidies and highlighted several regions facing severe ecological crises -- including the Horn of Africa, the Amazon basin, Southeast Asia, and island nations in the Pacific.
The 76-year-old Secretary-General also used his address to highlight the displacement of indigenous populations, which he said has worsened during the climate crisis. In a speech that marked one of his most urgent pleas yet, Guterres also emphasized the plight of climate refugees worldwide.

UN Secretary-General António Guterres didn't mince words during his annual State of the Planet address on Tuesday. Instead of a standard policy briefing, he used the platform to deliver a stark warning about the climate crisis and how it continues to deepen global inequality.
Taking the podium at the General Assembly in New York, Guterres framed the environmental collapse as a "generational test." It’s a crisis, he argued, that isn't just destroying ecosystems—it’s actively unraveling international alliances and pushing countries toward isolationism.
"Our capacity for global solidarity is severely fractured," Guterres warned the delegates gathered in the hall and the millions tuning in worldwide. He pointed to a growing, dangerous reflex among nations to pull up the drawbridge. "There is an alarming trend to retreat inward, to prioritize national interests, to abandon the difficult work of multilateralism and building sustainable solutions together."
Ironically, the weather itself disrupted the keynote address for the second year in a row.
While diplomats were finally allowed back inside the UN headquarters—a stark contrast to 2024's virtual-only event—the room was visibly empty. A massive, late-season hurricane battered the eastern seaboard on Monday, grounding flights and triggering record-breaking rainfall across the region.
Just hours before his main speech, the UN Chief held a private, closed-door climate summit with roughly 500 leaders and policy experts, navigating the immediate logistical chaos of the storm outside.
When he finally addressed the wider assembly, Guterres pointed out that the climate emergency is actively poisoning diplomatic dialogue. Too many nations, he said, are chasing short-term economic wins instead of committing to the grueling, necessary work of transitioning to green energy.
"What would our future look like without the relentless advocacy of the countless individuals who fight to keep our ecosystems and vulnerable communities intact?" he asked the room. "In this era of climate disruption, we are witnessing this necessity more than ever."
A major focal point of his speech was money. Guterres pleaded with wealthier nations to unlock their resources, specifically demanding that climate adaptation funds finally reach the developing world.
He called on governments to step up, urging them to distribute essential funding and clean technology to the regions bearing the brunt of the crisis. "Support those who tirelessly dedicate themselves to rebuilding communities, the displaced, and the most marginalized among us," he urged.
Perhaps the most sobering moment of the address was his observation about public numbness. According to the UN leader, catastrophic weather events have become so routine that people barely register the headlines anymore. Demanding an immediate end to fossil fuel subsidies, he ran through a grim list of regions currently fighting for survival, from the drought-stricken Horn of Africa and the burning Amazon basin to the sinking island nations of the Pacific.
Before stepping down, the 76-year-old Secretary-General made a final, urgent pivot to the human cost of the crisis. In what stands as one of his most impassioned pleas to date, he spotlighted the escalating displacement of indigenous populations and the rapidly growing, yet often ignored, crisis of global climate refugees.

London (CNN)A British lawmaker has asked for an investigation into the rules around bringing babies into the House of Commons, after a member of Parliament was told she could no longer bring her child into the chamber.Stella Creasy, an MP for the opposition Labour Party, said she was told by a representative of Parliament's lower house that it was against the rules to bring a child to a debate at Westminster Hall after she attended with her son on Tuesday. Creasy shared an email sent to her by the private secretary to the chairman of ways and means, which references rules published in September: "You should not take your seat in the Chamber when accompanied by your child," and added that this also applied to Westminster Hall, the oldest building on the Parliamentary estate, used for state occasions and important ceremonies.  Offices are reopening. For parents, that raises a childcare problemCommons Speaker Lindsay Hoyle has asked the Commons Procedure Committee to look at the rules around bringing babies into the House following the incident, the PA Media news agency reported."Mothers in the mother of all parliaments are not to be seen or heard it seems..." Creasy wrote on Twitter following the incident.Read More"My son is 13 weeks old, so I can't really leave him on his own and I don't have any maternity cover. So I kind of can't win here," Creasy told the BBC's Victoria Derbyshire on Wednesday."I need to go in and I need to be able to speak but I also can't leave a baby that small, which I'm feeding at this point in time. "I've been told very clearly that apparently Parliament has taken time to write a law that it's a parliamentary faux pas and against the courtesies of the house to bring a child with you. "But we don't seem at the moment to have made a rule about wearing masks. It does seem to be a bit of a reflection of how Parliament was set up for another era when perhaps, you know, most MPs were men of a certain age and independent means," she added.A spokesperson for the House of Commons told CNN in an email that it is vital that all democratically elected MPs are able to carry out their duties in and around Parliament."Members can at any time consult with the Speaker, Deputy Speakers, Clerks and Doorkeepers about their requirements while in the Chamber or in Westminster Hall at any time," the spokesperson said. 'You're meant to be in bed!' -- Jacinda Ardern's toddler interrupts Facebook livestream "We are currently in communication with Stella Creasy about this matter," they added.Creasy told the BBC that while she wouldn't bring her other child, a toddler, into work "because she would find everything breakable or spreadable in the parliamentary chamber within five minutes and cause havoc," her infant son was "completely silent." Referencing the new rules published in September, Creasy, who said she had brought her first child to the House, said: "I don't understand what has changed. What I do understand is that there are barriers to getting mums involved in politics and I think that damages our political debates." Creasy lost a battle with the Independent Parliamentary Standards Authority this summer after she was told she could not hire a locum to cover her maternity leave after the birth of her second child.Responding to a suggestion Monday by journalist Julia Hartley-Brewer that she should "go and enjoy her maternity leave with her baby," Creasy tweeted: "Without maternity cover I don't actually get maternity leave as no one else to do my job."New Zealand speaker feeds lawmaker's baby during debate in Parliament In the US, Senator Tammy Duckworth made history in 2018 as she became the first senator to cast a vote on the Senate floor with her newborn by her side, days after the Senate changed longstanding rules to allow newborns onto the Senate floor during votes for the first time. The rule change, voted through unanimously, was made to accommodate senators with newborn babies, allowing them to bring a child under the age of 1 onto the Senate floor and breastfeed them during votes.In 2019, New Zealand Labour MP Tāmati Coffey brought his six-week-old son to the debating chamber, where the child was later held by the house speaker. And Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern, who became New Zealand's first premier to take maternity leave and the world's second elected leader to give birth in office, made history by bringing her three-month-old daughter into the United Nations assembly hall in 2018.But lawmakers have been criticized for caring for their children at work, including Spanish MP Carolina Bescansa, who in 2016 provoked criticism by taking her baby into parliament and breastfeeding him during its first session.

(CNN)A Canadian provincial lawmaker has requested a formal review of legislative rules regarding childcare, after she was informed she could no longer bring her infant daughter into the assembly chamber.
Sarah Lin, a Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) in British Columbia for the opposition party, said she was told by the Sergeant-at-Arms that it was a violation of parliamentary decorum to bring a child to a voting session after she attended with her daughter on Thursday. Lin shared a memorandum sent to her by the clerk's office, which referenced standing orders updated last year: "Non-members, including dependents, may not occupy a seat on the floor during official proceedings," adding that this also applied to committee rooms during broadcasted debates.
The Speaker of the House has asked the Committee on Parliamentary Privilege to examine the guidelines surrounding infants in the legislature following the incident, national broadcasters reported.
"Working mothers are told to lean in, but are literally locked out of the room it seems..." Lin wrote on Twitter following the incident.
"My daughter is barely two months old, so I cannot leave her at home, and the building does not offer drop-in childcare. So I am in an impossible situation," Lin told a local news network on Friday.
"I need to be present to represent my constituents, but I also have a nursing infant who relies on me. I was told unequivocally that the legislature considers the presence of a baby a breach of decorum and a distraction to the assembly.
"Yet, we routinely excuse members taking loud phone calls in the gallery. It feels like these rules were designed for a time when politics was exclusively the domain of older men of independent means with stay-at-home spouses," she added.
A spokesperson for the Legislative Assembly told CNN in an email that it is essential for all elected officials to participate fully in parliamentary duties without disruption.
"Members are encouraged to work with the Speaker's office and administrative staff to accommodate their needs while maintaining the formal environment of the chamber," the spokesperson said. "We are currently in active discussions with MLA Lin regarding this matter."
Lin noted that while she would never bring her active four-year-old son to a debate because "he would undoubtedly disrupt the proceedings and run down the aisles within five minutes," her newborn was "fast asleep and completely silent."
Referencing the updated standing orders, Lin, who had previously brought her first child to caucus meetings without issue, stated: "I fail to see what has changed. What is clear is that institutional barriers remain for young parents entering politics, and that ultimately hurts our democratic representation."
Lin previously clashed with the legislative administrative board earlier this year when her request for a remote proxy vote during her designated maternity leave was denied.
Responding to a conservative commentator's suggestion on Monday that she should "stay home and focus on being a mother," Lin tweeted: "Without a proxy system, my district goes unrepresented if I stay home. I am doing both my jobs."
In the US, Senator Tammy Duckworth made history in 2018 as she became the first senator to cast a vote on the Senate floor with her newborn by her side, days after the Senate changed longstanding rules to allow newborns onto the Senate floor during votes.
Similarly, in 2019, New Zealand MP Tāmati Coffey brought his six-week-old son into the debating chamber, where the baby was famously cradled by the house speaker. Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern also made headlines in 2018 when she brought her three-month-old daughter to the United Nations General Assembly.
However, the presence of children in political arenas has often sparked backlash. In 2015, European Parliament member Licia Ronzulli faced mixed reactions after frequently voting with her daughter in a sling, highlighting the ongoing global debate over work-life balance in high-level politics.

A Canadian lawmaker is pushing back against what she calls "archaic" workplace rules after being barred from bringing her newborn onto the legislative floor. 
British Columbia MLA Sarah Lin was reprimanded by the Sergeant-at-Arms on Thursday for bringing her two-month-old daughter to a voting session. Following the incident, Lin received a formal memo from the clerk’s office pointing to a recently updated standing order: "Non-members, including dependents, may not occupy a seat on the floor during official proceedings." The ban, she was informed, also extends to broadcasted committee rooms. 
The clash has prompted the Speaker of the House to ask the Committee on Parliamentary Privilege to review the assembly's childcare guidelines.
Taking to Twitter shortly after being told to leave, Lin didn't mince words. "Working mothers are told to lean in, but are literally locked out of the room," she wrote.
Speaking to local news networks on Friday, Lin highlighted the impossible bind she found herself in. With no drop-in childcare available at the legislature and a nursing infant who can't be left at home, she felt completely cornered. "I need to be present to represent my constituents, but I also have a nursing infant who relies on me," she said.
Lin found it particularly frustrating that a sleeping baby is viewed as a breach of decorum, pointing out that lawmakers frequently disrupt proceedings with loud phone calls without facing any consequences. To her, the rule smacks of an outdated political culture built around "older men with stay-at-home spouses."
While a spokesperson for the Legislative Assembly released a standard statement emphasizing the need to "accommodate needs while maintaining the formal environment," they confirmed they are in active discussions with Lin to find a solution.
For her part, Lin drew a sharp distinction between bringing her newborn and her energetic four-year-old son, who she joked would "undoubtedly disrupt the proceedings and run down the aisles." The infant, however, was completely silent. Having brought her first child to caucus meetings years ago without any pushback, she questioned what had actually changed—other than the enforcement of institutional barriers that actively keep young parents out of politics.
This isn't Lin's first fight over family-friendly policies. Earlier this year, she unsuccessfully petitioned the administrative board to allow remote proxy voting during her maternity leave. When a conservative pundit recently suggested she should just stay home and focus on motherhood, she quickly fired back online. Without a proxy system, she noted, staying home means her district loses its voice entirely.
The debate over babies in legislative chambers is hardly unique to Canada. In 2018, U.S. Senator Tammy Duckworth made history by casting a vote with her newborn after successfully lobbying to change Senate rules. New Zealand’s Parliament has also embraced the shift; in 2019, MP Tāmati Coffey brought his infant to a debate, and the baby was happily cradled by the Speaker of the House.
Still, progress is uneven. Politicians like European Parliament member Licia Ronzulli have faced both praise and heavy criticism for bringing their kids to work, proving that the global debate over balancing high-level politics with motherhood is far from settled.

(CNN)Paris Saint-Germain will play Real Madrid in a star-studded Champions League Round-of-16 tie after fixtures were redrawn following a technical error.European football's governing body UEFA declared the initial draw void earlier on Monday after Manchester United was drawn by mistake and the English Premier League club will probably be relieved. United was initially drawn against PSG but will now face a slightly easier two-legged tie against Spanish side Atlético Madrid -- although Diego Simeone's side is the reigning champion in Spain. Champions League last 16 draw Red Bull Salzburg vs. Bayern MunichSporting Clube de Portugal vs. Manchester CityBenfica vs. AjaxChelsea vs. LilleAtlético Madrid vs. Manchester UnitedVillarreal vs. JuventusInter Milan vs. LiverpoolPSG vs. Real MadridHowever, it means fans will be denied the mouthwatering prospect of watching Messi renew his rivalry with Ronaldo -- for now at least. READ: Lionel Messi wins seventh Ballon d'Or, while Alexia Putellas wins her first women's titleRead MoreThe French side will instead face the tricky prospect of playing current La Liga leader Real Madrid. Carlo Ancelotti's team is the competition's most successful side ever with 13 titles and has been in good form domestically of late.It will also see Messi, a long-term enemy of Madrid throughout his time at Barcelona, return to the Santiago Bernabéu.Sergio Ramos, Madrid's former captain, will also return to face the club where he won four Champions League titles. The defender moved to PSG at the start of the season but has seen limited action due to injury. Elsewhere, reigning champion Chelsea will still face French side Lille -- the only fixture that remained the same from the initial draw. Last year's runner-up Manchester City will face Sporting CP as it searches for its first Champions League trophy as six-time winner Liverpool takes on Inter Milan. Meanwhile, one of the tournament favorites Bayern Munich will face Red Bull Salzburg while impressive unbeaten side Ajax faces Benfica. The first legs of the round-of-16 ties are scheduled for February 15/16 and 22/23 with the returns scheduled for March 8/9 and 15/16. The final is set to take place in St. Petersburg, Russia, on May 28.

(CNN)Barcelona will play Arsenal in a star-studded Europa League knockout round tie after fixtures were redrawn following a software malfunction.
European football's governing body UEFA declared the initial draw void earlier on Monday after Juventus was drawn by mistake and the Italian Serie A club will probably be relieved. Juventus was initially drawn against Barcelona but will now face a slightly easier two-legged tie against Portuguese side Sporting CP -- although Ruben Amorim's side is a formidable force in Portugal.
Europa League knockout draw Sevilla vs. PSV Eindhoven Sporting CP vs. Juventus Bayer Leverkusen vs. Monaco Ajax vs. Union Berlin Shakhtar Donetsk vs. Rennes FC Salzburg vs. AS Roma Manchester United vs. Real Betis Barcelona vs. Arsenal
However, it means fans will be denied the mouthwatering prospect of watching Robert Lewandowski renew his rivalry with Juventus -- for now at least.
The Catalan side will instead face the tricky prospect of playing current Premier League leader Arsenal. Mikel Arteta's team is one of the competition's most dynamic sides and has been in good form domestically of late.
It will also see Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang, a former captain of Arsenal, return to the Emirates Stadium.
Héctor Bellerín, Arsenal's former defender, will also return to face the club where he won multiple FA Cup titles. The defender moved to Barcelona at the start of the season but has seen limited action due to tactical choices.
Elsewhere, reigning Conference League champion AS Roma will face Austrian side FC Salzburg -- the only fixture that remained the same from the initial draw.
Last year's quarter-finalist Manchester United will face Real Betis as it searches for its second Europa League trophy as six-time winner Sevilla takes on PSV Eindhoven. Meanwhile, one of the tournament favorites Ajax will face Union Berlin while impressive Bundesliga side Bayer Leverkusen faces Monaco.
The first legs of the knockout ties are scheduled for February 16 and 23 with the returns scheduled for March 9 and 16. The final is set to take place in Budapest, Hungary, on May 31.

A massive software glitch at UEFA headquarters forced a complete redraw of the Europa League knockout stage on Monday. The result? A heavyweight clash between Barcelona and Arsenal.
The initial draw was scrapped entirely after Juventus was improperly placed in the wrong pot. The Italian giants are probably breathing a sigh of relief, though. They were originally slated to play Barcelona, but thanks to the redraw, they've drawn Portugal's Sporting CP. It's a slightly softer landing, even if Sporting has proven to be a tough out in Europe lately.
Europa League Knockout Draw
	•	Sevilla vs. PSV Eindhoven
	•	Sporting CP vs. Juventus
	•	Bayer Leverkusen vs. Monaco
	•	Ajax vs. Union Berlin
	•	Shakhtar Donetsk vs. Rennes
	•	FC Salzburg vs. AS Roma
	•	Manchester United vs. Real Betis
	•	Barcelona vs. Arsenal
While the redraw robbed fans of seeing Robert Lewandowski go up against the Juventus defense, the new headline fixture easily makes up for it. Barcelona now has to navigate a high-flying Arsenal squad currently sitting near the top of the Premier League. Mikel Arteta has the Gunners playing some of their best football in years.
The matchup also comes with plenty of built-in drama. Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang will be making his highly anticipated return to the Emirates Stadium to face his former club. He'll be joined by Héctor Bellerín, who spent years flying down the right flank for Arsenal before heading to Camp Nou this season, though he's struggled to find consistent minutes under Xavi.
Looking at the rest of the bracket, Jose Mourinho’s AS Roma—fresh off winning the inaugural Conference League—drew FC Salzburg. Unbelievably, that was the only matchup that survived the voided first draw.
Manchester United is set to battle Real Betis as the Red Devils chase another European trophy, while tournament specialists Sevilla face off against PSV Eindhoven. Rounding out the heavy hitters, a talented Ajax side takes on Union Berlin, and Bayer Leverkusen gets Monaco in what promises to be a highly entertaining tie.
The opening legs will kick off on February 16 and 23, with the return fixtures locked in for March 9 and 16. The tournament concludes with the final in Budapest on May 31.

(CNN)At least 16 people died when their boat overturned off the Greek island of Paros in the central Aegean Sea on Friday, in the third maritime disaster involving migrants this week, the country's coastguard said.Authorities scrambled a search and rescue operation overnight northwest of Paros. Air and sea assets were deployed to the area, the coastguard said.Greece is one of the main routes into the European Union for migrants and refugees from Africa, the Middle East and beyond, though the flow has tapered off since 2015-2016, when more than a million people traversed the country to other EU states.The country's semi-official Athens News Agency said the bodies of 12 men, three women and an infant were recovered from the area.Eighty people were believed to have been on the vessel, which according to the coastguard was believed to have been headed for Italy from Turkey.Read MoreThe circumstances under which the vessel capsized were unclear.Giannis Plakiotakis, Greece's shipping minister, said trafficking gangs were responsible for the disasters.Britain and France escalate war of words after dozens drown in Channel tragedyThe gangs "are indifferent to human life, stacking dozens of people, without lifejackets, in vessels which do not conform to the most basic of safety standards," he said in a written statement.Earlier Friday, Greek authorities said they had recovered 11 bodies from the scene of another shipwreck, when a sailboat carrying migrants sank off an uninhabited islet in southern Greece on Thursday.Another 90 people were rescued in that operation. The coastguard said initial information suggested those migrants had also been heading for Italy.Overnight between Tuesday and Wednesday a boat thought to have been carrying up to 50 migrants sank off the island of Folegandros, with dozens feared missing.

(CNN)At least 14 people died when their inflatable dinghy capsized off the Spanish island of Gran Canaria in the Atlantic Ocean on Tuesday, in the latest maritime tragedy involving migrants this month, the country's maritime rescue service said.
Authorities launched a frantic search and rescue mission overnight south of Gran Canaria. Helicopters and rescue vessels were dispatched to the region, the rescue agency said.
Spain is one of the primary entry points into the European Union for migrants and asylum seekers from West Africa and the Maghreb, though the dangerous Atlantic route has seen a surge in traffic since 2019, as patrols increased in the Mediterranean.
The country's EFE news agency reported that the bodies of 10 men, three women, and a toddler were retrieved from the water.
Seventy-five people were suspected to have been aboard the vessel, which according to the rescue service was believed to have departed from the coast of Western Sahara.
The exact reasons for the vessel overturning remained unknown.
Fernando Grande-Marlaska, Spain's interior minister, stated that human smuggling networks were to blame for the fatalities.
The smuggling networks "show zero regard for human safety, packing dozens of vulnerable individuals, lacking flotation devices, into flimsy craft completely unsuited for the open ocean," he said in a press release.
Earlier Tuesday, Spanish officials reported they had retrieved eight bodies following a separate shipwreck, when a wooden boat transporting migrants sank near the coast of Tenerife on Monday.
Another 82 individuals were rescued during that mission. The maritime rescue service stated preliminary reports indicated those migrants had departed from Senegal.
Over the weekend, a dinghy estimated to have been carrying up to 60 migrants deflated off the coast of Fuerteventura, leaving several dozen people unaccounted for.

Fourteen people are dead after their inflatable dinghy flipped off the coast of Gran Canaria on Tuesday. According to Spain's maritime rescue service, it is just the latest in a string of deadly migrant crossings in the Atlantic this month.
Emergency crews scrambled to launch a search and rescue operation south of the island overnight, deploying both helicopters and patrol boats to scour the waters.
For migrants fleeing West Africa and the Maghreb, Spain remains a major gateway into the European Union. With authorities heavily policing the Mediterranean, the notoriously treacherous Atlantic route has seen a massive spike in crossings since 2019.
Local news agency EFE confirmed the recovery of 14 bodies: ten men, three women, and a toddler.
Rescue officials suspect the dinghy departed from the coast of Western Sahara carrying roughly 75 people. It is still not entirely clear what caused the boat to capsize in the open ocean.
Spain's Interior Minister, Fernando Grande-Marlaska, placed the blame squarely on human smuggling rings. In a press release, he condemned the networks for showing "zero regard for human safety," accusing them of packing vulnerable people into flimsy, unseaworthy vessels without any life jackets.
The Gran Canaria disaster capped off a grim 48 hours for the region. Just hours earlier on Tuesday, Spanish officials confirmed they had pulled eight bodies from the water after a wooden migrant boat sank near Tenerife. While 82 people were pulled to safety in that operation, preliminary reports suggest the vessel had originally set sail from Senegal.
This comes just days after another tragedy over the weekend, when a separate dinghy carrying an estimated 60 migrants deflated off the coast of Fuerteventura. Dozens still remain missing.

New York (CNN Business)For many years, the world's most popular emerging markets have been the so-called BRICS: Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa.But given that Russia is no longer a market that Westerners can access following the invasion of Ukraine, it might be time for investors to stop lumping all of the emerging markets together."The BRICS had their day in the sun and that has faded," said Eric Winograd, senior economist at AllianceBernstein.Several major US index providers have removed Russian stocks from indexes at a price of "zero" or "effectively zero." Trading shares of several leading US-listed Russian companies, such as search engine Yandex and telecom MTS, have been halted. And the Moscow Stock Exchange has been closed since February 25, the day after the invasion.Russia could default on its debt within days"The idea that a country as large as Russia can be removed from indexes is a big deal," Winograd said.Read MoreIt seems likely that Russia will not be included in top emerging markets funds anytime soon. Even for those Westerners still willing to invest in Russian assets, it's not clear what comes next."Some investors are asking about exposure to Russia in emerging markets funds. With indexes starting to exclude Russia, it's still a wait-and-see game," said Mychal Campos, head of investing at Betterment.For investors looking to get exposure to emerging markets, Winograd said, each country should be looked at individually."The name of the game will be differentiation. Don't invest in emerging markets based on an acronym," Winograd said. "It's always strange to say that Argentina and South Korea are the same thing, for example. They're not." Forget the BRICS and look at TICKS or MIST?To stick with the acronyms, the shutdown of the Russian stock market has essentially turned the BRICS into the BICS — and that could be a permanent change, said Rahul Sen Sharma, managing partner with Indxx, a global index provider. "Will investors ever embrace Russia again? If there is no liquidity, it's a moot point. But it's also hard to believe that people will rush into Russia anytime soon," Sen Sharma told CNN Business.Sen Sharma said some investors may start to look at other emerging markets to replace Russia, such as Taiwan and South Korea. The BRICS could become the TICKS. He added that Poland, Turkey and Mexico are intriguing, as are the Philippines and Indonesia. You could dub Mexico, Indonesia, South Korea and Turkey the MIST markets. "People love their acronyms," Sen Sharma joked.Of course, any emerging market in Europe — think Poland —  is inherently risky because of how close it is geographically to Russia and Ukraine. So other Central and Eastern European nations may be a hard sell for Western investors.War in Ukraine has sparked a scramble for dollarsOther experts argue that investors are looking more at individual companies in emerging markets and less at the countries themselves."A typical investor sees emerging markets as a class of stocks. It's a piece of a portfolio," said Callie Cox, US investment analyst at eToro."The emerging markets landscape has been changing for awhile," Cox added, noting that many investors have been skeptical of Russia since its annexation of Crimea in 2014. "There has been more anxiety."

New York (CNN Business)For many years, the world's most popular technology stocks have been the so-called FAANGs: Facebook, Amazon, Apple, Netflix and Google. But given that Facebook (now Meta) and Netflix have seen massive shifts in their market dominance following recent economic changes, it might be time for investors to stop lumping all of the tech giants together.
"The FAANGs had their day in the sun and that has faded," said Sarah Lin, senior analyst at Horizon Wealth.
Several major US fund managers have reduced legacy social media stocks from portfolios to a fraction of their former weight. Trading shares of several leading consumer tech companies, has seen heavy volatility amid shifting user habits. And the broader tech sector has been under pressure since late 2021, the year after the pandemic boom.
Tech could face further corrections within months
"The idea that a company as large as Meta can lose so much value is a big deal," Lin said.
Read More
It seems likely that FAANG will not be the primary driver of top tech funds anytime soon. Even for those retail traders still willing to invest in legacy tech assets, it's not clear what comes next.
"Some investors are asking about exposure to artificial intelligence in tech funds. With indexes starting to rebalance away from the old guard, it's still a wait-and-see game," said Marcus Thorne, head of equities at Vanguard Partners.
For investors looking to get exposure to big tech, Lin said, each company should be looked at individually.
"The name of the game will be differentiation. Don't invest in tech based on an acronym," Lin said. "It's always strange to say that Apple and Netflix are the same thing, for example. They're not."
Forget the FAANGs and look at MATANA or the Magnificent Seven?
To stick with the acronyms, the inclusion of Microsoft and Nvidia has essentially turned the FAANGs into MATANA (Microsoft, Apple, Tesla, Alphabet, Nvidia, Amazon) — and that could be a permanent change, said David Vance, managing partner with IndexIQ, a global index provider.
"Will investors ever embrace the old FAANG again? If growth slows, it's a moot point. But it's also hard to believe that people will ignore AI anytime soon," Vance told CNN Business.
Vance said some investors may start to look at other tech sectors to replace streaming and social media, such as cloud computing and semiconductor manufacturing. The FAANG could become the FATANG. He added that AMD, Broadcom and Salesforce are intriguing, as are Adobe and Oracle. You could dub Microsoft, Nvidia, Apple and Tesla the MNAT markets.
"People love their acronyms," Vance joked.
Of course, any tech market in consumer software — think social media — is inherently risky because of how close it is financially to digital ad-spending cycles. So other ad-reliant companies may be a hard sell for institutional investors.
AI boom has sparked a scramble for semiconductors
Other experts argue that investors are looking more at individual companies in tech and less at the sectors themselves.
"A typical investor sees big tech as a class of stocks. It's a piece of a portfolio," said Elena Rostova, US investment analyst at TradeNet.
"The tech landscape has been changing for awhile," Rostova added, noting that many investors have been skeptical of social media since major privacy updates in 2021. "There has been more anxiety."

For a long time, the FAANG acronym—Facebook, Amazon, Apple, Netflix, and Google—ruled Wall Street. They were the undisputed heavyweights of the tech sector.
