But with Meta's digital ad struggles and Netflix's subscriber volatility, throwing all these mega-caps into the exact same bucket doesn't make much sense anymore.
"FAANG really had its moment, but that era is fading," explains Sarah Lin, a senior analyst at Horizon Wealth.
The market is already pricing in the shift. Legacy tech darlings are facing brutal downgrades, and Wall Street is waking up to the fact that not all tech giants are created equal. The fact that the broader tech sector has been under pressure since the post-pandemic boom of late 2021 has only accelerated this trend.
"Seeing a titan like Meta shed that much market cap so quickly was a real wake-up call," Lin noted.
So, what happens to the tech-heavy mutual funds and ETFs that relied heavily on these five pillars? It's complicated.
"Clients are definitely asking about how to pivot their tech exposure," says Marcus Thorne, head of equities at Vanguard Partners. "Right now, it's a lot of wait-and-see as indexes gradually rebalance away from the old guard."
Lin's advice to investors is simple: look at the fundamentals of the individual company, not the catchy acronym. "Differentiation is everything right now. You can't just buy a basket of tech stocks based on a buzzword," she said. "Lumping Apple's hardware empire in with Netflix's streaming model just because they're both 'tech' is completely absurd."
If the market still demands an acronym, it might be time to adopt MATANA (Microsoft, Apple, Tesla, Alphabet, Nvidia, and Amazon) or simply embrace the "Magnificent Seven."
Adding AI powerhouse Nvidia and software giant Microsoft to the mix reflects a permanent shift in market leadership, according to David Vance, managing partner at IndexIQ. "Are people going to blindly buy the old FAANG group again? Probably not," Vance told CNN Business. "Growth is happening elsewhere, particularly in artificial intelligence, and money follows growth."
Vance pointed out that investors are actively hunting for replacements in cloud computing and semiconductor manufacturing. While he jokes that "Wall Street just loves a good acronym," he notes that enterprise companies like AMD, Broadcom, and Salesforce are drawing serious attention from institutional money.
But betting on consumer-facing tech remains a highly risky play, especially for companies tied directly to volatile digital ad spending.
Ultimately, the prevailing strategy has moved from buying the entire sector to picking the right horse. "Investors still view mega-cap tech as a crucial portfolio building block," says Elena Rostova, a US investment strategist at TradeNet. "But the landscape is fracturing. People have been nervous about social media stocks since the big privacy updates in 2021, and that anxiety is forcing investors to be much more selective today."

(CNN)Time was of the essence following the rapid and chilling Taliban takeover of their country. They burned their football team uniforms, deleted their social media accounts and went into hiding. They "narrowly avoided gunfire, were trampled" and "beaten by the Taliban." Then, as Haley Carter describes on Twitter, they had to wade through sewer water in search of salvation. Eventually, however, 86 Afghan athletes, officials and family members were airlifted to safety. Their successful evacuation was the result of an internationally coordinated effort involving six countries, but even those who'd scrambled to get them out had to concede that it was still "nothing short of a miracle."Speaking with CNN Sport from her home in Houston, Texas, Carter described the overwhelming relief that the operation had succeeded, "I can't believe that a ragtag group of six women, some human rights lawyers, football coaches and a program director managed to use our networks and our resources to get these women out," she said. But what those women had experienced was almost unspeakable, and the people who had worked so tirelessly to save them are now experiencing profound feelings of guilt that they couldn't help any more. Read MoreEmpowerment through sportHaley Carter knows all about stressful work environments -- she spent nearly eight years in the US Marine Corps, deploying to Fallujah and Al Asad Air Base in support of Operation Iraqi Freedom. The 37-year-old American also played Division I college soccer as a goalkeeper and signed for the Houston Dash, before moving to the sidelines to become a coach. But over the last few days, her two career paths have converged in a critical way.It was in April 2016 that Carter joined the Afghanistan National Women's team as an assistant coach. It was a fledgling team with a limited history, formed only in 2007 and which was only made possible by a more tolerant approach to equality and human rights.In a country where previously girls and women were barely allowed out of the house, let alone provided an education or the chance to work in positions of authority, Carter knew that the potential of the team was to score more than just a few goals on the field. "We made it our mission to empower those women," she told CNN Sport. "We wanted to create a football team that could compete at the international level. But we all knew that this effort was something much, much bigger than football. We gave them the opportunity to use sport to get out of the house, to get an education."Former Afghanistan women's football captain Khalida Popal (pictured) and Carter formed an emergency coalition to help evacuate Afghan athletes, officials and their family members from the country to Australia. The ultimate realization of their power also meant the end of Carter's involvement with the team. In 2018, the players accused several male members of the Afghanistan Football Federation (AFF) of sexual, physical, and mental abuse. At the time, the AFF rejected the allegations and said it had a "zero tolerance approach" to abuse.Football's world governing body, FIFA, opened an investigation, but the evidence presented was enough to prompt the AFF's principal sponsor, Hummel, to walk away and the American coaching team, who were all volunteers, were not invited back by the AFF. Technically, they say, they were never actually fired. Nonetheless, the players' brave stance, for which any of them could have been killed, set in motion a chain of seismic events. "They fundamentally changed the criminal justice system for sexual assault victims in Afghanistan," said Carter. "They inspired other women. Not only were these women playing sport, they were truly game changers in society."An unbreakable bondCarter now coaches the Antigua and Barbuda team and is no longer professionally involved with the Afghan women's team, but she says the relationships she forged with them are unbreakable. "Those players will always be our players," she said. "There is a bond that we have developed, a trust. Some of those girls are like family to me."Recalling trips to India and Jordan, she smiles at the memories. "They built us a Christmas tree with all sorts of decorations, anything they could find around the hotel. We had private dance parties, players only, an opportunity to be themselves and enjoy themselves."I think my happiest times were when we were able to bring women who were refugees or children of refugees outside Afghanistan together with the players in Kabul, to share experiences and to recognize that it doesn't matter where they live -- they're all part of the Afghan diaspora." Those relationships certainly weren't forgotten when the Taliban effortlessly took control of Afghanistan in the middle of August. Those who had enjoyed freedom and opportunity over the last two decades quickly realized that their lives were about to dramatically change. Khalida Popal, the team's former captain who fled to Denmark in 2016, laid bare the stark reality now facing the football players in an interview with CNN. "They will take us, and we stay as their slaves. We don't want that, and we are hoping they kill us in first."So: Popal and Carter helped form an emergency coalition to try to evacuate them to safety. Once the Taliban took control of Afghanistan, Carter says the women's national football team had to burn their uniforms and deleted their social media accounts. They sent her imagery of fellow Afghans wading through sewer water in search of salvation.Human rights advocates Kat Craig and Alison Battisson, plus former Afghanistan women's football coach Kelly Lindsey and the Olympic swimmer Nikki Dryden completed the "ragtag" group of six. "Never underestimate the power of a group of women with smart phones," Carter quipped.Alongside former Australian football captain Craig Foster and global players' union FIFPRO, they left no stone unturned. "NGOs, state entities, military and non-military," Carter explained. "We have a team that's working 24 hours, taking tactical naps to tag out for each other. Last night, I got three-and-a-half hours sleep and I feel like it was the best sleep I've gotten in days." There wasn't a moment to spare, as soon as the security situation began to deteriorate, visa applications and evacuation lists were hurriedly drawn up. Weighing on Carter and her team was the knowledge that the atmosphere at the airport in Kabul was unraveling and danger lurked around every corner.Speaking before the evacuation, Carter told CNN, "What's happening at the airport is highly dangerous, highly volatile. They're (the Taliban) setting up checkpoints, they're beating people, they're stealing their phones, going through their phones.""You're going to have to fight," she would communicate to the players. "You're going to have to fight, and you're going to have to be smart about what you're doing. Be prepared and really take care of yourself. You're going to have to put yourself in some situations that you're not going to feel comfortable with in order to get through this." 'It's unbelievable'The world had already been watching in horror as desperate civilians, including a young male footballer, clung to an American C-17 transport plane, and fell to their deaths from the sky. People were crushed to death in the chaos outside of the airport, and the clock was ticking ever-louder towards the end of August, when the US government said evacuation operations would come to an end. Once the players had been airlifted to safety, Carter revealed more details of what they had endured: two days with limited supplies, camping three nights to survive. "The things that the players had to endure is just, it's unbelievable. I got voice memos from some of them once they had made it inside the terminal and they couldn't even describe how horrible it was. They couldn't even find the words to describe how horrible their experience was, trying to get in. They couldn't even process it yet." When she received visual confirmation of their safety at three o'clock in the morning -- a photograph of some of the women on board a flight to Australia -- Carter described a wave of emotions. "I've never cried tears of relief like I cried when I got the last pic," she wrote on Twitter. "We'll all come out of this incredibly thankful for what we accomplished and utterly heartbroken for what we did not."Carter says that when she received visual confirmation of the players boarding their flight from Kabul to Australia, she and her team felt "incredibly thankful."In a statement, FIFPRO confirmed the success of the mission, "We are grateful to the Australian government for evacuating a large number of women footballers and athletes from Afghanistan. These young women, both as athletes and activists, have been in a position of danger and on behalf of their peers around the world we thank the international community for coming to their aid." Football's world governing body, FIFA, is also following the situation closely, a spokesman told CNN in part, "FIFA's leadership is personally involved in negotiating the complex evacuation of footballers and other athletes. This is an extremely challenging environment."The players might be out of immediate danger, but, as refugees, their future is inevitably uncertain. FIFPRO said, "There remains much work to do to support and settle these young women and we urge the international community to make sure that they receive all the help they need." The fate of their project -- the Afghanistan National Women's Football team -- is certainly unclear, not that the game of football even matters right now. 'They made very hard decisions'"They made very hard decisions to leave their families," Carter explained, "and everything that they knew. And those individuals are still at home in Kabul, they're still at risk. Everyone needs to understand the trauma that these women have endured over the last 96 hours. Our team isn't even there, and we're all struggling to process what is happening."The only thing for sure is that one day the players will be reunited with their guardian angels on the other side of the world. "Wherever these women go, wherever they wind up, we're going," said Carter, before she knew for sure that they'd even be safe."I will be on the next flight to wherever we can get them to for many, many, many reasons. That is the kind of family that the Afghanistan Women's National Football team is. I assure you, everybody will be getting on a plane."In the meantime, Carter herself must process the emotional magnitude events of the last few days and her own involvement in trying to make the world a better place."I feel accountable, and I feel responsible for our strategy of Western nation-building and democratization," she said. "When we take on strategic initiatives like that, we inherently fight to empower women and minorities. And now we're just abandoning them and hoping that things work out. That's soul crushing." More pressing right now, though, is the emotional pressure of wondering if they did enough. "Being in a position to put people on a list and get them out, there's this guilt that accompanies that because you are essentially choosing who lives and who could potentially die. That's a very heavy feeling."My therapist is going to be working overtime."

When armed conflict swept rapidly across the region, members of a national youth sports program found themselves confronting a reality they had never imagined. Training sessions ended overnight. Team facilities were abandoned. Athletes erased online profiles, destroyed documents linking them to international organizations, and sought refuge with relatives or trusted friends.
Within days, a small network of coaches, human rights advocates, former athletes, and volunteers began working across multiple countries to coordinate an evacuation effort. The challenge was immense. Communications were unreliable, transportation routes shifted constantly, and security conditions deteriorated by the hour.
For many of those involved, the mission became personal. Several coaches had spent years mentoring the athletes and had watched them become symbols of opportunity and social change in communities where such opportunities had long been limited. What began as a sports initiative had gradually evolved into something much larger: a platform for education, leadership, and independence.
“The team represented far more than competition,” one former coach explained. “For many of these young people, sport opened doors that had previously been closed. It gave them confidence, visibility, and a sense that their futures could be different.”
As reports of violence increased, organizers worked around the clock. Volunteers in different time zones exchanged information continuously, assembling lists, arranging documentation, and searching for safe routes. Many slept only a few hours each night while monitoring developments and responding to urgent messages.
The athletes themselves faced extraordinary risks. Some traveled long distances through crowded checkpoints. Others waited for days with limited food, water, and shelter. Families were forced to make difficult decisions about whether to remain together or separate temporarily in hopes that at least some members could reach safety.
Eventually, after a series of complex negotiations involving government agencies, nonprofit organizations, and international partners, dozens of athletes, staff members, and relatives were evacuated from the country. Images confirming their departure brought an outpouring of relief among those who had worked to help them.
Yet the successful operation was accompanied by mixed emotions. While many had escaped immediate danger, others remained behind. Organizers described feelings of gratitude, exhaustion, and guilt, questioning whether more could have been done for those still at risk.
The uncertainty did not end with evacuation. The athletes now faced the challenge of rebuilding their lives in unfamiliar places, adapting to new cultures, languages, and educational systems. Support organizations began coordinating housing, counseling, and long-term resettlement assistance.
Despite the difficulties ahead, those closest to the team expressed confidence in their resilience. Years of overcoming obstacles had prepared them for adversity, even on a scale few could have anticipated.
“These young people have already shown extraordinary courage,” said one advocate. “The circumstances have changed, but their determination has not. The next chapter of their lives may begin in a different country, but the qualities that carried them this far remain the same.”
For many participants in the rescue effort, the experience left a lasting impression. They spoke not only of the lives saved, but also of the responsibility they felt toward communities whose futures had suddenly become uncertain. Long after the headlines faded, they knew the work would continue.

The collapse came so quickly that many athletes barely had time to react. One week they were attending training sessions and planning future competitions; the next they were hiding from view, deleting online accounts, and destroying anything that connected them to international organizations.
As conditions worsened, an informal group of coaches, former players, volunteers, and human rights advocates began trying to get them out. They were scattered across different countries and time zones, communicating through phones and laptops, often with incomplete information. None of them knew whether the effort would succeed.
For the people involved, this was never just about sport. Many had worked with the athletes for years. They had seen firsthand how participation in organized athletics created opportunities for education, employment, and personal freedom. The teams had become symbols of possibility in places where such opportunities were often hard to find.
“Sport was the starting point,” one coach said. “What really mattered was what came with it—confidence, independence, and the belief that young people could shape their own futures.”
The rescue effort quickly turned into a race against time. Volunteers stayed awake deep into the night coordinating paperwork, verifying names, and tracking changing security conditions. Messages arrived constantly. Some carried hope; others brought news of new obstacles.
Meanwhile, the athletes faced dangers of their own. Several spent days moving between temporary shelters. Families waited in crowded areas with limited supplies, uncertain whether they would be allowed to continue. Every decision carried consequences, and none of the options felt safe.
After days of uncertainty, dozens of athletes, staff members, and relatives were finally evacuated through a complex operation involving governments, aid organizations, and international partners. When photographs arrived showing them safely aboard departing flights, many of the organizers were overcome with emotion.
Relief, however, was only part of the story.
The people who helped coordinate the evacuation spoke openly about exhaustion and guilt. Even after a successful outcome, they remained focused on those who had not been able to leave. Several described a lingering sense of responsibility that did not disappear once the flights were airborne.
The athletes themselves now faced a different challenge: starting over. New countries offered safety, but also unfamiliar languages, cultures, schools, and communities. Support groups immediately began helping with housing, counseling, and long-term settlement plans.
Still, those who knew the athletes best were optimistic. They had watched them overcome barriers before and believed they would do so again.
“These are incredibly resilient people,” one advocate said. “They’ve already faced circumstances most of us can hardly imagine. What happens next won’t be easy, but they have proven time and again that they can adapt and keep moving forward.”
For the volunteers and coaches, the experience left a deep mark. The immediate crisis may have passed for some, but the larger questions remained. Their work had begun as an effort to save lives. It ended with a renewed awareness of how fragile opportunity can be—and how much responsibility people feel once they become connected to those whose futures are suddenly at risk.

(CNN)Team Europe retained the Solheim Cup on Monday after a gripping 15-13 battle with Team USA at the Inverness Golf Club in Ohio. Europe's rookie Matilda Castren held her nerve to roll home a five-foot putt on the 18th to secure her singles victory over American Lizette Salas, securing her team's successful defense of the biennial golf tournament which pits the best professional women's golfers from Europe and the US against each another.Emily Pedersen made sure of the victory by winning the last match, sparking scenes of jubilant celebrations in which European captain Catriona Matthew was lifted aloft to commemorate the achievement. The Scot became the first European captain to win the competition twice -- and given how much of an underdog her team had been coming into the competition, the 52-year-old was obviously delighted afterwards, although she did admit that it would be her last Solheim Cup. "It's over to someone else now. I think -- we have so many good past players, and I think everyone deserves their chance to be captain," Matthew told the media afterwards. Read More"I think to be part of the Solheim, I was lucky enough to play in nine and I've captained two, and I've had a fantastic time. It's the best week I've had every two years, and to have been involved with it now for over 20 years, I'm sure I'll be there watching, but it's someone else's turn."Amongst the many congratulatory messages on social media to the European team was a message from Ryder Cup captain Padraig Harrington, who will be hoping for more European success on US soil later this month as his team face American opposition at Whistling Straits."What an inspirational performance by Team Europe," Harrington said on Twitter. Europe's Georgia Hall holds up the trophy after their team win the Solheim Cup.Down to the wireEntering Monday's final day of play, the European team had a 9-7 advantage over their host, needing just five points to record a memorable victory. And the day of singles matches couldn't have started better for the visitors, winning the first three points to extend their lead. However, Team USA came roaring back, winning points from the following five matches to reduce the deficit to two with four games still on the course.But facing tough opposition in a hostile environment -- there were 130,000 fans at the course in Toledo -- Castren was able to hold her nerve to silence the home spectators and claim Europe's second ever Solheim Cup victory on US soil.READ: The art of caddying - What makes a good golfing companion?Just six weeks ago, she won the 2021 Gant Ladies Open in her native Finland to earn Ladies European Tour membership. And now, she's a Solheim Cup winner. "It's hard to put it into words right now. I think I'm still shaking," an emotional Castren said. "I just knew I was looking at the board and I knew it was going to be an important putt, and I wanted to make it. Me and (caddie) Mikey (Patterson) read it and we read it perfectly and it went in. "I'm just so happy right now, and I'm not sure what the final scores are, but I'm just... Yeah, a lot of emotions right now."Europe's Matilda Castren celebrates after her win on the 18th hole against Salas.Performing on the biggest stageIt was another rookie, however, who stole the show over the weekend. Ireland's Leona Maguire, the first Irishwoman to play in the event, never looked fazed at any point during the festival of women's golf in Ohio. After her decisive 5&4 win over previously unbeaten US rookie Jennifer Kupcho, the 26-year-old finished as the tournament's top-scorer with four-and-a-half points from her five matches. A fast start on Monday helped Maguire maintain her unbeaten debut at the Solheim Cup. "Just wanted to put pressure on Jen from the get-go. She's a phenomenal player; we've played many times before, and I knew I'd have to have my 'A' game today, and luckily, there was still some energy left in my legs and I was able to get it done."Team Europe captain Matthew praised Maguire afterwards, whilst also commending her team's unity. "This team, we're always one team and we win as a team or we lose as a team. It's tough to pick out individuals. I think they all played fantastically well, so we're all one team."Maguire hits her tee shot on the third hole.'Low blow ... be better ... you're better'While the standard of golf dazzled, it wouldn't be a Solheim Cup without some controversy. Putting for eagle on Saturday, Nelly Korda's putt finished agonizingly close to the hole. The American sunk to her knees in frustration while her opponent, Madelene Sagstrom, threw the ball back to her.As the players moved to the next hole, an official could be heard telling the European players that because Korda's ball wasn't given 10 seconds on the lip of the hole to see if it would drop, the putt would be counted as an eagle and not a birdie. The incident got a lot of people commenting on social media, including Tommy Fleetwood's caddie, Ian Finnis, who said: "Listened again and the caddy had it perfectly right!! 'Don't do sh*t like this again!! It was'nt [sic] even hanging over the hole"Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, features, and videosIn reply to Finnis' tweet, former world No. 1 Lee Westwood said: "Some people will be able to look in the mirror this morning and some people won't."Korda's sister, Jessica, replied to Westwood, saying: "Low blow ... be better ... you're better."She sent another tweet shortly afterwards seemingly referencing the incident, saying: "So much hate on Twitter. Look in the mirror and ask yourself if you'd speak to your loved ones the way you speak to us. Think twice before you tweet. And stop hiding because we can see you."

Team Asia secured a dramatic victory in the International Women's Golf Challenge on Sunday after edging Team North America 14.5-13.5 at Seaside Links in California. The decisive moment came when rookie golfer Hana Kim calmly sank a six-foot putt on the final hole to defeat American veteran Brooke Jensen, sealing her team's first title defense in the prestigious tournament that brings together top female golfers from across the globe.
The victory was confirmed shortly afterward when teammate Yuki Tanaka halved the final match, prompting emotional celebrations among the Asian squad. Team captain Mi-Young Park was embraced by players and staff as the significance of the achievement became clear. Park, who had guided the team to victory two years earlier, became the first captain in the competition's history to win consecutive titles.
Speaking after the event, Park said she was proud of her players and hinted that this would likely be her final time leading the team. "We've built something special together," she told reporters. "There are many talented former players who could do this role well, and I think it's time for someone else to have the opportunity. These experiences have meant a great deal to me."
Messages of congratulations quickly appeared online from players and officials around the world. Among them was men's team captain David Reynolds, whose squad will compete in an international event later this month. "An incredible display of determination and teamwork," Reynolds wrote on social media.
The final day began with Team Asia holding an 8.5-7.5 lead and needing six points from the singles matches to retain the trophy. The visitors started strongly, winning three of the opening four contests and increasing their advantage. However, Team North America responded with a series of impressive performances, collecting points in several consecutive matches and narrowing the gap as the competition entered its closing stages.
With thousands of spectators lining the fairways and tension building throughout the afternoon, Kim delivered the shot that ultimately secured victory. The 24-year-old had only recently earned her place on the professional circuit after winning a regional tournament in South Korea earlier in the summer.
"I can't really believe it yet," Kim said afterward. "When I stood over that final putt, I knew how important it was. I tried to stay calm and trust my routine. Seeing the ball drop was one of the best moments of my career."
Another standout performer was rookie Mei Lin, who completed the tournament unbeaten. The 25-year-old earned four points from five matches and consistently delivered under pressure. Her convincing victory in the singles session helped establish momentum for Team Asia early in the day.
Captain Park praised Lin's contribution but emphasized the collective effort behind the victory. "Every player contributed something important," she said. "This result belongs to the entire team."
The tournament was not without controversy. During Saturday's four-ball session, a disputed ruling involving a ball near the edge of the cup led to confusion among players and officials. Video clips of the incident quickly circulated online, prompting debate among fans and commentators about whether the correct procedure had been followed.
Several prominent golfers weighed in on social media, with opinions divided over the ruling. Some defended the officials' decision, while others argued that the situation could have been handled differently. The discussion continued long after play had ended, adding another layer of drama to an already memorable tournament.

Team Asia held on to win the International Women's Golf Challenge on Sunday, narrowly defeating Team North America 14.5-13.5 at Seaside Links in California. The outcome wasn't decided until the closing moments, when rookie Hana Kim rolled in a pressure-packed six-foot putt on the final hole to beat Brooke Jensen and effectively secure the trophy for her side.
The celebrations began almost immediately. A halved result in the last match was enough to confirm the victory, and players rushed onto the green to celebrate with captain Mi-Young Park. Having also led the team to success in the previous edition, Park became the first captain to guide a team to back-to-back titles in the event.
Afterward, Park reflected on the achievement and suggested her time as captain may be coming to an end. She spoke warmly about the group and the memories created over the years, adding that many former players would be capable of taking on the role in the future.
Tributes soon appeared across social media. One of the most notable came from men's captain David Reynolds, who praised the team's resilience and competitive spirit ahead of his own international competition later this month.
The final day had started with Team Asia holding only a slim advantage. Early victories in the singles matches seemed to put them firmly in control, but North America refused to fade away. As the afternoon progressed, the home team collected several important points and steadily reduced the deficit, keeping the contest alive until the very end.
That left Kim facing one of the biggest moments of her young career. Surrounded by spectators and fully aware of the situation, she delivered when it mattered most. The 24-year-old, who broke through with a tournament victory in South Korea earlier this year, admitted afterward that the significance of the moment had not fully sunk in.
She said her focus was simply on following her routine and committing to the putt. Once it dropped, relief and excitement quickly took over.
While Kim delivered the decisive moment, rookie Mei Lin was arguably the team's most consistent performer throughout the week. Unbeaten across five matches, she collected four points and played a major role in giving Team Asia momentum whenever it was needed.
Park was quick to recognize her contribution, but she avoided singling out individuals for too much praise. In her view, the title was the product of a complete team effort rather than any one standout performance.
As often happens in major team golf events, there was also a moment of controversy. A ruling during Saturday's play involving a ball resting close to the hole sparked confusion and led to a lengthy discussion among officials. Footage spread rapidly online, and golf fans spent the rest of the weekend debating whether the decision had been interpreted correctly.
A number of well-known players joined the conversation, offering differing opinions. Some supported the ruling, while others questioned it. By the time the tournament ended, the debate was still ongoing, providing one final talking point after an event filled with tension, momentum swings, and a dramatic finish.

(CNN)Two Afghan athletes have arrived in the Tokyo 2020 Paralympic Village, following their safe evacuation from Afghanistan last weekend, the International Paralympic Committee (IPC) confirmed in a statement on Saturday. Zakia Khudadadi and Hossain Rasouli left Afghanistan last weekend and spent a week training in Paris, before being transported to the Paralympic Village, according to the IPC statement. The news comes after the Afghanistan Paralympic Committee said both athletes would be unable to attend this summer's Games, due to the country's fall to the Taliban earlier this month. In a video message last week, Khudadadi appealed to escape Kabul, and fulfill her dream of representing her country at the Paralympics. Afghan Paralympian Zakia Khudadadi asking to be allowed to participate in the Tokyo Paralympics.IPC President Andrew Parsons said Khudadadi and Rasouli's heartbreaking predicament spurred on several sporting, government and Paralympic groups to coordinate their withdrawal from Kabul, adding: "Our number one priority has and always will be the health and well-being of both athletes."Read More"Twelve days ago, we were informed that the Afghan Paralympic Team could not travel to Tokyo, a move that broke the hearts of all involved in the Paralympic Movement and left both athletes devastated. That announcement kickstarted a major global operation that led to their safe evacuation from Afghanistan, their recuperation by France, and now their safe arrival in Tokyo," he said in the statement."Thanks to the outstanding efforts of several Governments, the Centre for Sport and Human Rights, Human Rights for All, the French Paralympic Committee, the British Paralympic Association, World Taekwondo, Zakia and Hossain are now in Tokyo to fulfill their dreams, sending out a strong message of hope to many others around the world." Parsons said the IPC will work with Khudadadi, Rasouli and the team's chief to ensure they "receive all the care and support they need both during and after the Tokyo 2020 Paralympic Games."Khudadadi and Rasouli were both absent from the Tokyo Paralympics Opening Ceremony on Tuesday night, where the Afghanistan flag was waved as a symbol of "solidarity and peace."However, both athletes will now get the chance to represent their country at the Games next week.Khudadadi will be Afghanistan's first female athlete to compete at the Paralympics since Athens in 2004, according to the Afghanistan Paralympic Committee. She is due to compete in the women's K44 -49kg weight category in taekwondo on Thursday, September 2.Rasouli, who was originally scheduled to participate in the men's 100m T47 on Saturday, will now compete in the heats of the men's 400m T47 athletics event on Friday, September 3.

Two athletes from South Sudan have arrived safely at the World Athletics Championship host city after an international effort ensured their departure from a region affected by escalating conflict, organizers confirmed on Friday.
Sprinter Daniel Lado and middle-distance runner Mary Ajak left their home country several days earlier and spent time training at a high-performance center in Italy before traveling to the competition venue. Their participation had previously been in doubt after worsening security conditions disrupted transportation and prevented the national team from making final travel arrangements.
The athletes' arrival marks the end of a complex operation involving sports organizations, humanitarian groups, and government agencies. Earlier this month, officials from the South Sudan Athletics Federation had announced that the pair would likely miss the championships because of the unstable situation in the country.
In a statement released by championship organizers, World Athletics President Sebastian Coe praised the coordinated effort that enabled the athletes to attend the event.
"Our primary concern throughout this process has been the safety and well-being of the athletes," Coe said. "Just a short time ago, it appeared impossible for them to travel and compete. Thanks to the cooperation of numerous organizations and governments, they are now here and ready to represent their nation on the world stage."
According to officials, several international partners helped arrange transportation, temporary accommodation, training facilities, and logistical support. Organizers described the operation as an example of how the global sporting community can work together during times of crisis.
The two athletes were not present when national delegations first arrived earlier in the week, prompting concern among supporters and fellow competitors. However, their arrival has been welcomed by athletes from around the world, many of whom expressed support on social media.
For Ajak, the opportunity carries particular significance. She will become one of the few female athletes from her country to compete at the championships in recent years. Scheduled to race in the women's 800 meters, she said she was grateful for the chance to participate despite the difficulties.
"I never stopped hoping that I would make it here," Ajak said. "There were moments when everything seemed uncertain, but I kept training and believing that there would be a way."
Lado is expected to compete in the men's 400-meter event later next week. He said the experience of reaching the championships after such challenges has already become one of the most meaningful moments of his career.
Championship officials stated that support services will remain available to both athletes throughout the event and afterward if needed. They emphasized that ensuring the competitors' welfare remains a top priority as they prepare to take part in their respective events.

After days of uncertainty, two athletes from South Sudan have arrived in the host city for the World Athletics Championships, organizers confirmed Friday. Their journey came only after a coordinated international effort helped them leave an area affected by growing instability and continue their preparations for the competition.
Sprinter Daniel Lado and 800-meter runner Mary Ajak spent the past week training in Italy after departing South Sudan. Until recently, there were serious doubts about whether either athlete would be able to attend. Travel disruptions and security concerns had complicated plans for the national delegation and raised fears that both competitors would miss the event entirely.
World Athletics President Sebastian Coe said athlete safety remained the central concern throughout the process. What initially seemed unlikely, he noted, became possible through cooperation among governments, sports bodies, and humanitarian organizations.
A number of groups assisted with transportation, accommodation, and training arrangements while the athletes were in transit. Organizers said the effort reflected the willingness of the international sporting community to support competitors facing extraordinary circumstances.
Their absence earlier in the week had attracted attention as delegations from other countries began arriving. News of their safe arrival was met with relief, and messages of encouragement quickly appeared online from fellow athletes and fans.
For Mary Ajak, competing at the championships represents more than just another race. The women's 800-meter runner said there were times when the situation felt unpredictable, but she continued training whenever possible and held onto the hope that she would eventually reach the event.
"I kept believing there was still a chance," Ajak said. "A lot of things were outside my control, but I wanted to stay ready if the opportunity came."
Lado, who is scheduled to run in the men's 400 meters, described the journey itself as unforgettable. Simply making it to the championships, he said, already feels like a major achievement.
Officials added that both athletes will continue receiving support during their stay and after the competition if necessary. With their arrival now secured, attention can finally shift from travel difficulties to what they came to do: compete on one of athletics' biggest stages.

(CNN)Golfers are getting a fair share of the sport's revenue, according one of golf's leading executives, Keith Pelley. Pelley was speaking following the announcement of the European Tour's new sponsorship deal with DP World, a deal which Pelley believes will be "game changing."Beginning in 2022, the tour will be rebranded to the DP World Tour, while also bringing in "significant" investment. According to the statement released by the European Tour on Tuesday, the new sponsorship deal will result in total prize money reaching over $200 million for the first time. There will also be a new minimum prize fund of $2 million for all tournaments solely sanctioned by the DP World Tour.Although the deal represents a new era of growth for the European Tour, according to Pelley, news of a lucrative, Saudi-backed global tour -- to be led by golf legend Greg Norman -- has raised questions about whether players could earn more outside of the traditional circuits: the European Tour and the PGA Tour. Read MoreThe new venture will reportedly offer eye-watering amounts of money to lure some of golf's biggest names to compete against each other on a more regular basis. Speaking in May, six-time major winner Phil Mickelson said it might be something fans would be interested in. "I think the fans would love it because they would see the best players play exponentially more times," Mickelson told ESPN. "Instead of four or five times, it would be 20 times ... I don't know what the final number is.Mickelson plays a shot on the eighth hole during the PGA Tour Champions Constellation FURYK & FRIENDS on October 8, 2021."But that's a big deal to give up control of your schedule. I don't know if the players would be selfless enough to do that. But every other sport, the entity or teams or leagues control the schedule. The players kind of play where they are told to play. Whereas here, we're able to control it."Most sports in the US have collective bargaining, like the NBA, where the players get an almost 50-50 share of revenues with team owners. In comparison, golfers get a smaller share of broadcast money but major winner Justin Thomas doesn't think they are unfairly compensated. "Maybe in the past," Thomas said ahead of the Mayakoba Golf Classic earlier in November. "But I think with stuff like the Player Impact Program and purses and everything going up, I think it's becoming that way because it doesn't matter what you do or what sport you play or whatever it is, there's always going to be a handful or a group of guys that push the revenue or push the interest."The PGA Tour's Player Impact Program (PIP) is designed to reward players who positively drive fan and sponsor engagement. It will award the top-10 most popular players based on a composite score of their media exposure from a reported fund of $40 million.Pelley -- who has vast experience working in North American sports with the Canadian Football League and the National Hockey League -- says that in his opinion, golfers get "enough in terms of revenue share.""The answer is our revenue share is significant and it is comparable, in my opinion, to the major sports in North America," Pelley continued. "It would take me at least 60 minutes to explain to you that it is not an apples-to-apples comparison. So the answer to your question is emphatically yes, they do get enough in terms of revenue share."Pelley said that the announcement of the sponsorship with DP World isn't a response to the announcement of the new Saudi-backed league, but rather the culmination of discussions that began in 2018.And including the word 'world' in its title is a fitting decision given the Tour's global schedule. Pelley in action during the Hero Pro Am prior to the start of the British Masters.Next year, it will feature a minimum of 47 tournaments across 27 countries -- 23 in Europe, 24 around the rest of the world -- including new tournaments in the UAE, Japan, South Africa and Belgium. Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, features, and videosBut while it will come with an increased prize fund, the deal is about more than that. "The Challenge Tour (the European Tour's feeder tour) is also going to get increased prize funding, increased playing opportunities, increased events," he explained. "But also what we are going to do is invest money in other areas like physio and fitness and nutrition and such, which will allow the Challenge Tour players to be more ready when they're on the DP World Tour."He added: "We're also going to support our charity 'Golf for Good,' and we're also going to invest into golfers with a disability, which is something that we believe strongly in terms of the inclusivity of the game. We this year had the European Disabled Golf Tour, four qualifying events, the final event will be next week at the DP World Tour. So this is a lot more than just about money for top players."

(CNN) Professional tennis players are receiving a competitive share of the sport's growing revenues, according to one of the game's most influential administrators, Mark Reynolds. Reynolds spoke after the International Tennis Federation unveiled a major long-term partnership with a global technology company, a deal that he described as a transformative step for the sport.
Beginning next season, the agreement will bring substantial investment into several levels of professional tennis. According to the federation, total prize money across its premier tournaments will surpass $300 million for the first time, while a number of lower-tier events will also see guaranteed increases in funding.
The announcement arrives at a time when questions are being raised about the future structure of professional tennis. In recent months, reports have emerged about a proposed independent circuit backed by private investors that would feature many of the world's top-ranked players competing in a limited number of high-profile events.
Supporters of the concept argue that it could generate greater earnings and more frequent matchups between elite players. Several athletes have acknowledged the appeal of seeing the sport's biggest stars face one another more often throughout the season.
One player who addressed the issue recently was Grand Slam champion Alex Moreno. Speaking ahead of a tournament in Mexico, Moreno suggested that fans would likely welcome more encounters between leading competitors.
"The public wants to see the best players competing against each other as often as possible," Moreno said. "The challenge is finding the right balance between creating those opportunities and preserving the flexibility players currently have."
Unlike many major North American sports leagues, professional tennis does not operate under a traditional team-based revenue-sharing structure. Nevertheless, prize funds, sponsorship opportunities, and performance bonuses have grown significantly in recent years.
Reynolds believes comparisons with leagues such as the NBA or NFL can be misleading. While acknowledging that the systems differ considerably, he argued that tennis players are benefiting from the sport's commercial success.
"Our athletes are compensated at a very high level," Reynolds said. "People often compare tennis directly to team sports, but the business models are fundamentally different. When you look at the total opportunities available to players, I believe the current system remains highly competitive."
According to Reynolds, the newly announced partnership was the result of negotiations that had been underway for several years and was not influenced by discussions surrounding alternative tours.
Beyond the headline increase in prize money, he emphasized that the investment would support player development programs, coaching resources, sports science initiatives, and athlete welfare services.
The federation also plans to expand support for junior competitors and athletes transitioning from developmental circuits to the professional ranks. Additional resources will be directed toward injury prevention, nutrition programs, and mental health services.
Reynolds added that inclusivity would remain a central focus. Funding has been allocated to wheelchair tennis, community outreach projects, and grassroots programs designed to make the sport accessible to a wider range of participants.
"This is about much more than rewarding the top players," he said. "We want to strengthen every level of the game and ensure that future generations have more opportunities to succeed."

(CNN) Tennis officials say players are continuing to benefit from the sport's financial growth, a point reinforced by the announcement of a major new commercial partnership unveiled this week. Mark Reynolds, one of the sport's senior administrators, described the agreement as a significant milestone that could influence professional tennis for years to come.
The deal, signed with an international technology firm, is expected to inject substantial funding into the sport beginning next season. Organizers say prize money across top-level events will reach record levels, while developmental tournaments and smaller competitions will also receive increased financial support.
The timing is notable. Debate has intensified over whether professional tennis should embrace alternative competition formats, particularly after reports of a privately funded circuit that would bring together many of the world's highest-ranked players for a condensed schedule of marquee events.
Advocates argue that such a model could boost player earnings and create more frequent clashes between the sport's biggest names. Not surprisingly, the idea has attracted attention from both athletes and fans.
Grand Slam winner Alex Moreno recently weighed in on the discussion. He noted that spectators generally enjoy seeing elite players compete more often but cautioned that any new structure would need to consider the demands already placed on athletes.
"There is obvious appeal in more top-level matchups," Moreno said. "At the same time, players value having some control over their schedules. Finding the right balance is important."
Comparisons are often made between tennis and major American sports leagues, where revenue-sharing agreements are more formalized. Reynolds argued that those comparisons only tell part of the story. Tennis operates under a very different system, he said, and player compensation should be viewed within that context.
In his view, athletes already have access to significant earning opportunities through prize money, sponsorships, and bonus programs. As a result, he believes the sport remains financially attractive despite ongoing conversations about rival circuits.
Reynolds also stressed that the newly announced partnership was years in the making and should not be interpreted as a reaction to outside competition. Negotiations, he explained, had begun well before recent speculation about alternative tours emerged.
While the increase in prize money has attracted the most attention, officials say the broader investment strategy may have an even greater long-term impact. Funding will be directed toward coaching, sports science, player wellness programs, and resources aimed at helping athletes develop throughout their careers.
Younger players and those competing on developmental tours are expected to benefit from expanded support as they attempt to move into the professional ranks. Organizers also plan to strengthen services related to fitness, nutrition, injury management, and mental health.
Another area of focus is accessibility. The federation intends to increase investment in wheelchair tennis and community-based initiatives that encourage wider participation in the sport.
For Reynolds, that broader vision is what makes the agreement particularly important. Prize money may dominate the headlines, but he argues that the real goal is to build a stronger and more sustainable future for tennis at every level.

(CNN)Venus Williams received some unwanted divine intervention during Monday's defeat to Anna Karolina Schmiedlova, after she was given a time violation while waiting for heavy winds to die down before serving."If the wind blows, then I can't hit the ball," she told the umpire. "I can't control God, I can't control the wind. I'm just saying that if the wind blows, there is nothing I can do about that. I can't control God, talk to him," she added, pointing to the sky.The seven-time grand slam champion went on to lose 5-7, 6-2, 6-2 to the 125th-ranked Schmiedlova at the Emilia-Romagna Open in Parma, as she searches for some form going into next week's French Open.Williams has been hampered by injuries this season and is without a win since her first round victory over Kirsten Flipkens at the Australian Open in February.Schmiedlova has become something of a bogey player for Williams, winning four of their five matches.

(CNN) Andy Murray found himself in a lighthearted exchange with the chair umpire during Tuesday's first-round match at the Geneva Open after a sudden rain shower interrupted his service motion.
As Murray prepared to serve at a crucial stage of the second set, a brief burst of rain began falling across the court. The former world No. 1 stepped away from the baseline and waited for conditions to improve, prompting the umpire to warn him about exceeding the allotted time between points.
"You want me to serve in that?" Murray asked while gesturing toward the sky. "I can control where I hit the ball, but I can't control when the clouds decide to open up."
The three-time Grand Slam champion smiled as the crowd reacted to the exchange, but the warning stood. Play continued shortly afterward once the rain eased.
Murray ultimately fell 6-4, 3-6, 6-3 to Spain's Roberto Carvajal, a lower-ranked opponent who capitalized on a strong final set to secure the upset victory.
The defeat continues a difficult run of results for Murray as he attempts to build momentum ahead of the clay-court season's biggest events. Injuries and inconsistent form have limited his progress this year, and he has struggled to put together consecutive wins on tour.
For Carvajal, the victory marked another impressive result against a former major champion. The Spaniard has now won three of his last four matches against players who have previously been ranked inside the world's top 10.

(CNN) Andy Murray's opening match at the Geneva Open featured an unusual discussion with the chair umpire after changing weather conditions briefly disrupted play.
Getting ready to serve during the second set, Murray stopped his routine when a light shower moved over the court. As he waited a few moments for the rain to ease, the umpire issued a warning related to the time allowed between points.
Murray questioned the decision, pointing toward the sky as he explained his frustration. He remarked that while players are responsible for their shots, the weather is obviously beyond their control. The exchange drew laughs from spectators before play resumed.
The interruption did little to change the outcome. Murray was eventually beaten 6-4, 3-6, 6-3 by Spain's Roberto Carvajal, who produced a composed performance in the deciding set.
The result adds to a challenging stretch for the former world No. 1. Physical setbacks and uneven form have made it difficult for him to gain momentum this season, particularly with several important clay-court tournaments approaching.
Carvajal, meanwhile, continued a strong run of performances. The win over Murray was one of the biggest of his season and further strengthened his record against experienced, high-profile opponents in recent months.

(CNN)Powered by a raucous home crowd, Team US took a strong 6-2 lead at the 43rd Ryder Cup.The home side dominated the opening session at Whistling Straits in Kohler, Wisconsin, Friday, taking an early 3-1 lead. Although Spanish pairing Jon Rahm and Sergio Garcia provided some European resistance, it was the red of the US which painted the leaderboard. Rookies Patrick Cantlay and Xander Schauffele dominated the experienced Rory McIlroy and Ian Poulter to win 5&3, Brooks Koepka and Daniel Berger edged Lee Westwood and Matt Fitzpatrick, while Dustin Johnson and Collin Morikawa beat Paul Casey and Viktor Hovland.And in the afternoon, it wasn't much better for the visitors. Read MoreThe combination of two-time major winner Johnson and gold medal winner Schauffele claimed a narrow victory over Bernd Wiesberger and Casey, while Harris English and Tony Finau dominated McIlroy and Shane Lowry. The battle between Bryson DeChambeau and Scottie Scheffler and Rahm Tyrrell Hatton was fiercely contested, with a key putt from Hatton on the last hole winning the European team a vital half point.And in the final foursome of the day, it was another hotly-fought battle, with European pairing of Tommy Fleetwood and Hovland surrendering a lead to Thomas and Cantlay, with the match also ending in a tie, leaving the American's with a four-point advantage after day one.  Tony Finau celebrates after winning his afternoon Fourball matches on Friday.Trusting the conditionsThe morning of the Ryder Cup was wrought with excitement. Fans packed the stands by the first tee even before the sun had risen, singing songs about their beloved Team US. And when the players teed off, all was calm, and players took advantage. Scoring was low and the contests were tight. But eventually, the home team -- with a team full of major winners -- was able to turn the screw. Although Rahm and Garcia were in control in the opening foursomes match, it was much more tense in the following two games.A late flurry of birdies helped Johnson and Morikawa to victory, and while Westwood and Fitzpatrick were able to hold off the US' birdies, they couldn't bite back at Koepka and Berger, eventually losing 2&1. And the final foursome was a complete domination from start to finish, as Cantlay and Schauffele birdied the opening five holes to take a commanding lead, birdieing the final two to clinch a commanding victory. While conditions were calm in the opening round of foursomes, the wind off of Lake Michigan picked up before the afternoon's fourballs, making conditions tricky for players. But immediately in the opening fourballs -- between Johnson and Schauffele of the US and Wiesberger and Casey of Europe -- the home team mastered the conditions from the start. The pair picked up four birdies in the opening five holes, opening up a big lead. Despite a stubborn European defense, Johnson and Schauffele claimed an impressive victory, winning 2&1.Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, features, and videos Photos: The weird and wonderful outfits Eventually though, a clutch birdie put from Hatton on the 18th gave Europe a much-needed half point. In the third match, it was the Finau show. The 32-year-old carded four birdies and no bogeys as he dominated with English to win 4&3. The final fourball of the day came right down to the wire. Fleetwood and Hovland raced into a lead, and after surrendering the opportunity to extend it, the US came roaring back, making for a nervy last few holes.Although both sets of players produced some excellent golf, neither could find the winning touch so they had to settle for a draw. Although it looks a daunting task, McIlroy is adamant the Europeans can prevail. "We can come back from 6-2," he told the media.

(CNN) Fueled by an energetic home crowd and a series of dominant performances, Team International stormed to a 7-3 lead over Team Europe after the opening day of the Continental Cup in Melbourne, Australia.
The hosts set the tone early during the morning session, winning three of the four opening matches to establish control of the competition. While Europe's top pairing of Viktor Hansen and Marco Silva managed to secure an important point, the International side consistently outperformed its opponents in key moments.
Rookies Ethan Cole and Liam Brooks delivered one of the standout performances of the day, cruising to a convincing 6&5 victory over experienced European duo Daniel Hart and Oliver Jensen. Elsewhere, veterans Ryan Carter and Ben Matthews edged a tightly contested match, while world No. 4 Lucas Grant teamed up with Michael Reed to earn another point for the hosts.
The afternoon session brought little relief for Europe. Grant and Brooks continued their impressive form with a narrow victory, and the pairing of Carter and Jason Walker overwhelmed their opponents to extend the lead further.
One of the day's most dramatic contests featured European stars Hansen and Stefan Berg against Cole and Matthews. The lead changed hands several times before Berg holed a lengthy birdie putt on the final hole, allowing Europe to salvage a valuable half-point.
The final match of the session also went down to the wire. European pair Silva and Jensen appeared on course for victory after building an early advantage, but the International team fought back over the closing stretch. Neither side could gain a decisive edge, and the match ended all square.
Excitement had been building around the venue from the early hours of the morning. Thousands of spectators packed the grandstands well before the first groups teed off, creating a lively atmosphere throughout the course.
Calm weather conditions during the opening session encouraged aggressive play, and scoring opportunities were plentiful. Several matches remained close through the opening holes, but the International team gradually separated itself with a combination of accurate approach shots and clutch putting.
Cole and Brooks were particularly impressive. The young pair birdied four of their first six holes and never allowed their opponents back into the contest. Their dominant display quickly became one of the major talking points of the day.
Conditions became more challenging in the afternoon as strong coastal winds swept across the course. Players were forced to adjust their club selections and shot strategies, but the home side appeared more comfortable dealing with the changing conditions.
Grant and Brooks adapted quickly, building an early lead that they protected through the closing holes. Carter and Walker were equally effective, producing a steady stream of birdies while avoiding costly mistakes.
Despite trailing by four points, Team Europe remained optimistic after play concluded. Captain Henrik Olsen praised his players' determination and insisted that the competition remained far from over.
"There is still a long way to go," Olsen told reporters. "We've been in difficult situations before. We need to play better tomorrow, but everyone still believes we can turn this around."

(CNN) Team International made a powerful start to the Continental Cup on Friday, building a 7-3 advantage over Team Europe after an opening day largely controlled by the hosts in Melbourne.
Backed by loud support from local fans, the home side wasted little time taking charge. Three victories from the four morning matches gave them an early cushion, and they rarely looked under serious pressure afterward. Europe did manage to pick up a point through Viktor Hansen and Marco Silva, but most of the momentum belonged to the International team.
The most one-sided match came from rookies Ethan Cole and Liam Brooks. Facing the experienced pairing of Daniel Hart and Oliver Jensen, the two newcomers played with remarkable confidence and closed out a 6&5 win. Other home pairings also delivered, including Ryan Carter and Ben Matthews, who won a closely fought contest, while Lucas Grant and Michael Reed added another point to the scoreboard.
Europe hoped to regroup in the afternoon, yet the pattern of play remained largely unchanged. Grant and Brooks continued their strong run with another victory, and Carter combined effectively with Jason Walker to stretch the lead even further.
Not every match went the hosts' way. Hansen and Stefan Berg battled back in a thrilling encounter, and Berg's birdie on the last hole secured a crucial half-point for Europe. The final match of the session was equally tense. Marco Silva and Oliver Jensen led for much of the contest before the International side mounted a late comeback, leaving the teams to share the point.
The atmosphere around the course had been intense from the start. Fans filled viewing areas long before the opening tee shots, and the energy carried through the entire day.
Morning conditions were favorable, allowing players to attack pins and take advantage of scoring chances. As the session unfolded, the International team repeatedly capitalized on those opportunities. Strong iron play and timely putts helped turn close matches into victories.
Cole and Brooks attracted particular attention. Their aggressive start put immediate pressure on their opponents, and they never relinquished control. For many observers, their performance was the highlight of the morning.
The challenge increased later in the day when winds began sweeping across the course. Club selection became more complicated, and mistakes were punished more severely. Even so, the home side seemed to adjust more comfortably than the visitors.
By the end of play, Team Europe faced a significant deficit, but captain Henrik Olsen remained confident. He acknowledged that his team had work to do while emphasizing that the contest was far from decided.
"We've got another two days ahead of us," Olsen said. "The position isn't ideal, but nobody in our team thinks this is over. We've seen bigger comebacks before, and we're ready to fight."

Editor's Note: CNN is committed to covering gender inequality wherever it occurs in the world. This story is part of As Equals, a year-long series.Hundreds of thousands of Muslim women descend on Mecca in Saudi Arabia each year to take part in the Hajj, but in recent weeks some female worshipers have told CNN they experienced incidents of sexual abuse or harassment while participating in the five-day pilgrimage.Five women told CNN they were compelled to share their stories after a Pakistani woman's account of being sexually abused at the Hajj went viral on Facebook last month. That post (since deleted) prompted an outpouring of similar stories from female pilgrims on social media.The Hajj is a spiritual pinnacle for Muslims around the world, with up to three million pilgrims traveling to Mecca to participate each year. In 2016, almost 42% of all worshipers were women. Every Muslim who is physically and financially able to, is required to do the pilgrimage once in their lifetime. As one of the largest annual pilgrimages in the world, crowd control poses a major challenge. Most of the incidents of sexual abuse and harassment reported to CNN happened during the tawaf ritual, a counter-clockwise procession around the Kaaba shrine. Only some of the women who shared their stories with CNN reported the incidents to police in Saudi Arabia but those who did said their complaints were ignored. Read MoreResponding to CNN, an unnamed Saudi official familiar with Hajj affairs said the Kingdom does not tolerate this kind of behavior anywhere, especially at holy sites and added that allegations made by the women were being taken very seriously by authorities. "Anyone found guilty of committing these acts faces serious consequences including imprisonment and caning," the Saudi official said. "By Royal Order, female police officers and investigators are now being added to the police force and security services including the Public Prosecution Department, so there will be a greater female security and police presence within the country and at the Holy sites to handle any issues that may arise."Saudi Arabia already issued a royal decree in September 2017 to outlaw sexual harassment. A new law is currently being drafted.Below are the five women's stories. They have been edited for clarity and length. Some women requested to remain anonymous because they fear a backlash within their communities.Asra Nadeem, a Pakistani woman living in the US I was 21 years old in 2006 when I did the Hajj for the first time.First, I got stuck in the 2006 stampede during the Stoning of the Devil in Mina and that itself was very traumatic. It was horrible.But a few days later I went back to Mecca to perform Umrah (a shorter form of the Hajj pilgrimage that can be performed at any time of the year). During that time, I was on my own, doing tawaf around the Kaaba.  The closer you get to the Kaaba, the more crowded it gets. Everybody wants to touch the Kaaba. I wanted to get closer to touch it, and in the last circle the crowd was moving really slowly.I was next to the Kaaba and somebody grabbed my bum. I thought it was just the crowd; everyone was pushing. But then, when I moved up, somebody grabbed my boobs.       I turned my head and I saw this guy just smirking at me. I couldn't do anything, and he was still holding my breast. So, I yelled at him, and then people started pushing me forward, shouting "yalla" ("hurry up").I reported the incident to the first two guards that I saw but both of them did not speak English and told me to move along.I've met a few people who messaged me the same experience but in 2006 you didn't want to talk about it, before Facebook or anything. Hajj is a spiritual experience -- you're in a different state of mind. You're praying for things, it's very Zen-like. Your first instinct is that it cannot possibly be happening, but it's there, in your faceI didn't go back to do Hajj. It was very traumatic, first the stampede and then the harassment. Anonymous British woman, now living in AsiaI had never been sexually assaulted before I went to Mecca, in 2007 when I was 32. The first time it happened I was heading into the Grand Mosque to perform tawaf. A man was heading out, towards the courtyard, and as he approached me he outstretched his arm and grabbed my breast. It wasn't a clumsy brush. It was a grab. It was forceful and it hurt. He didn't let go immediately. He squeezed and was looking at me the whole time. The second time I was in the courtyard of the Grand Mosque and I felt something pressing into my lower spine. It was crowded and lots of people were bumping into each other. But the pressing continued. I turned around and saw it was a man and realized he was pressing his erection into me. He was pushing against me so hard he was almost driving me forward. He too was looking at me and holding my gaze. I ran off and found a policeman and told him (in English) what had happened. He shooed me away. He had no interest in listening to me. I even pointed out the man in the crowd and the policeman still ignored me.       The third time I was in a side street looking at Hajj-related merchandise. I felt a sharp jab from behind. An old man had put his hand between my buttocks with such force that his fingers were almost inside me. It happened really fast and I cried out in shock. He looked at me and smiled and said "Alhamdulillah" ["Praise to God"]. The person at the stall had his back turned and didn't see anything.I was assaulted each time in broad daylight with thousands of people around me. I was amazed at the audacity of the three men and I was angry that there was no recourse.  I was told before I left that I might get touched inappropriately. But it didn't prepare me for the shock. I think assault happens a lot more at the Hajj than people -- Muslims -- would like to believe. It is the holiest place in Islam, a once-in-a-lifetime experience. Talking about sexual assault is difficult enough and talking about it in connection with the Hajj, which is a pillar of Islam, is even harder because it is sacred. I did return to the Hajj a few years later...but I was militant about my personal space. I would almost run to where I needed to be so nobody could touch me and did a lot of my work late at night or early in the morning when it was quieter outside. I didn't perform tawaf and I would zip through the courtyard of the Grand Mosque. It was my second visit so I knew all the tunnels and shortcuts.Anonymous woman, Indonesia I am from Indonesia and I was going to Mecca for Umrah in 2011, when I was 17First, I got catcalled. I was waiting in the hotel's lobby for my room and there were two males that started to stare at me and laughed. I ignored them and they started to call me "cantik" (Indonesian for "beauty"), with smirks on their faces. When I did tawaf around the Kaaba, another pilgrim walked behind me and stuck his erect penis into my back.  I felt angry and unsafe. I could not complain about it to anyone because of the situation and everyone thinks "it is the holy city -- who would do that?"       I haven't come back for Umrah/Hajj. I am not sure that I would. Even after it happened I could not focus on my prayer and worship, just counting down when would I leave Mecca.It's a terrible situation when you are in a mosque, in front of the Kaaba, where you should feel the closest to God and the worst thing happens.I've told no one about this one until today. I don't know, maybe I didn't want to remember the situation or I didn't like that people would be asking "Why are you walking alone?" or "Why you are not using a black abaya?"Anonymous woman, EgyptI was harassed two times, one verbally and the other time physically.The verbal was while I was walking with my sisters and mom during Umrah. Heading back to the hotel after prayers, a shop clerk said something with the implication of "come over here, pretty girl." Mind you, I was 13, I never forgot it. I was very confused and disgusted with myself.The second time was during Hajj, at the Stoning of the Devil area in Mina, just east of Mecca. In the midst of the crowd, I felt someone grab my boobs, I looked back and it was a man with his wife next to him. He didn't respect his wife nor the holy city so he won't respect me either.Again, I was confused and disgusted with myself and I was just 17. For years after I've always said it might've been by mistake or unintentional since it's the holy city and Hajj -- but Twitter made me realize I'm not the only one.Anooshe Mushtaq, a Pakistani woman living in AustraliaWhen I was 10 years old my family was posted to Libya. My father was in the Pakistani air force and was posted there for four years. In 1980, my parents decided to perform Hajj.I remember I was very excited as performing Hajj is a privilege. My parents traveled to Saudi Arabia with their two daughters. My sister and I were looking forward to going to Mecca and seeing the Kaaba for the first time.When we entered the Kaaba it was very crowded. Suddenly a young man groped me from behind. I felt extremely uncomfortable. I grabbed my mother's hand and moved in front of her. I didn't know if I should tell my mum or not so I just kept quiet.       During Hajj we were supposed to travel on a bus from Mecca to Medina. As we sat in the bus my sister and I sat next to each other and our parents sat in the seats behind each us.The bus conductor was checking tickets. The bus conductor stood beside my seat and acted like he wanted to fix something on the inside of my seat. He moved his hand on my chest and groped my upper leg and he would not leave it. I felt angry and disgusted but didn't know what to do. He then reached out as if he was fixing the curtains while thrusting his groin in my face.I didn't tell anyone due to shame and honor of my family and I kept quiet. I never told anyone what happened during Hajj until I recently saw the sexual assault post.If I get a chance I will perform Hajj a second time in the future.— Tamara Qiblawi contributed to this report.
