London (CNN) When photographs emerged showing Prince George accompanying his parents at a series of official engagements over the summer, royal observers quickly began debating what role the young prince might play in the future of the monarchy.
The discussion reflects a broader fascination with the royal line of succession and how the institution prepares younger generations for public life. As the eldest child of Prince William and Catherine, Princess of Wales, George remains second in line to the throne behind his father.
While public appearances by senior royals are often carefully planned, constitutional experts point out that succession itself is governed by established law. Individual family members do not choose their place in the order, nor can it be altered by personal preference.
A file photograph shows Prince William and Prince George attending a public event together.
The structure has remained largely unchanged for generations, although Parliament has occasionally updated the rules. In 2013, changes ended the system under which younger sons could take precedence over older daughters in the line of succession.
As a result, Princess Charlotte retained her position ahead of her younger brother, Prince Louis, marking a significant modernization of succession rules.
For many royal watchers, however, legal details are less interesting than questions about how younger royals will adapt to increasing public attention.
The monarchy has faced considerable scrutiny in recent years, with debates surrounding its future, relevance, and relationship with the public becoming more frequent.
Supporters argue that continuity remains one of the institution's greatest strengths. Critics maintain that hereditary systems are difficult to reconcile with modern democratic values.
Regardless of those debates, George's future role remains largely defined by circumstances beyond his control. His position within the royal hierarchy was determined at birth and would only change through events affecting those ahead of him.
WHAT ELSE IS HAPPENING?
King attends charity awards ceremony
The King traveled to Manchester this week to present awards recognizing community volunteers from across the United Kingdom. During the ceremony, he praised individuals who had contributed to local initiatives supporting education, health services, and environmental projects.
Speaking to attendees, he emphasized the importance of civic engagement and thanked volunteers for their dedication to helping others.
Prince and Princess of Wales visit youth center
The Prince and Princess of Wales spent part of the week visiting a youth center in Birmingham focused on mentoring and career development programs.
The visit included discussions with students, teachers, and local organizers. Kensington Palace said the engagement formed part of the couple's ongoing interest in education and early intervention initiatives.
Royal documentary attracts attention
A new documentary examining the history of royal residences premiered this week on British television.
The program explores the development of several royal estates and includes interviews with historians, architects, and former palace staff. Early reviews have been mixed, though audience interest appears strong.
PHOTO OF THE WEEK
King Charles speaks with conservation volunteers during a visit to a coastal restoration project.
The King joined environmental groups working to restore natural habitats along part of Britain's coastline. Organizers said the project aims to improve biodiversity while protecting vulnerable ecosystems from erosion and climate-related challenges.
During the visit, Charles spoke with volunteers and researchers involved in the effort and reiterated his long-standing interest in environmental issues.
FROM THE ROYAL VAULT
This week marks the anniversary of the opening of Buckingham Palace to summer visitors in the 1990s.
The decision represented a significant change in how the royal family interacted with the public, allowing visitors access to parts of the palace previously seen only through official photographs and television broadcasts.
Over the years, the annual opening has become one of London's most popular attractions, offering visitors a glimpse into one of the country's most recognizable institutions.

A version of this story appeared in CNN's Royal News newsletter, which follows developments involving the British royal family and public life at the palace.
London (CNN) A handful of recent appearances by Prince George alongside his parents have once again sparked curiosity about the future of the monarchy.
Photos from official events circulated widely online, prompting familiar questions from royal enthusiasts about how the young prince is being prepared for a life that will almost certainly involve significant public responsibility.
George, the eldest child of the Prince and Princess of Wales, is currently second in line to the British throne. Yet despite the attention surrounding him, his constitutional position is remarkably straightforward. Like every other member of the royal succession, his place was determined automatically at birth.
Observers often speculate about future kings and queens, but succession is governed by law rather than personal choice. Neither popularity nor preference determines who comes next.
One notable reform arrived in 2013, when rules were updated so that birth order—not gender—would decide succession among royal siblings. That change ensured Princess Charlotte would remain ahead of Prince Louis despite being female, a move widely viewed as a modernization of centuries-old tradition.
Still, legal arrangements only explain part of the public fascination. Many people are equally interested in how younger royals navigate life under constant observation.
In recent years, the monarchy has found itself at the center of debates about tradition, accountability, and its place in contemporary Britain. Supporters see stability and continuity; critics question whether hereditary institutions still fit modern democratic societies.
For now, those arguments remain largely separate from George himself. His future role is tied less to public discussion than to constitutional reality.
WHAT ELSE IS GOING ON?
The King honors community volunteers
King Charles traveled to Manchester this week to recognize volunteers whose work has supported local communities across the country.
Addressing guests at the ceremony, he praised their commitment and highlighted the importance of civic participation in strengthening communities.
Waleses focus on youth programs
The Prince and Princess of Wales visited a youth center in Birmingham where they met students and staff involved in mentoring initiatives.
The engagement centered on education, skills development, and support programs designed to help young people transition into employment.
New royal documentary draws viewers
A television documentary exploring the history of royal residences premiered this week.
Featuring historians and heritage experts, the program examines how several well-known royal properties evolved over time. Viewer interest has been strong since its release.
PHOTO OF THE WEEK
During a visit to a coastal conservation project, King Charles met volunteers working to restore natural habitats and protect vulnerable stretches of shoreline.
Environmental causes have long been among the King's priorities, and he used the visit to highlight the importance of long-term conservation efforts.
FROM THE ROYAL VAULT
This week also marks an anniversary connected to Buckingham Palace's summer opening to the public.
What began as a relatively novel initiative decades ago has since become a fixture of the tourist calendar. Each year, thousands of visitors pass through rooms that were once accessible only to invited guests and officials.
The opening offered a rare look inside one of Britain's most recognizable buildings—and helped reshape the relationship between the monarchy and the public in subtle but lasting ways.

Glasgow, Scotland (CNN)Boris Johnson said he had been humbled to hear the stories of countries on the front lines of the climate crisis and hailed some of the early agreements announced so far at the COP26 summit in an exclusive interview Tuesday with CNN's Christiane Amanpour. Speaking to CNN on the sidelines of talks in Glasgow, Scotland, the British Prime Minister struck a somber tone on the state of the climate emergency, saying "I think you've got to be doom and gloom until we fix this thing" and insisting the world was "inching forward" on solving the crisis. "The threat is huge, I think it's been very humbling to listen to some of the testimonies from countries like Bangladesh, the Maldives, the Seychelles in the front line," Johnson told CNN. "Are we starting to inch forward with COP? Arguably we are, and in some important ways you're seeing some good commitments on trees and forests, some contributions accelerating the move away from coal."He also said his government was committing to reducing its reliance on coal, despite the prospect of a controversial new mine opening in northwest England."I don't want more coal, and our government doesn't want more coal. We'll do what we're legally able to do," Johnson said.Read MoreJohnson also struggled to explain his decision not to wear a mask at all times while sitting next to 95-year-old environmentalist David Attenborough during the summit on Monday. The Prime Minister sparked criticism after he was pictured in some photographs without a mask next to the veteran broadcaster and climate campaigner, though Johnson was seen wearing a mask in other photos of the pair.Asked about the controversy, Johnson stumbled before saying: "I've been wearing masks in confined spaces with people I don't normally talk to ... it's up to people to take a judgment whether they're at a reasonable distance from someone ... that's the approach we take." . Johnson and Attenborough pictured at the COP26 summit on Monday. Johnson also praised Attenborough's address to COP26 delegates on Monday as "spellbinding" and drove home the magnitude of the crisis. "I thought David Attenborough's presentation yesterday morning was absolutely spellbinding because he set out for everybody to understand so clearly the link between the rising carbon and the percentage in the world's atmosphere and the rise in temperature. You see that link over thousands of years and suddenly you see this spike in carbon and the beginnings of the rise in temperature and you know what is going to come."Johnson was also asked about his 2013 claim that the previous British government had "put in a load of wind farms that failed to pull the skin off a rice pudding." He responded by saying that "everybody has the right" to change their mind and hailed the developments in technology were the driving force for his change of heart."If you look at the sophistication of the cells ... the size of the turbines ... propeller blades twice the size of the London Eye ... imagine that. These are enormous creations. They're actually rather beautiful."Asked about the seesawing nature of US policy on climate and whether the world could trust any American administration on the issue, Johnson struck an optimistic tone. "What's changed now is the voters in our countries want change and want us to fix this thing. I believe this goes for all great Western democracies. But I think it also goes for populations around the world," Johnson said. He cited the example of Covid-19, saying that when populations "see something that they think is a natural disaster," they change their behaviors."People can see climate change is happening. They can see wildfires and flooding. They can see that something out of the normal weather events is taking place," Johnson said. "And it's moving up their agenda." 5 takeaways from COP26 Day 2: Leaders tackle methane, high-risk countries speak up, China weighs in"I believe Joe [Biden] understands that, and I think people are enthusiastic about his agenda to fix it. But i think any future president of the United States is going to be responding to strong, strong, democratic pressure to join and support the rest of the world in fixing climate change." Johnson also sought to defend his government's widely-criticized handling of the Covid-19 pandemic and said he did not plan to bring in further measures, including mandating the wearing of masks, despite a recent rise in cases and deaths in the UK.The Prime Minister said that while he was "watching the data all the time" and that we "have to remain humble in the face of the nature of what the disease can do," he believed that at the moment "we don't see any reasons to deviate the plan that we're on." He went on to hail the UK's vaccine rollout, saying that the UK was "starting to deal with Covid as part of our lives."This story has been updated to include more from the full interview and additional context.

Paris, France (CNN) — French President Emmanuel Macron said the world was at a "decisive turning point" on climate action and defended the ambitions of the Paris Agreement framework in an exclusive interview Wednesday with CNN's Becky Anderson, speaking on the margins of a major international environmental conference in the French capital.
Macron acknowledged the frustration felt by developing nations bearing the brunt of climate change while having contributed least to it. "We have to be honest — the countries suffering most are not the ones responsible for the bulk of emissions. That asymmetry is a moral problem, and it has to be at the center of everything we negotiate," he told CNN.
The French president hailed recent pledges on methane reduction and deforestation as meaningful steps, while cautioning that commitments on paper needed to translate into concrete policy. "Announcements are not action. We have made that mistake before. What matters now is the legislation, the financing, and the accountability mechanisms that follow."
Macron was also pressed on France's continued reliance on nuclear energy and whether it was compatible with a green transition. He pushed back firmly: "Nuclear gives us low-carbon baseload power. I will not apologize for that. The climate does not care about ideology — it cares about emissions." He added that France was simultaneously accelerating investment in offshore wind and solar infrastructure.
On the question of China's absence from key summit sessions, Macron urged patience while insisting that bilateral dialogue remained active. "We speak regularly with Beijing. The channels are open. But I will not pretend there is no gap between what the science demands and what some major emitters are currently willing to commit to."
Asked whether younger generations had reason for hope, Macron struck a carefully balanced tone. "Despair is not a strategy. Neither is blind optimism. What young people are right to demand is honesty — about how severe this is, and about whether those in power are genuinely acting. My job is to make sure the answer to that second question is yes."
He also addressed domestic criticism that his government's climate policies disproportionately burden working-class households, referencing the Yellow Vest protests that erupted in 2018 partly in response to a fuel tax rise. "The transition must be fair or it will not happen. We learned that lesson. Social justice and climate justice are not in opposition — they have to be built together."

Paris, France (CNN) — French President Emmanuel Macron called the current moment a "decisive turning point" for climate action and mounted a defense of the Paris Agreement framework in an exclusive interview Wednesday with CNN's Becky Anderson, speaking on the sidelines of a major environmental conference in the French capital.
Macron didn't shy away from the frustration simmering among developing nations — those absorbing the worst climate impacts while having done the least to cause them. "We have to be honest," he said. "The countries suffering most are not the ones responsible for the bulk of emissions. That asymmetry is a moral problem, and it has to sit at the center of everything we negotiate."
He welcomed recent pledges on methane and deforestation, but was quick to add a caveat. Commitments on paper, he said, are only as good as the legislation and financing that follow. "Announcements are not action. We have made that mistake before."
France's nuclear energy policy drew pointed questions, and Macron didn't flinch. "Nuclear gives us low-carbon baseload power. I will not apologize for that. The climate doesn't care about ideology — it cares about emissions." He noted that France was pressing ahead with offshore wind and solar investment at the same time, framing the two as complementary rather than contradictory.
China's absence from key summit sessions hung over parts of the discussion. Macron urged patience, insisting bilateral conversations were ongoing, but acknowledged the gap plainly. "I will not pretend there is no difference between what the science demands and what some major emitters are currently willing to commit to."
On hope — the question reporters keep putting to leaders — Macron resisted both poles. "Despair is not a strategy. Neither is blind optimism." Young people, he said, are right to demand honesty, about the scale of the crisis and about whether governments are genuinely responding to it.
Domestically, the transition has not been painless. The 2018 Yellow Vest protests, ignited in part by a fuel tax hike, cast a long shadow. Macron acknowledged the tension directly. A green transition that falls hardest on working people, he argued, is one that will eventually collapse under its own political weight. "Social justice and climate justice are not in opposition. They have to be built together."

(CNN)Former Yorkshire County Cricket Club academy player Irfan Amjad has said in a new interview with the BBC that he was racially abused by a member of staff when he was 16 years old.Speaking to the BBC, Amjad alleges that his batting style was criticized by a member of staff, referencing his Pakistani heritage."I was stunned, didn't know what to do and was shocked. I had never been directly racially insulted like that to my face. I didn't know what to do," he said.In a statement provided to CNN on Tuesday, Yorkshire CCC said: "It is essential that those who have experienced racism, discrimination and abuse are able to come forward to share their experiences. We were unaware of this allegation until now but will investigate appropriately."Cricketer Gary Ballance admits using racial slur towards former Yorkshire teammate Azeem RafiqCNN has reached out to Amjad for comment. He told the BBC he never reported the incident.Read More"It crosses your mind [reporting it] but I was a 16-year-old boy, and I didn't know what to do. I kept it to myself," he said."Even before the incident I felt distant, it happened and then I felt isolated after that. Going forward, I didn't feel I could speak up or give my views on the game in team meetings," he said.On Friday, chairman Roger Hutton resigned amid the club's handling of racism and bullying towards former Yorkshire cricketer Azeem Rafiq.Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, videos and featuresThe now former chairman of Yorkshire CCC has been summoned to the UK Parliament to answer questions over the club's handling of allegations of "institutional" and "endemic" racism on November 16.Rafiq's accusations of racism came to light in 2020, and the club subsequently apologized to Rafiq in August and said that he was the "victim of inappropriate behaviour."

(CNN) Former Leicester City Football Club youth academy player Marcus Osei has alleged in a new interview with Sky Sports that he was subjected to racial abuse by a member of the coaching staff when he was 17 years old.
Speaking to Sky Sports, Osei claims that a senior coach made derogatory comments referencing his Ghanaian heritage during a training session, following criticism of his defensive positioning.
"I was completely taken aback. No one had ever spoken to me like that in a professional environment. I didn't know how to react — I just went quiet," he said.
In a statement provided to CNN on Wednesday, Leicester City FC said: "We take all allegations of racism and discrimination extremely seriously. We were not previously aware of this specific allegation, and we are committed to conducting a thorough and transparent investigation."
CNN has reached out to Osei for further comment. He told Sky Sports he chose not to report the incident at the time.
"You think about saying something, but I was only 17 and still trying to prove myself. I didn't want to rock the boat or jeopardize my place at the club," he said.
"It made me feel like an outsider from that point on. I started doubting myself in team environments, wondering whether I truly belonged there," he added.
The allegations come amid growing scrutiny of racism within English football's academy system. Earlier this month, the club's director of youth development stepped down following separate complaints from multiple former players regarding the culture within the academy.
A parliamentary inquiry into racism across professional football academies is expected to begin hearings next month, with several clubs anticipated to be called to give evidence. The broader issue gained renewed attention following high-profile cases involving players at multiple Premier League clubs, prompting the Football Association to announce a review of its safeguarding procedures for youth players in October.

Osei made the claims in an interview with Sky Sports, describing a training session in which a senior coach criticized his defensive positioning before making the comments.
"I was completely taken aback. No one had ever spoken to me like that in a professional setting. I just went quiet," he said.
Leicester City told CNN on Wednesday it had not previously been aware of the allegation. "We take all allegations of racism and discrimination extremely seriously and are committed to conducting a thorough and transparent investigation," the club said.
CNN has reached out to Osei for additional comment. He told Sky Sports he never reported the incident at the time.
"You think about saying something. But I was 17, still trying to prove myself — I didn't want to jeopardize my place at the club," he said. The experience, he added, followed him: "I started doubting whether I truly belonged there. In team meetings I just kept my head down."
The allegations surface as English football faces wider scrutiny over racism in its academy system. The club's director of youth development stepped down earlier this month amid separate complaints from former players about the culture inside the academy.
A parliamentary inquiry into racism across professional football academies is expected to open hearings next month. Following high-profile cases at several Premier League clubs, the Football Association announced in October a review of its safeguarding procedures for youth players.

(CNN)When Sky Brown took her last run in the women's park skateboarding final at the Tokyo Olympics, she knew she had to step up. Making mistakes in her first two runs, she went into her final attempt a few points behind Misugu Okamoto, needing to beat the 15-year-old Japanese skateboarder for third place. And that's exactly what 13-year-old Brown did: achieving a flawless final run complete with a kickflip indy to score 56.47 to take bronze -- making history as Great Britain's youngest-ever medal winner. "This is probably, honestly, one of the best memories ever," she told CNN Sport's Coy Wire.'One big family'Read MoreAs soon as Brown nailed her run, the Ariake Urban Arena erupted with the spirit of humility and sportsmanship, as she was immediately flanked by her peers in a moving display of solidarity. In fact, each of the competitors embraced each other every time they finished a run in the final. According to Brown, the unanimous support and encouragement exhibited at Tokyo is part and parcel of the sport. "Skateboarding is one big family," she says. "We push each other and we have fun together, and I think skateboarding is just a friendly sport."The British skateboarding prodigy shared the podium with Japan's gold medalist Sakura Yosozumi and silver medalist Kokona Hiraki, who she refers to as "two of my favorite people."Sky Brown hugs Sakura Yosozumi during the women's skateboarding park finals at the Tokyo Olympics. 'Do it because you love it'"I have lots of heroes. I look up to so many people," she says, counting Brazilian skateboarders Leticia Bufoni and Rayssa Leal -- the latter claiming silver in the women's street skateboarding event at Tokyo -- as two of her role models "right now."  Brown is certainly inspired by older skateboarders in the sport, but she also hopes to use her platform to inspire a younger generation of athletes to work hard and believe in their dreams. "Get out there, believe in yourself and try your best. But you've got to have fun, you've got to enjoy it," she says.For an athlete who hasn't had an easy journey to the Olympics, Brown radiates positivity. She took up the sport when she was three under her father's supervision, learning tricks on YouTube and eventually enrolling in Great Britain's skateboarding program. Brown started skateboarding at age three, teaching herself tricks by watching YouTube videos. However, in May 2020, she endured a life-threatening injury when she fell 15 feet while training, landing on hard ground and sustaining a fractured skull, broken left wrist and hand, and lacerations to her lungs and stomach. But she didn't give up on her Olympic dream, posting on Instagram, "I'm excited to come back even stronger and even tougher. My heart wants to go so hard right now. I'm just waiting for my body to catch up." Her motto for young girls chasing their ambitions? "Be brave, be strong, have fun and do it because you love it," she tells CNN Sport. "Don't let anybody stop you, get out there and prove people wrong." The futureFrom being unveiled as a Barbie Role Model to writing a book to winning the Summer X Games in 2021, Brown has packed a lot into the life of someone who's barely a teenager. Already looking to compete at the Paris 2024 Olympics -- where she told BBC Sport she's hoping to take part in skating and surfing events -- Brown is always one step ahead of the curve. While she insists that she's having "the best time ever" competing at global venues with her peers, she's also trying to savor every moment, so she can "take it slow and enjoy the journey.""I think it's important to not just do one thing. Do all different kinds of things because life's too short," she says.

(CNN) When Momiji Nishiya stepped onto the podium at the Tokyo Olympics, she became the youngest athlete to win gold in the newly debuted women's street skateboarding event — just 13 years old, competing in front of a home crowd that wasn't allowed to be there. The stands were empty due to Covid-19 restrictions, but the energy among the skaters themselves more than filled the silence.
Nishiya's winning performance combined technical precision with a composure that belied her age, landing tricks that drew admiration even from her rivals. Finishing just behind her were fellow Japanese skater Funa Nakayama and Brazil's Rayssa Leal, who at 13 matched Nishiya's age and became an instant fan favorite worldwide after footage of her skating in a fairy costume went viral years earlier.
'We're all friends out here'
What struck many viewers watching the street skateboarding final was not just the skill on display, but the warmth between competitors. After each run, skaters gathered to cheer, hug, and celebrate one another regardless of scores or national affiliations. For Leal, who took silver, the atmosphere reflected something fundamental about skateboarding culture. "We grow up skating together, watching each other's videos," she told reporters after the event. "When your friend lands something amazing, you're happy for them. That's just how it is."
The podium, occupied entirely by teenagers, represented a generational shift in the sport — and a glimpse at what Olympic skateboarding could look like for years to come. All three medallists had been skating since they were toddlers, drawn in by YouTube videos and local skate parks before finding their way onto the world stage.
Finding the love early
Leal's story, in particular, captured global attention. Growing up in Vitória, Brazil, she began skating at age seven after watching videos online and begging her father for a board. With limited access to formal training facilities, she spent years skating in the streets, honing her style organically. That grassroots foundation, she says, is still the core of who she is as a skater. "I never want to lose the feeling of just skating for fun," she said. "That's where everything started."
Her silver medal at Tokyo marked only the beginning. Sponsors, magazine covers, and speaking invitations followed rapidly, but Leal has remained focused on the thing that set her on this path in the first place: the sheer joy of being on a board. She has spoken openly about wanting to use her visibility to encourage girls in Brazil and beyond to take up the sport, especially in communities where access to sports infrastructure is limited.
What comes next
With Paris 2024 firmly on her radar, Leal has been expanding her competitive repertoire while also nurturing her public profile. She has collaborated with major brands and appeared in campaigns aimed at getting more young girls into skateboarding, echoing a broader push from World Skate and the IOC to grow the sport's global footprint.
Still a teenager with the bulk of her career ahead of her, Leal speaks about the future with an ease that suggests she is in no hurry to rush toward it. "I just want to keep skating, keep learning, keep having fun," she said. "If I do that, I think everything else follows."

(CNN) When Momiji Nishiya stepped onto the podium at the Tokyo Olympics, she was 13 years old and already a gold medalist. The women's street skateboarding event was making its Olympic debut, and Nishiya won it in front of empty stands — Covid-19 had banned spectators — though you wouldn't have known the arena was quiet from the energy among the skaters themselves.
Her performance was technically sharp and eerily calm for someone barely a teenager, drawing open admiration from her competitors. Funa Nakayama, also Japanese, took silver. Brazil's Rayssa Leal claimed bronze at the same age as Nishiya — 13 — completing a podium that looked less like a traditional Olympic lineup and more like a middle school reunion.
'We're all friends out here'
One of the more striking things about the street skateboarding final wasn't the difficulty of the tricks. It was how the skaters treated each other. After every run, they clustered together to cheer, hug, and celebrate — regardless of scores, regardless of country. For Leal, who had become a global sensation years earlier after a video of her skating in a fairy costume went viral, this was just skateboarding being skateboarding. "We grow up skating together, watching each other's videos," she told reporters. "When your friend lands something amazing, you're happy for them. That's just how it is."
Three teenagers on the podium. All three skating since they were toddlers. It felt less like a passing of the torch and more like confirmation that the torch had already changed hands.
Finding the love early
Leal's route to Tokyo was built on concrete — literally. Growing up in Vitória, Brazil, she started skating at seven after watching videos online and convincing her father to buy her a board. There were no elite training centers, no coaching pipelines. Just streets, a board, and a kid who couldn't get enough of it. That foundation, she says, still drives everything. "I never want to lose the feeling of just skating for fun. That's where everything started."
The silver medal changed her life in the ways you'd expect — sponsors, campaigns, the sudden weight of public attention. But she has kept returning to the same idea: visibility matters most if it opens doors for other girls, especially in places where sports infrastructure barely exists.
What comes next
Paris 2024 was already on Leal's mind before the Tokyo podium ceremony was over. She has been broadening her competitive range while also leaning into her platform, collaborating with brands and fronting campaigns aimed at drawing more girls into skating globally.
She's still a teenager. Most of her career remains ahead of her. And she seems, genuinely, unbothered by the magnitude of that. "I just want to keep skating, keep learning, keep having fun," she said. "If I do that, I think everything else follows."

(CNN)Liverpool's bid to retain the English Premier League title slipped further from its grip on Wednesday after suffering a shock defeat to relegation-threatened Brighton. A scrappy goal from Steven Alzate was enough for all three points and left Liverpool seven points behind league leader Manchester City, who have a game in hand. Liverpool and City face each other on Sunday in what many believed would be an early title decider. "As this moment stands, we're not in the title race," Liverpool full back Andy Robertson told reporters after the game. Debate over greatest goalscorer of all time continues as Cristiano Ronaldo nets 762nd goalJurgen Klopp's side, which seemed to have turned the corner after recent good performances, looked short of ideas against Brighton and managed just one shot on target the entire match. Read MoreIt has now failed to score in three consecutive league games at Anfield, an unimaginable record considering the team's attacking prowess over the last two seasons. Robertson credited Brighton as "the better team," but admitted his own side were "hugely disappointing." "We didn't really do anything of note," he said. "With a long record of going unbeaten, we've lost two. When you don't show up, you don't get results."The loss further highlighted the vulnerabilities of a side that many thought could go on to dominate English football for years to come. Klopp has had to deal with injuries to key players, including almost all of his senior central defender options, which has made navigating a congested fixture list difficult, especially during a global pandemic."The only explanation now is that we are a fatigued team, mentally more or less," he told reporters after the match. "We looked mentally not fresh. They deserved to win.""We wanted to play better and more convincing. We lost too many balls in promising situations. I know the boys can play pass A to B but today B did not seem reachable."We had a really tough week. We were not fresh tonight and that's when you have to find a way."READ: Man Utd puts nine past Southampton to equal Premier League recordLiverpool players looked dejected after Brighton's Steven Alzate scored on Wednesday. 'The more you win, the better you feel'Liverpool's loss will have added an extra gloss to Manchester City's night, which moved further clear at the top of the league after beating Burnley 2-0 on Wednesday. Goals by Gabriel Jesus and captain of the night Raheem Sterling secured a comfortable victory which stretched the team's winning run to 13 games in all competitions. Sterling credited the "togetherness" of the squad and the key defensive work that has given them a sixth consecutive clean sheet, equaling a club record. "The more you win, the better you feel, and that's what we've done today," he told reporters after the match."We knew exactly what they would bring to the game being physical, second balls and putting balls in the box, and I thought the boys at the back dealt with them really well."It's good to get another win and keep another clean sheet. We've been on a good run and we need to continue that now."While pleased with the performance, City manager Pep Guardiola looked ahead to the clash with Liverpool and noted the importance of recovery in a busy schedule. "Right now, we enjoy this game and tomorrow we start to think about the next game," he said. "It is the same points but against a contender. At the end, the aim is to be champion. Against teams lower in the table, people can say it's guaranteed but we know how difficult it is."We had to do our best and now we face a different team with a different type of play. Now come another tough, tough group of games. Today, we enjoy the game, have good food, and tomorrow, we start to think about Liverpool."

(CNN) Arsenal's hopes of ending their long wait for a Premier League title took a significant blow on Tuesday after a surprise home defeat to struggling Wolverhampton Wanderers. A second-half header from Matheus Cunha proved the difference, leaving Arsenal eight points behind league leaders Chelsea, who also have a game in hand.
The two sides are due to meet at Stamford Bridge on Saturday in what had been billed as a pivotal moment in the title race. "We have to be honest with ourselves — right now the gap is very big," Arsenal midfielder Thomas Partey told reporters after the final whistle.
Mikel Arteta's side had entered the match on the back of three consecutive victories and were expected to dominate a Wolves outfit fighting to avoid the drop. Instead they looked labored and disconnected in the final third, registering only two shots on target across ninety minutes.
The defeat extended a troubling run at the Emirates, where Arsenal have now won just once in their last five home league outings. Captain Martin Odegaard acknowledged the team had been well below their best. "We were not sharp enough, not aggressive enough. Wolves wanted it more than us tonight, and that showed," he said.
Arteta pointed to a relentless schedule and a growing injury list as contributing factors. Striker Bukayo Saka has been sidelined for three weeks, and the absence of his pace and creativity in the final third has been keenly felt. "We are managing a very thin squad across many competitions," Arteta said. "But that cannot be an excuse. We have to find solutions and the performance tonight was not acceptable."
Chelsea extend lead at the top
Chelsea's evening, meanwhile, could hardly have gone better. A commanding 3-1 victory over Aston Villa at Stamford Bridge extended their winning run to eleven matches in all competitions. Goals from Raheem Sterling, Cole Palmer, and a late Nicolas Jackson strike sealed the points and sent the home crowd into a jubilant mood.
Head coach Enzo Maresca praised the squad's collective intensity and noted a seventh clean sheet in eight league games had been narrowly denied only by a late consolation. "We are in a very good moment, but I never allow my players to feel comfortable," Maresca said after the match. "There are many games remaining and we know from experience how quickly things can change."
Sterling, who opened the scoring with a composed finish just before halftime, emphasized the squad's focus heading into the weekend. "Every game feels like a final at this stage of the season," he said. "We know Arsenal are coming and that it won't be easy, but we feel confident in the way we've been playing."

(CNN) Arsenal's long wait for a Premier League title got longer on Tuesday. A surprise home defeat to relegation-threatened Wolverhampton Wanderers — settled by a second-half header from Matheus Cunha — left them eight points adrift of Chelsea, who also hold a game in hand.
The two title rivals meet at Stamford Bridge on Saturday, a fixture that had been circled for weeks as a potential turning point. At this rate, it may already be too late. "We have to be honest — right now the gap is very big," midfielder Thomas Partey said after the final whistle.
Arteta's side had arrived at the Emirates in decent form, three straight wins behind them, and faced a Wolves team scrapping to stay in the division. None of that seemed to matter. Arsenal looked labored and disconnected for long stretches, managing just two shots on target all night.
It continued a worrying pattern at home, where they have won only once in their last five league matches. Captain Martin Odegaard didn't sugarcoat it. "We were not sharp, not aggressive enough. Wolves wanted it more than us and that showed," he said.
Arteta cited injuries and fixture congestion, pointing in particular to the three-week absence of Bukayo Saka, whose pace has been badly missed in the final third. "We are managing a very thin squad," he said. "But that cannot be an excuse. The performance tonight was not acceptable."
Chelsea pull further clear
While Arsenal stumbled, Chelsea had a near-perfect evening. A 3-1 win over Aston Villa at Stamford Bridge — goals from Raheem Sterling, Cole Palmer, and a late Nicolas Jackson — stretched their winning run to eleven games across all competitions and sent the home crowd home in good spirits.
Head coach Enzo Maresca refused to let his squad settle into self-congratulation. "We are in a good moment, but I never allow my players to feel comfortable," he said. A seventh clean sheet in eight league games had gone begging only thanks to a late consolation from Villa.
Sterling, who opened the scoring just before halftime with a composed finish, kept his focus firmly on Saturday. "Every game feels like a final at this stage," he said. "We know Arsenal are coming and it won't be easy — but we feel confident in how we've been playing."

(CNN)Steven Gerrard, manager of Scottish football team, Rangers, says he's "not surprised" Sparta Prague fans booed Glen Kamara during their 1-0 defeat in the Europa League on Thursday.The Finnish midfielder alleged he'd been racially abused by Slavia Prague defender Ondřej Kúdela in March and Kúdela received a 10-match ban from UEFA. He missed out on playing in Euro 2020 for the Czech Republic as a result.When Rangers returned to Prague for this match, the crowd started loudly booing when Black players, in particular Kamara, touched the ball.Sparta's Letna Stadium was supposed to be closed to fans for this match after UEFA sanctioned the club when fans directed racist chants at Monaco's Aurelien Tchouameni. However, UEFA allowed more than 10,000 children to attend Thursday's match with some accompanying adults.Read MoreThe 25-year-old was sent off in the 74th minute.Sparta Prague fans watch Kamara during the two teams' Europa League match.Gerrard said after the match that -- while he did not hear the booing during the match when Kamara touched the ball -- he was "not surprised.""If that's the case and that is the truth, it's obviously very disappointing. But I'm not surprised because we're playing behind closed doors, apparently, for a reason and it's not the first time there's been issues at this place," Gerrard told BT Sport. The former Liverpool and England international also said football's governing bodies need to do more to combat racism and discrimination."The powers that be don't do enough. Tonight, the punishment was meant to be a fine and behind closed doors where you don't play in front of any people," he said. "OK, there's kids at the game and children, I understand that, but it's near enough a full house so they're not getting punished playing a game behind closed doors. That's not the reason why we've lost the game but my opinion is if you're going to fine someone and punish them, go with the full punishment. Empty stadium and a fine and more." Gerrard confirmed at his Friday press conference that's he had watched back the footage and Rangers will write to UEFA to complain. "I'm fully aware now having watched the game back with audio on. I'm actually surprised that I wasn't aware of it during the game. I've spoken to Glen Kamara. That conversation will remain private. Glen's OK ... I'm sure he's disappointed just like myself," he explained. "Now, I think it's time for the authorities and club to step in and take that away from us so we can focus on the game on Sunday ... These things keep raising their head far too often and, unfortunately, the punishments are not enough. "I've been told that Rangers will take it up with UEFA. I think that's already happened. The wheels are already in motion. I'll certainly be pushing in my direction to make sure that's the case."Sparta Prague replied to the allegations of racism with a strong statement titled: "Stop abusing our children!""It is absolutely unbelievable that after a match we have to watch innocent children being attacked and face unfounded accusations of racism. Insulting children on the internet and in the media is unacceptable, desperate and ridiculous," it said. "We are seeing unprecedented xenophobic statements against the Czech Republic, its citizens and even its children on the social medias," it continued. "You are describing the behaviour of children incorrectly, arrogating to yourself the right to judge the expression of emotions of six-year-old children who have no idea what racism is. It's an impertinence."CNN has reached out to UEFA for comment.Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, features, and videosKamara receives a red card against Sparta Prague.Czech Foreign Minister Jakub Kulhanek tweeted that he'd speak to the British ambassador about the situation. "That's enough! Disgusting insults of Czech children in the media and online don't have a place in football, and definitely don't have a place in the good relationship between [the UK and the Czech Republic]. I will summon the British ambassador on Monday to discuss the situation."Aamer Anwar, Kamara's lawyer, criticized Sparta Prague, saying in a statement to Sky Sports News: "I had hoped when I heard of the stadium closure of Sparta Prague that both Glen and other Black players wouldn't have to put up with any abuse or racism and could just get on with playing football."But this evening should be an embarrassment for the Czech side that, despite their fans being banned, it still made little difference that the stadium was filled with 10,000 schoolchildren."A huge proportion of those children booed Glen's every touch of the ball along with every other Black player for Rangers. Tonight shows yet again that Prague has a serious problem with racism and as usual UEFA is nowhere to be seen."Glen and the Rangers players have shown total class, but no player should have to face this at their work and on the European stage."Aleks Klosok contributed to this report.

(CNN) Antonio Conte, manager of Italian club Inter Milan, says he is "deeply concerned" by the racist abuse directed at his striker Marcus Thuram during their Champions League group stage match against Dynamo Kyiv on Tuesday, which Inter won 2-0.
Thuram, the French international and son of World Cup winner Lilian Thuram, reported that he had been subjected to monkey chants from a section of the away support in the 58th minute, shortly after scoring Inter's opening goal. The 26-year-old visibly reacted to the abuse before teammates gathered around him, and the referee briefly paused the match.
UEFA's regulations require referees to follow a three-step protocol when racist incidents occur during matches — pausing play, suspending the game, and ultimately abandoning it if abuse continues. On Tuesday, play was paused once before resuming, but the match was not suspended or abandoned despite further reported incidents in the second half.
Conte said after the match that he had spoken with Thuram in the dressing room and described him as "shaken but strong."
"Marcus is a strong man and a strong player. But no one should have to experience that, not in football, not anywhere," Conte told reporters in his post-match press conference. "What happened tonight is not acceptable. UEFA has rules. I want to see those rules applied fully and without compromise."
The Italian manager also questioned whether football's governing bodies were doing enough to enforce their own standards. "We keep seeing these incidents. We keep hearing the same statements afterward. And then next week, another match, another incident," he said. "At some point you have to ask: are the punishments actually working? Because from where I'm standing, the answer is no."
Inter Milan confirmed in a club statement that they would be filing a formal complaint with UEFA and called for a full investigation. "Inter Milan condemns in the strongest possible terms any form of racist abuse. We stand fully behind Marcus and will pursue every avenue available to us to ensure that those responsible are identified and that UEFA acts decisively," the statement read.
Dynamo Kyiv released a brief response denying that their supporters were responsible for organized chanting and pledging to cooperate with any UEFA investigation.
UEFA confirmed it had opened disciplinary proceedings against Dynamo Kyiv and that the referee's report had formally documented the incidents. A spokesperson said the matter would be referred to UEFA's Control, Ethics and Disciplinary Body, with a hearing scheduled within two weeks.
Thuram, who has previously spoken publicly about racism in football and society, posted on social media after the match. Without naming the club or specific individuals, he wrote: "I play this game because I love it. Hate will never change that. But it has to stop."
His father, Lilian Thuram, who has long been an outspoken anti-racism campaigner, shared the post and added: "Proud of my son. This fight is not over."
CNN has reached out to both Dynamo Kyiv and UEFA for further comment.

"Marcus is a strong man and a strong player. But no one should have to experience that — not in football, not anywhere," he told reporters. "What happened tonight is not acceptable. UEFA has rules. I want to see those rules applied fully and without compromise."
He didn't stop there. Conte questioned, bluntly, whether governing bodies were serious about enforcement. "We keep seeing these incidents. We keep hearing the same statements afterward. And then next week, another match, another incident. At some point you have to ask: are the punishments actually working? Because from where I'm standing, the answer is no."
Inter confirmed they would file a formal complaint with UEFA and called for a full investigation. Dynamo Kyiv's response was brief — denying their supporters had engaged in organized chanting and pledging to cooperate with any inquiry.
UEFA said it had opened disciplinary proceedings and that the referee's report formally documented the incidents. A hearing before the Control, Ethics and Disciplinary Body was scheduled within two weeks.
Thuram addressed the night on social media without naming anyone directly. "I play this game because I love it," he wrote. "Hate will never change that. But it has to stop." His father, Lilian — long one of football's most prominent anti-racism voices — shared the post with a single line: "Proud of my son. This fight is not over."
CNN has reached out to both Dynamo Kyiv and UEFA for further comment.

A version of this story appeared in CNN's Meanwhile in China newsletter, a three-times-a-week update exploring what you need to know about the country's rise and how it impacts the world. Sign up here.Hong Kong (CNN)How to contain a runaway Covid outbreak, in a city committed to keeping cases at zero? That's the question now facing Hong Kong officials, as daily infections top 4,000, and previous fail-safe systems begin to buckle under the strain of their own uncompromising rules. For almost two years, Hong Kong had relied on a combination of stringent quarantines and sophisticated track-and-trace efforts to isolate positive cases, keeping the city comparatively virus-free -- even as the rest of the world began to loosen restrictions.  But those measures no longer appear sufficient in the face of the latest Omicron wave, which officials have described as a "tsunami." Hong Kong's insistence on sending all positive cases to hospital, irrespective of the severity, has led to at least one hospital being so overwhelmed it was forced to move patients on gurneys outside, lining them up in the parking lot.Read MoreMeanwhile, a raft of tightened restrictions and targeted lockdowns have led many experts and residents to question the sustainability of such an approach as the city enters the third year of the pandemic.Hong Kong Chief Executive Carrie Lam at a news conference on February 15.On Wednesday, Chinese President Xi Jinping took the unusual step of directly calling on Hong Kong officials to take "all necessary measures," according to comments published on the front pages of two Chinese state-run newspapers. Xi's intervention has raised fears that further restrictions similar to those seen in mainland China, including a possible citywide lockdown, could soon follow. So far, the local government has firmly ruled out such a move, suggesting it would be impractical to confine more than 7 million people to their homes. But Xi's comments make clear that Hong Kong has little choice but to adhere to China's hard-line zero-Covid strategy -- whatever the cost.The biggest outbreak yetThe newest wave began in January, and quickly spun out of control despite authorities' increasingly desperate efforts.In mid-January, the government killed more than 2,500 hamsters and other small animals after a single Covid case was linked to a pet store, sparking widespread public outrage.Days later, a housing cluster prompted the government to lock down several buildings home to thousands of people. Soon, residents began complaining of trash piling up in the hallways, and of pay cuts for those who weren't able to work during the lockdown.In hindsight, it was an early sign of the chaos to come, and of a government unequipped for a wave of this magnitude despite having had more than two years to prepare.To date, less than 70% of the city's population has been fully vaccinated against Covid, according to government data, and vaccination rates among the elderly remain comparatively low despite vaccines being available since February 2021. Health officials push a trolley of supplies outside a locked-down residential building in Hong Kong on January 23. With numbers rising, authorities reimposed a series of familiar restrictions: closing all schools; shuttering bars, gyms, salons and numerous public spaces; suspending restaurant dine-in past 6 p.m., capping public gatherings; doubling down on a citywide mask mandate; and prohibiting more than two households to mix in private. But these measures -- part of the government's zero-Covid playbook each time a new outbreak arises -- failed to stop the surge. On Wednesday, the city reported a record 4,285 new cases. Before this wave, Hong Kong had never seen more than 200 new cases in a day.Many of the government's rules were formed with the zero-Covid goal in mind, such as hospitalization for all those who test positive for Covid -- regardless of their condition -- mandatory tests for anybody who may have been exposed, and quarantine of close contacts.Hong Kong's 'zero Covid' strategy frustrates travel-starved residentsThese rules and processes may have worked when Hong Kong was only dealing with a few dozen cases at a time -- but the scale of the latest outbreak has stretched the system to breaking point. As of Monday evening, seven of Hong Kong's 17 public hospitals had either reached or exceeded 100% inpatient bed occupancy -- leading to the makeshift outdoor wards, which could pose a problem this weekend with rain and cold temperatures expected. Long lines stretch across the city, with people waiting hours to get tested.But rather than consider different approaches, the government -- under pressure from Beijing -- has dug its heels in, more determined than ever to contain the outbreak."At this moment, we still feel that (dynamic zero-Covid) is the best strategy for Hong Kong," the city's leader Carrie Lam said last week -- referring to a new label that aims to quickly suppress all outbreaks.Even before Xi's reported instructions, the Chinese central government stepped in earlier this week -- seemingly growing impatient with Hong Kong's inability to rein in the virus itself.  China will send health experts and medical supplies to Hong Kong, and help build new quarantine and isolation facilities, officials said -- bringing to mind the temporary hospitals that were constructed and operational within weeks in Wuhan, the original epicenter of the pandemic, at the start of it all.Lam has publicly welcomed the central government's support, admitting Tuesday that Hong Kong was struggling to cope with the exponential growth of cases. "The problem we are facing is, given the magnitude, pace and severity of this fifth wave, it has outgrown our capacity," she said. A widening gapThough Hong Kong has been aligned with Beijing in pursuing zero-Covid, it could have taken a different route -- one now playing out in a very similar city, 1,600 miles (about 2,600 kilometers) away.Last August, Singapore -- which for years has competed with Hong Kong for the title of Asia's top international business hub -- was the first Asian country to declare it was moving away from a zero-Covid policy to living with the virus instead. It was soon followed by Australia, New Zealand, Thailand, and others.People walk in the Kreta Ayer district of Singapore ahead of the Lunar New Year on January 29.The mood in Singapore is now drastically different than in Hong Kong. A high vaccination rate and reopened travel with two dozen countries mean daily life has more or less resumed. Though cases there are spiking as well, people are able to go to the movies, meet friends at the bar, even attend sports events and live concerts. The few restrictions left -- like caps on social gatherings -- may soon be lifted too when the Omicron wave subsides, the health minister said Monday. "Like most Singaporeans, I am looking forward to it," he added.But there is little of that optimism in Hong Kong, with leaders defiant even as the city's tough travel restrictions render it increasingly isolated from the world. "With the full support of the central (Chinese) government, the government's united effort, and citizens' full support, we have to fight against this wave of the virus," Lam said on Tuesday. "Surrendering to the virus is not an option."

Shanghai (CNN) — How do you keep a city of 25 million people locked down indefinitely — and at what cost? That is the question confronting Chinese authorities as Shanghai's strict Covid-19 lockdown, now entering its fifth week, shows no sign of lifting, and frustration among residents reaches a boiling point rarely seen in public in mainland China.
Since late March, virtually the entire city has been confined to homes or residential compounds, with movement permitted only for mandatory PCR testing or medical emergencies. Food deliveries, coordinated through neighborhood committees, have been erratic, leaving some residents without adequate supplies for days at a time. Videos of people shouting from windows, banging pots, and arguing with hazmat-suited officials have circulated widely on Chinese social media before being censored — an unusual volume of dissent for a country with tight controls on public expression.
The lockdown was initially described by officials as a temporary, rolling measure targeting the city's two main districts in sequence. It has since expanded to cover all of Shanghai, with no clear end date communicated to residents.
Daily case numbers remain in the tens of thousands, the vast majority asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic. Despite this, China's zero-Covid policy — reaffirmed publicly by President Xi Jinping as recently as last month — mandates that all positive cases be transferred to centralized quarantine facilities, regardless of clinical condition. The result has been a severe strain on the city's infrastructure. Quarantine centers, hastily converted from exhibition halls, stadiums and schools, are reported to be overcrowded, with inadequate sanitation and insufficient food in some locations.
Shanghai's lockdown also carries significant consequences beyond its borders. The city is home to China's largest port and one of the world's busiest container shipping hubs. Weeks of disruption have contributed to growing backlogs and added pressure to global supply chains already fragmented by two years of pandemic disruption.
A diverging world
The contrast with other major economies is stark. Across much of Europe, North America and Southeast Asia, governments have moved to treat Covid as endemic — dropping mask mandates, reopening borders and scaling back testing requirements. In the United Kingdom, all legal Covid restrictions were lifted in February. In the United States, the Biden administration ended the public health emergency declaration in May.
Even within Asia, the divide is visible. South Korea lifted its outdoor mask mandate in April. Japan, long one of the most cautious countries in the region, has steadily reopened to international tourists. Thailand and Vietnam, having once pursued aggressive suppression strategies, have pivoted to living with the virus after vaccination rates climbed.
China remains the last major economy committed to zero-Covid, a position that has grown increasingly difficult to defend as the Omicron variant, far more transmissible than previous strains, renders elimination all but unachievable according to many epidemiologists. Experts outside China have largely concluded that the question is no longer whether to transition away from zero-Covid, but when and how.
For now, Chinese authorities show little sign of reconsidering. State media has continued to frame the lockdown as a necessary and patriotic sacrifice, while officials point to the comparatively low death toll in China relative to Western nations as evidence the policy has worked. But with economic growth forecasts being revised downward, factories idled, and residents increasingly vocal in their frustration, the pressure on Beijing is quietly mounting — even if it remains, for now, out of public view.
CNN's Beijing bureau contributed to this report.

Shanghai (CNN) — Five weeks into a lockdown of one of the world's largest cities, Chinese authorities are showing no sign of backing down — and the 25 million people confined inside Shanghai are running out of patience.
Since late March, residents have been restricted to their homes or residential compounds, allowed outside only for mandatory PCR testing or medical emergencies. Food distribution, managed through neighborhood committees, has been inconsistent enough that some people have gone days without adequate supplies. Videos of residents shouting from apartment windows, banging pots in protest, and confronting hazmat-suited officials have spread rapidly on Chinese social media — before being taken down. The volume of public dissent is unusual in a country where such expressions are tightly controlled.
When the lockdown began, officials described it as a targeted, rolling measure covering two districts in sequence. It now covers the entire city, with no clear end date.
Tens of thousands of cases are being recorded daily. The overwhelming majority are asymptomatic or mild. That distinction doesn't matter under China's zero-Covid policy, reaffirmed publicly by President Xi Jinping as recently as last month: every positive case must be transferred to centralized quarantine, regardless of how sick — or not sick — the person actually is. Exhibition halls, stadiums and schools have been converted into quarantine centers. Reports of overcrowding, poor sanitation and insufficient food have filtered out, despite efforts to control the narrative.
The economic ripple effects extend well beyond city limits. Shanghai's port is the largest in China and among the busiest in the world. Weeks of disruption have deepened backlogs and added strain to global supply chains that were already fragile after two years of pandemic turbulence.
A diverging world
Elsewhere, the direction of travel is clear. Across Europe, North America and much of Southeast Asia, governments have moved on — dropping mask mandates, reopening borders, scaling back testing. Britain lifted all legal restrictions in February. The US ended its public health emergency in May. In Asia, South Korea dropped its outdoor mask mandate in April. Japan has cautiously reopened to tourists. Thailand and Vietnam, once among the region's more aggressive suppressors, have pivoted toward living with the virus as vaccination rates climbed.
China stands alone among major economies still committed to elimination. That position has become harder to sustain as Omicron — far more transmissible than earlier variants — has led many epidemiologists outside China to conclude that zero-Covid is no longer a realistic long-term goal. The question, in most of the world, has shifted from whether to transition away from the policy to how and when.
Beijing, for now, isn't engaging with that question publicly. State media frames the lockdown as necessary sacrifice. Officials point to China's comparatively low death toll as proof the approach has worked. But with growth forecasts being cut, factories sitting idle, and citizens more openly frustrated than at almost any point in recent memory, the pressure is building — quietly, and not yet visibly, but building all the same.
CNN's Beijing bureau contributed to this report.

(CNN)The 2021 NCAA college football season has looked a lot different than 2020: stadiums are packed with fans, the tailgates, the marching bands and the cheerleaders are back, too.All those things that the sport has sorely missed during the height of the pandemic will be on full display when the two best teams from the nation's best conference meet in what's become the sport's capital city in the last decade or so.It's top-ranked Georgia and third-ranked Alabama facing off in the SEC Championship game at Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta and it doesn't get a whole lot better than this!Weathering stormy TidesAlabama has been the dominant force in college football for the last dozen years. The Crimson Tide have won six national championships and seven SEC titles under legendary coach Nick Saban.Read MoreA tremendous 106 of their players have been drafted into the NFL in that span -- 38 of them in the first round. Six of those first round picks came from last year's squad, which steamrolled its way through a 13-0 season on its way to another national title.Losing all that talent to the NFL might cause some teams to rebuild, but not Saban and the Crimson Tide. They just reload.Nick Saban has won six national titles at Alabama since he took the helm in 2007.Alabama was the nation's top-ranked team and the national title favorite again as 2021 began, with a new quarterback under center in Bryce Young and a new offensive coordinator in Bill O'Brien.After a 2-0 start, they got all they could ask for in their first true road game at Florida. The Gators rallied from an 18-point deficit and had a chance to tie it with three minutes left but failed on a two-point conversion. 'Bama hung on for the 31-29 win.The Tide were 5-0 when they went to Texas A&M, and that's when their unbeaten run came crashing down -- Seth Small's last-second field goal giving the Aggies a shocking 41-38 win.Crimson Tide players were left wondering if their dreams of a national title were now over as they waded through the sea of A&M fans that had stormed the field celebrating the upset. But you can never underestimate the heart of a champion and that's what Alabama showed the rest of the way.They won five straight games, including a pair of narrow wins over LSU and Arkansas, to secure another SEC title game berth. But they wouldn't have stayed in the national title hunt without a comeback for the ages against in-state rivals Auburn.After struggling the whole game, Bryce Young led the Tide on a 97-yard touchdown drive in the final minute and 43 seconds to tie the game at 10. Young wasn't going to be denied in overtime. His two-point conversion pass broke the deadlock in the 4th extra period, giving 'Bama a win that seemed unlikely the first 58 minutes of the game.Bryce Young sparked Heisman talk after leading the Crimson Tide to a gritty victory over Auburn.Young's performance led many to consider him a top contender now for the Heisman Trophy and helped his team weather yet another storm."Over the course of the season, I think sometimes when things have gone poorly, players have responded very well," Saban told reporters after the Crimson Tide's win over Auburn. "It's great to have the resiliency to overcome it, but you'd like to be able to sustain (it) with consistency, so you don't get in those situations."They'll need to find that consistency as they face their toughest challenge of the year against the Georgia Bulldogs.Dawg days are over?After winning a fourth national title in seven years as Alabama's defensive coordinator, Kirby Smart decided to leave his mentor and friend Nick Saban and take the head coaching job at conference rival Georgia in early 2016. The Bulldogs had been a good team (19 straight bowl appearances) but now they wanted to be great.Georgia's last national title was in 1980 and they've only won the SEC twice since 1982, but they believed Smart was the man that could take them to the promised land.Kirby brought his mentor's blueprint for success with him to Athens: a powerful defense, a solid running game and an efficient passing attack -- and it paid dividends right away.Georgia head coach Kirby Smart has brought his mentor's approach to Athens -- with much success -- but he still lacks a national title.Georgia got to the national title game, but fell, in Smart's second season. And that loss to end the 2017 season was a painful one, as it was against -- you guessed it -- Alabama.The game was in Atlanta and the Bulldogs led 13-0 at halftime. 'Bama's star QB Jalen Hurts was struggling, so Saban decided to start freshman Tua Tagovaiola in the second half.Tua led an epic comeback, capping it off with a 41-yard touchdown pass to DeVonta Smith, giving the Tide a 26-23 overtime win. It was their fifth national title under Saban and his first without his top assistant, who had to find a way to overcome the sting of that defeat and move forward. Georgia made some big-time bowl games the next three years but none of them were in the college football playoffs, and fans were starting to get a bit restless.But 2021 has been a different story. The Dawgs have forged a perfect 12-0 record, outscoring their opponents by almost 34 points a game.They lead the nation in fewest points allowed and fewest yards allowed per game and their closest win was a 10-3 victory over Clemson in the season opener. That's the kind of season Alabama's used to having, not Georgia.The Bulldogs have been No. 1 in the country ever since Alabama's loss at Texas A&M and roll into the SEC title game with a ton of confidence and even a bit of swagger."This team has responded to all the things we've challenged them with," said Smart after beating Georgia Tech on Saturday. "We hit them with different things each week, things we want to really focus on -- accountability from their standpoint of holding other players accountable from week to week for different things. And they've done it each and every time."What's at stake?So for the first time since that national title game meeting almost four years ago, Alabama and Georgia meet again. And it's in Atlanta again, this time for the SEC crown.Both teams feel as though they need this win but for very different reasons.For Alabama, which enters No. 3 in the college football playoff rankings, the chances of a seventh national title in the Nick Saban era are on the line here: no team with two losses has made the college football playoff since the four-team format began in the 2014 season.A lot could happen in other conference title games on Saturday which could allow the Crimson Tide to sneak in with a second loss, but they can't count on that happening.Georgia's No. 1 in the playoff rankings and with a loss, they'd still secure a top-four spot, even if it isn't as the number one seed.But for this team, it's about beating Alabama. Kirby Smart's 0-3 against his former boss and the Bulldogs have lost six straight in the series overall -- their last win coming in 2007, which was Saban's first season with the Crimson Tide.The Bulldogs head into the SEC Championship guaranteed a spot in the College Football Playoffs, but a win over 'Bama would be huge for the team."Every year is independent of the previous," Smart said. "Our job is to go play the best possible game we can. That's what we've been trying to build towards this year. We haven't played our best yet, and we think our best is still out there. That's the goal."Another Georgia team, the Atlanta Braves, had to get past a Los Angeles Dodgers team which had haunted them for years on their road to a first World Series title in 26 years. In order for the Bulldogs to win the national title which has eluded them for the last 41 years, they're going to have to climb over their own boogeyman: Alabama.

(CNN) The 2023 college basketball season has delivered everything fans could have hoped for: packed arenas, deafening student sections, and the kind of rivalry matchups that remind you why March became its own cultural institution.
All of that will be on full display when two of the sport's historic powerhouses collide in the Big East Championship at Madison Square Garden in New York City — the self-proclaimed mecca of basketball. It's top-ranked Connecticut and third-ranked Marquette, and the stakes could hardly be higher.
The Husky Machine
UConn has long been one of college basketball's most decorated programs. The Huskies have won five national championships and have become synonymous with tournament excellence under coach Dan Hurley, who has steadily rebuilt the program into a genuine superpower since arriving in Storrs in 2018.
This season, UConn entered the year as the clear favorite after dismantling the field en route to a national title the previous spring. Their roster turned over significantly, losing four players to the NBA Draft, but Hurley simply reloaded. That has become something of a trademark.
The Huskies opened the year 7-0 before a tense road trip to Villanova nearly derailed things. Trailing by nine with under four minutes to play, freshman guard Solo Ball — who had struggled to find his footing early in the season — took over, scoring eight straight points and setting up the game-winning layup with eleven seconds left. UConn survived, 74-73.
They have not lost since. A 17-1 conference record heading into Championship weekend is as dominant a run as the Big East has seen in years, and Ball has gone from question mark to the frontrunner for Big East Player of the Year. His emergence has given UConn something even their previous title team lacked: a genuine go-to scorer in the closing minutes of tight games.
"He's grown up before our eyes," Hurley told reporters after UConn's regular season finale. "Early on, he was trying to do too much. Now he trusts the system, trusts his teammates, and when the moment gets big, he rises."
The Golden Eagles Circle
Marquette's path to this moment has been equally compelling, if a little more turbulent. Head coach Shaka Smart, now in his third season in Milwaukee, finally has the pieces to make a genuine title run — and his team knows it.
The Golden Eagles opened the year unranked but won eleven of their first thirteen games, climbing steadily into the top ten. Their calling card has been suffocating perimeter defense and a balanced offense that features five players averaging double figures — the first Marquette team to accomplish that in over two decades.
Their only real stumble came in late January, a two-game losing skid against Providence and Georgetown that briefly threatened to derail their conference title hopes. Smart responded by simplifying the offense and shifting to a smaller, quicker lineup — a gamble that paid off immediately. Marquette won eight straight to close the regular season, including an emphatic 81-64 road win over then-No. 6 Creighton.
Senior guard Kam Jones has been the engine of it all, averaging 22 points per game since the lineup change and drawing comparisons to previous Marquette greats. "We went through a tough stretch and it made us better," Jones said after the regular season ended. "We know who we are now. We stopped trying to be perfect and started playing together."
What's at stake?
For UConn, a Big East title would be the program's sixth in the Dan Hurley era and would all but cement their status as the No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament. More than that, a win would validate their claim to being the best team in the country — a claim a few voters still dispute despite their record.
For Marquette, the calculus is different. Smart is 0-4 against UConn since arriving at the program, and the Golden Eagles have not won the Big East Tournament since 2003. A win on Saturday would shatter both of those storylines at once, and with Selection Sunday one day away, it would send the program into the tournament on the kind of momentum that has historically translated to deep runs.
Two years ago, a different Marquette team reached the Elite Eight before bowing out to eventual champion Kansas. Smart and his players believe this group is better. Whether they're right depends on 40 minutes in the Garden.
"We've put ourselves in a position to compete for something real," Smart said this week. "Now we have to go do it. Nobody's going to hand us anything — especially not them."

(CNN) College basketball's 2023 season has given fans just about everything they could ask for — packed arenas, raucous student sections, and the kind of rivalry matchups that turn March into its own national holiday.
Saturday brings the best of it. Top-ranked Connecticut and third-ranked Marquette meet in the Big East Championship at Madison Square Garden, the self-declared mecca of the sport, and both programs have something serious to play for.
