Stats don't lieIf Guardiola is downplaying his side's chances of clinching the quadruple this season, you feel it might be a different story within the dressing room. Speaking ahead of the tie against Monchengladbach, young defender Oleksandr Zinchenko gave an interesting insight into the mindset of the players when he said he believed his side could win all the remaining competitions. Typically, Guardiola was quick to pour cold water on Zinchenko's claims, saying: "I am older than Mr. Zinchenko, I have more experience and I do not agree with him." 'What a beautiful journey': Pele congratulates Cristiano Ronaldo for surpassing Brazilian's 'record of goals in official matches'But whether he admits it publicly or not, you get the sense that Guardiola must believe it's possible. After all, he has form. During his managerial career, the 50-year-old turned Barcelona into a world-beating side and transformed Bayern Munich into a domestic powerhouse. His time at Barca in particular will never be forgotten. Guardiola created one of the best teams ever assembled with the likes of Lionel Messi, Xavi and Andres Iniesta changing the way football was played in Europe. In his first year in charge during the 2008/2009 season, Barcelona won six trophies, including the Champions League, La Liga and Copa del Rey. Despite not quite having the same caliber of players, his current Manchester City squad bears distinct similarities to those great sides and the statistics simply speak for themselves. City has not conceded a goal in the Champions League since the opening group match -- that's more than 11 hours of football -- and has won 24 of its last 25 matches. It's a team that smothers games, dominates possession and limits its opponents to only a handful of chances. It's not as though Guardiola needs to worry about keeping players fresh, either. City's squad is packed full of world-class talent allowing the manager to chop and change his starting lineup with ease. On many occasions this season, the majority of players on City's substitutes bench could make the starting lineup of almost every top team in Europe. City didn't even miss star playmaker Kevin de Bruyne during the Belgian's injury layoff earlier this year, with players such as Phil Foden and Ilkay Gundogan providing the team with goals and an abundance of chances. READ: Barca boss Ronald Koeman praises Lionel Messi after record-equaling appearanceJUST WATCHEDShaun Wright-Phillips on City's win over QPR to win Premier LeagueReplayMore Videos ...MUST WATCHShaun Wright-Phillips on City's win over QPR to win Premier League 04:46Strong defense But what's different about this season is City's defense. Guardiola has seemingly settled on a solid partnership of John Stones and Ruben Dias at the back and the team has reaped the rewards. "You can't deny that Ruben and John made an incredible step forward for the team this season," Guardiola said. "We defend with the ball. That is the most important thing -- that when the opponent arrives we are solid enough."Winning the Premier League early will only help City's bid for a quadruple, allowing the manager to rotate his squad more freely. City is currently 14 points clear of second-placed Manchester United and can wrap up the title as early as May 8 if it doesn't drop any points."We are close but still we have games to play. Sometimes when you win earlier it's not good for other competitions," said Guardiola, per BBC Sport. "When you have to fight to win one title, you have to fight to win the other ones. It's important not to think too far and just focus on the next one."As much as Guardiola is keen to play down the likelihood of such a remarkable achievement, you'd be a brave person to bet against them on current form. Next up for City is a potentially tricky tie against Everton in the FA Cup on Saturday. Win that and it will be yet another step closer to the quadruple.

Bayern Munich's relentless run of form continued on Wednesday as the German champions secured a convincing 3-1 victory over RB Leipzig to advance to the semifinals of the German Cup.
The win extended Bayern's impressive unbeaten streak and kept alive hopes of winning multiple trophies this season. Already leading the Bundesliga standings and still competing in European competition, the team continues to demonstrate the consistency that has made it one of the most feared sides in world football.
Manager Thomas Tuchel praised his players after the match but was careful not to get carried away by discussions of potential silverware. While supporters have begun speculating about another historic campaign, the coach insisted that the focus remains firmly on the next challenge.
"We are playing well, but there is still a long way to go," Tuchel said after the game. "Football changes very quickly, and success depends on maintaining the same level every week."
The victory once again highlighted Bayern's depth. Several key players were rested, yet the team maintained its intensity and control throughout the match. Young talents stepped in alongside experienced internationals, helping Bayern dominate possession and create numerous scoring opportunities.
Much of the club's success this season has been built on a strong defensive foundation. The partnership at the heart of the defense has provided stability, while the midfield has effectively limited opponents' chances before they develop into serious threats.
At the other end of the pitch, Bayern's attacking players continue to deliver. Goals have come from across the squad, reducing the team's dependence on any single individual and making it difficult for opponents to contain them.
With crucial fixtures approaching in both domestic and European competitions, Bayern remains in contention for a remarkable season. The club's supporters are beginning to dream of another trophy-laden campaign, although players and coaches continue to stress the importance of staying focused on one match at a time.
Their next challenge arrives this weekend against a top-four rival, a match that could further strengthen Bayern's position and bring them another step closer to achieving their ambitions.

Bayern Munich took another step forward on Wednesday night, beating RB Leipzig 3-1 and keeping what could become a memorable season firmly on track.
The result sends Bayern into the semifinals of the German Cup and adds to a growing list of positive results. With the team also competing for the Bundesliga title and progressing in Europe, talk of multiple trophies has naturally started to gather momentum.
Not surprisingly, Thomas Tuchel was reluctant to join in. Speaking afterward, the Bayern manager praised the performance but avoided making any bold predictions about what the team might achieve by the end of the season.
"It's a good win, nothing more than that right now," he said. "There are still many important matches ahead, and things can change very quickly in football."
One reason for Bayern's confidence is the strength of the squad. Even with a few regular starters given a rest, the team looked comfortable for long stretches of the game. Younger players contributed when called upon, while the more experienced members of the squad ensured the performance never lost its structure.
Defensively, Bayern appears far more settled than it did earlier in the campaign. The back line has developed a stronger understanding, and the team as a whole has become harder to break down.
Going forward, the threat comes from several different areas. Rather than relying on one star player to produce goals, Bayern has seen contributions from attackers, midfielders, and even defenders throughout the season.
For now, the club remains in the hunt on several fronts. Supporters may be looking ahead and imagining what is possible, but inside the dressing room the message seems much simpler: keep winning, and everything else can wait.
A difficult league match awaits this weekend, providing another test of whether Bayern can maintain the momentum that has carried them through recent months.

(CNN)Stage parents seldom fare well in movies and TV, but "King Richard" bucks that trend, placing Will Smith on center court in an overwhelmingly flattering portrait of tennis patriarch Richard Williams. Smith aces the performance, which is the main attribute in elevating the story above the standard sports-movie conventions.The film actually follows a fairly concentrated stretch in the formative years of Venus (Saniyya Sidney) and Serena Williams (Demi Singleton) under the watchful eye and tutelage of their father Richard, who speaks frequently of the 78-page plan he mapped out for conquering the nearly all-White tennis world before they were old enough to hold a racquet."I'm in the champion-raising business," Williams announces as he makes the rounds looking for a coach, met with dismissive and vaguely racist replies, including "You ever think about basketball?"Williams can be imperious (hence the title) and overbearing, much to the occasional chagrin of his daughters and wife (Aunjanue Ellis), who is no wallflower when it comes to the coaching chores or speaking her mind.The portrait that emerges from director Reinaldo Marcus Green and writer Zach Baylin consistently paints him as a heroic figure, protecting his daughters from dangers in the community, stressing their education as well as their ground strokes and working tirelessly to advance their careers, perusing tennis magazines while holding down a job as a night watchman.Read MoreAny excesses, such as having the girls practice in a pouring rain, are filtered through that prism. And obviously, Williams' determination to "stick to the plan" paid off handsomely, despite the irritation and exasperation of tennis coaches (the most prominent played by Tony Goldwyn and Jon Bernthal) riled by his refusal to follow the customary script, including his decision to pull Venus out of juniors tournaments.Demi Singleton as Serena, Will Smith as Richard Williams and Saniyya Sidney as Venus in the Warner Bros. drama 'King Richard.'Smith not only captures the cadence of Williams' voice but the unwavering confidence that he embodied, absolutely convinced that he knew what was best for his girls. It's down the movie's final stretch, as those plans begin to bear fruit, that "King Richard" feels more and more conventional, from the stirring music to the questions of how and when to cash in on Venus' prowess while the younger Serena is left behind, but as we all know, not for long.Smith (who also produced the film) is receiving a vigorous push for awards consideration, and he certainly nails the role in a way that belongs in that conversation, even if the movie as a whole seems unlikely to join him.Perhaps foremost, "King Richard" is framed as a response to the criticism and second-guessing that the outspoken Williams faced as his daughters took the tennis world by storm, which might explain why both signed on as executive producers.Indeed, it's hard to escape the sense that "King Richard" exists in part to let the Williams family set the record straight. And when it comes to giving Williams his due as both a tennis guru and a father, in this case, love means everything."King Richard" premieres Nov. 19 in US theaters and on HBO Max, from Warner Bros., like CNN, a unit of WarnerMedia. It's rated PG-13.

(CNN) Parents who push their children toward greatness have often been portrayed as controlling or misguided figures on screen, but "Rising Star" takes a different approach. The sports drama places its focus squarely on a determined father whose relentless belief in his daughter's talent becomes the foundation of the story. Anchored by a commanding lead performance from Michael B. Jordan, the film rises above familiar genre formulas even when it occasionally leans on them.
The movie chronicles the early years of track-and-field prodigy Maya Johnson (played by Nia Thompson), following her development under the guidance of her father, Marcus (Jordan). Long before college recruiters and national attention arrived, Marcus had already mapped out a detailed vision for her future, convinced she could become one of the country's elite athletes.
"We're not chasing medals," Marcus repeatedly tells skeptical observers. "We're building a legacy."
That confidence often borders on stubbornness. Marcus spends much of the film challenging coaches, questioning established training methods and insisting that his daughter progress at a pace he believes is right. His determination frequently creates tension with others, including Maya's mother (Regina Hall), who shares responsibility for her daughter's development but worries about the pressure being placed on her.
Director Gina Prince-Bythewood and screenwriter Aaron Covington present Marcus largely through an admiring lens. He is depicted as a tireless advocate who balances multiple jobs, shields his family from negative influences and remains unwavering in his belief that Maya deserves opportunities often denied to athletes from her background.
Even moments that could be interpreted as excessive—early-morning workouts, strict routines and difficult sacrifices—are framed as evidence of his commitment rather than flaws in judgment. As Maya's talent begins attracting national attention, the film increasingly validates Marcus' unconventional methods.
The story becomes somewhat more conventional in its later stages. Predictable conflicts emerge over sponsorship opportunities, coaching decisions and the demands that accompany growing fame. Inspirational montages and swelling music occasionally push the film toward familiar sports-drama territory.
Still, Jordan's performance remains compelling throughout. He captures Marcus' charisma, intensity and unshakable confidence, creating a character whose certainty can be frustrating while remaining understandable. His portrayal gives emotional weight to scenes that might otherwise feel overly familiar.
The film also appears interested in revisiting public perceptions of ambitious sports parents. By emphasizing Marcus' sacrifices and motivations, "Rising Star" argues that his actions stemmed less from personal ambition than from a desire to create opportunities for his daughter.
That perspective shapes nearly every aspect of the narrative. The result is a movie that functions not only as an inspirational sports story but also as a defense of a father whose methods were often questioned by those around him.
Whether audiences fully embrace that interpretation may vary. What is difficult to dispute, however, is the strength of the central performance. Thanks largely to Jordan's work, "Rising Star" succeeds in turning a familiar sports biography into an engaging character study.
"Rising Star" opens nationwide Friday. It is rated PG-13.

(CNN) Ambitious sports parents rarely receive sympathetic treatment in movies, but "Rising Star" approaches the subject from a very different angle. Rather than questioning a father's relentless involvement in his child's career, the film largely celebrates it, with Michael B. Jordan delivering the kind of performance that keeps the story engaging even when it follows a familiar path.
The drama centers on Marcus Johnson, a father convinced from an early age that his daughter Maya is destined for athletic greatness. Years before she becomes a nationally recognized track prospect, he has already built an elaborate plan for her future and spends much of his life trying to make it a reality.
Marcus' confidence is impossible to miss. He challenges coaches, ignores conventional wisdom and rarely backs down when others suggest a different approach. At times that certainty strains relationships, particularly with Maya's mother, played by Regina Hall, who supports her daughter's dreams but worries about the expectations being placed on her.
Director Gina Prince-Bythewood presents Marcus as a deeply committed parent rather than an overbearing one. The film repeatedly highlights the sacrifices he makes—working long hours, studying training methods and searching for opportunities that might help Maya advance.
As a result, actions that could be viewed as excessive are usually framed as signs of dedication. Grueling practice schedules and strict discipline become evidence of his determination rather than reasons for criticism. Not surprisingly, the story gradually rewards that persistence as Maya's success begins to validate many of his decisions.
The film settles into more conventional territory during its final act. Questions about sponsorships, media attention and career choices introduce conflicts that will feel familiar to anyone who has seen a sports drama before. The inspirational soundtrack and emotional crescendos occasionally reinforce that sense of predictability.
What prevents the movie from becoming generic is Jordan's performance. He captures both the appeal and the frustration of a man who never doubts his own judgment. Marcus can be difficult, stubborn and exhausting, yet Jordan makes it clear why people continue to follow his lead.
The movie also seems intent on reshaping how audiences view figures like Marcus. Rather than portraying him as someone living through his child's achievements, it presents him as a father driven by conviction and a belief in possibilities others failed to see.
That argument is woven throughout the film, which often feels as much like a defense of Marcus as it does a chronicle of Maya's rise.
Not every viewer will be persuaded by that perspective, and the storytelling occasionally leans too heavily on sports-movie conventions. Even so, the strength of the lead performance gives the film an emotional center that carries it through its weaker moments.
In the end, "Rising Star" works best not as an underdog story but as a portrait of determination—one anchored by a performance strong enough to make the familiar feel fresh.
"Rising Star" arrives in theaters Friday and carries a PG-13 rating.

CNN is committed to covering gender inequality wherever it occurs in the world. This report is a collaboration between CNN and The Fuller Project. It is part of As Equals, an ongoing series.  Seconds after Veronica opened her eyes in the hospital, she knew who had put her there. Her body ached; her head throbbed.The night before, the 25-year-old mother-of-five was busy buying medication for her children in Moroto, a town in northeast Uganda. When Veronica (whose surname we're not using to protect her identity) returned home, her husband picked up a sharp object and stabbed her in the right eye. He then beat her, and when she blacked out, he fled, she said.Two legal volunteers from the Association of Women Lawyers (FIDA-U), an organization that provides legal aid and access to essential services for women, were already working on the ground in the community. They heard Veronica's screams and decided to investigate. After finding her lying unconscious on the floor, they called their colleague Jacob Lokuda, a front-line legal clerk who responds rapidly to violent incidents, and who recounted what happened."She had lost a lot of blood," said 25-year-old Lokuda, "it was very nasty."The three men carried her to Moroto hospital, roughly four miles away. By car, the journey is a 20-minute drive; by foot, it took over one hour. Veronica drifted in and out of consciousness, mumbling that she thought she was already dead. 

"He had gone beyond reason"
Veronica

Read MoreOn May 4, Ugandan President Yoweri Museveni began to loosen the country's strict anti-coronavirus restrictions after more than six weeks in lockdown. While businesses including hardware shops and wholesale stores have now reopened, the existing ban on all public and private transport remains intact.This means, critics say, that many women will continue not only to be trapped at home with a potential perpetrator, but they remain unable to travel to seek medical treatment, refuge or help.The country currently has 160 confirmed Covid-19 cases and no fatalities, according to Johns Hopkins University.Many women, like Veronica, have found themselves forced to spend more time with partners who were already abusive. Economic worry is an added tension. More than 80% of Ugandans work in the informal sector and many have lost their jobs due to the Covid-19 restrictions."We stopped a lot to catch our breath," says Lokuda, who had already jogged 40 minutes to Veronica's village from his own home. "We didn't have any protective gear, such as gloves, but she needed medical attention," he adds."He had gone beyond reason," Veronica said over the phone. In late March, Museveni indicated that domestic violence is not life threatening and should not be considered so during the Covid-19 pandemic."We're just dealing with a few things [that are] life-threatening. Childbirth, snakebite, heart attack -- finish. What else is there? We're not dealing with all problems. Somebody is drunk and has beaten his wife? No, no, no," he said.He has since addressed the issue and said he is devising a "comprehensive plan" on how best to handle the situation.     While ambulances have been given travel permits, the number of vehicles is low and many citizens live in villages with a patchy phone signal.Those working in essential services such as health care are allowed on the roads, yet legal aid providers were not deemed essential until last week. Now, 30 lawyers working for the Uganda Law Society are permitted to provide urgent legal services."This is a positive step," says Irene Ekonga, FIDA-U's director of programs, "but it's a drop in the ocean."Movement is an issue, agrees Rose Nalubega, acting commissioner for the national police's Sexual and Children Offences department in Kampala, the bustling Ugandan capital."Response is our greatest challenge," she said in an interview. "We're operating in the normal way but [Covid-19] has escalated the problem. We were not prepared, but we try."Ugandans can apply for a special travel permit from the resident district commissioner, but locals have complained their offices are often empty. For organizations like FIDA-U, time is of the essence -- and applying for a permit waiver can take hours."In these emergency situations, the first response is what really counts," says Lokuda. "I put everything down because we have to move fast, but there are lots of delays."Those found driving without permission can be arrested or have their vehicle impounded.Several weeks ago, Lokuda received an 8 a.m. call about an alleged rape. As it was still early, and the young woman's village was a little further out, he decided to seek permission for transport. After negotiations, he didn't arrive until 2 p.m."It was too late," he sighs. "The perpetrator had already run away but at least we managed to bring her for a medical examination."Police were called and they took her to a station before Lokuda brought her to the hospital in the car he'd been given approval to travel in.

"Violence against women is accepted here"
Josephine Aparo, Senior Coordinator at International Justice Mission

In one month, police noted a surge in gender-based violence cases, with an estimated 3,280 recorded between March 30 and April 28, according to Frank Tumwebaze, the Minister of Gender, Labour and Social Development.In 2019, an average of 1,137 domestic violence cases were reported monthly.FIDA-U say they witnessed a 522% increase in the number of cases reported by phone (from nine on average to 56 calls per week) since the lockdown was first introduced, though they believe many more domestic violence cases are going unrecorded.Violence against children has also soared: the Uganda Child Helpline dealt with 881 cases since the lockdown began in late March (the average is 248).The surge in domestic abuse is set against a backdrop of already high levels in the country. Forty-six percent of ever-married women say they are afraid of their current or most recent spouse or partner compared to 23% of ever-married men, according to figures published by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics in 2016."Violence against women is accepted here," says Josephine Aparo, Senior Coordinator at International Justice Mission (IJM) Uganda, who work with police and prosecutors to bring perpetrators to court.After a 14-day period, the President will announce the next phase of reopening on 19 May. Yet experts are concerned the existing problems will continue."[Once the transport ban lifts], women in close proximity with abusive partners might be able to report and seek refuge elsewhere," says Ekonga."Issues like economic difficulties are still likely to persist, as well as dependence on male partners for financial support. Cases might drop slightly but by and large, I think they will remain higher than the rates before the lockdown."Veronica had been with her husband for 12 years before this assault, which she reported to police in a local station. She had previously reported him some years ago because, she says, he wasn't taking care of the children properly. He didn't have a job, and Veronica's work as a street vendor selling meat dried up due to Covid-19 restrictions. They had little money for food or rent. While her case is being followed up by the Ugandan police, several rights organizations say the police have been unable to adequately respond to incidents or make arrests."The police are usually under-resourced," explains Tina Musuya, executive director of the Center for Domestic Violence Prevention. "During an emergency like this? Violence against women is a forgotten territory."Amid the pandemic, police vehicles were reallocated to the Covid-19 response, which left a shortage elsewhere. Several organizations who supported law enforcement with cars before the transport ban have since been given permission to do so again. IJM, for example, is providing cars to assist police investigations into gender-based violence crimes.Many women and children flee their abusers with nowhere to go: all domestic violence shelters have closed across the country, bar one, though this may change as the lockdown gradually lifts. The police have recently opened a new temporary shelter in Kampala, and launched a toll-free line in a bid to handle the increasing number of cases."It's astonishing," says Asia Russell of Health GAP, a HIV advocacy organization with staff in Uganda. "The mode of implementation of the Covid-19 response has killed people," she adds, referring to vulnerable groups such as women experiencing violence, with chronic illnesses and those who are pregnant."Where is the infrastructure for communities who are experiencing increased terror? Don't they matter?" Russell says.Weeks after Veronica was released from hospital, her eye is still painful.She and her husband haven't spoken since that "fateful night," she says, before adding firmly: "I'm not going to have contact with him again."She still has difficulty sleeping, but she feels safe and, for now, that will do.Louise Donovan is a Nairobi-based correspondent with The Fuller Project, a journalism nonprofit reporting on global issues impacting women.Top image: Shutterstock/CNN Photo Illustration by Gabrielle Smith

As lockdown measures continued across the country, aid workers in rural Kenya reported a sharp increase in cases of domestic violence, highlighting the unintended consequences of restrictions designed to slow the spread of Covid-19.
One recent case involved a 29-year-old woman from a village outside Eldoret who was admitted to a local hospital after an alleged assault by her partner. According to community outreach workers, the woman had been returning from a nearby market when an argument at home escalated into violence.
Neighbors who heard the disturbance contacted a local support organization, which dispatched volunteers to the scene. By the time they arrived, the woman was seriously injured and required immediate medical attention. With public transportation suspended under emergency regulations, volunteers transported her several miles to the nearest clinic using limited available resources.
Advocacy groups say such incidents have become increasingly common since movement restrictions were introduced. Many families have faced growing financial pressure as businesses closed and employment opportunities disappeared. At the same time, victims often find it more difficult to leave dangerous situations or seek assistance.
Human rights organizations argue that access to support services has been severely disrupted. Travel restrictions, limited transportation, and reduced staffing have slowed emergency responses and complicated efforts to investigate allegations of abuse.
Officials acknowledge the challenges but say resources have been stretched by the demands of the public health crisis. Law enforcement agencies continue to respond to reports, though response times have often increased in remote areas.
Recent data collected by local authorities suggests a substantial rise in reported domestic violence cases during the lockdown period. Child protection organizations have also reported an increase in calls involving abuse and neglect.
Experts caution that the problem extends beyond the immediate health emergency. Economic hardship, unemployment, and social isolation may continue to place strain on vulnerable households even after restrictions are lifted.
For survivors, access to medical care, legal support, and safe shelter remains critical. Aid groups have urged governments to ensure that protection services remain available during future emergencies and that victims can reach help without unnecessary delays.
Several weeks after leaving the hospital, the woman is continuing her recovery with support from relatives. Although the physical injuries are healing, she says the emotional impact of the incident remains difficult to overcome.

The effects of pandemic restrictions have reached far beyond public health, and in parts of Kenya, support organizations say one of the clearest signs has been a rise in domestic violence cases.
A recent incident near Eldoret illustrates the challenge. A 29-year-old woman was taken to hospital after allegedly being attacked by her partner following a dispute at home. Residents nearby heard shouting and contacted a local community group that assists people facing abuse.
When volunteers arrived, they found the woman injured and in need of urgent treatment. Getting her to medical care was not straightforward. Transport services in the area had been reduced because of Covid-19 restrictions, forcing those helping her to improvise a way to reach the nearest clinic.
Organizations working with survivors say stories like this have become more common over the past few years whenever strict movement controls are imposed. Financial stress has played a major role. Many households have struggled with lost income, while victims often find themselves spending more time in close quarters with abusive partners.
Even when someone decides to seek help, practical obstacles remain. Reaching a shelter, filing a police report, or obtaining medical care can take far longer than normal when transportation is limited and public services are under pressure.
Authorities say they continue to investigate complaints, but they also admit that resources have been stretched. Rural communities in particular can face lengthy delays before assistance arrives.
Figures released by local agencies point to a noticeable increase in reports of domestic abuse during lockdown periods. Children's organizations have observed similar trends, with more cases involving neglect and violence being brought to their attention.
Advocates worry that the effects will not disappear automatically once restrictions end. Economic uncertainty, debt, and unemployment can continue to fuel tensions long after a public health emergency has passed.
The woman at the center of this case has since left the hospital and is staying with family members. Her injuries are gradually improving, but she says recovery involves much more than physical healing. Feeling safe again, she explains, is taking longer.

(CNN)Lionel Messi's home debut for Paris Saint-Germain was far from how he would have dreamt it, with the Argentine forward being substituted by Mauricio Pochettino in the second half as his side went in search of a winning goal against Lyon. Lucas Paqueta had given the visiting team the lead after 54 minutes, before Neymar equalized from the penalty spot just over 10 minutes later.However, the game's main talking point came when Pochettino opted to replace Messi with right back Achraf Hakimi with 15 minutes of the match still remaining, seemingly to the six-time Ballon d'Or winner's chagrin.Pochettino and Messi didn't shake hands and the pair had a brief exchange as Messi made his way to the bench.PSG went on to win the Ligue 1 clash thanks to a Mauro Icardi header from Kylian Mbappé's cross in stoppage time and Pochettino defended his decision to replace Messi.Read MoreREAD: Paris Saint-Germain now has 'no excuses' in quest for Champions League domination after signing Lionel MessiLionel Messi gestures towards Mauricio Pochettino after being substituted."I think we all know we have great players in this 35-man squad," Pochettino told reporters. "Only 11 can play, we can't play more. The decisions in the game are made for the good of the team and each player."Every coach thinks about that. Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't. Sometimes players like it and sometimes they don't. At the end of the day, that's why we're here."These are decisions that have to be made by the coach. As for his reaction, I asked him how he was, he said he was okay. That was it. That was our exchange."Messi looked lively at times during the match -- hitting the woodwork with a free kick and bringing a smart save out of Anthony Lopes -- but PSG's marquee signing still looked far from being the player fans had become used to seeing at Barcelona.The fearsome front three of Messi, Neymar and Mbappé will no doubt need more time to gel, but Sunday's game provided glimpses of the threat they could become.

(CNN) The spotlight was firmly on the club's newest superstar, but his first appearance in front of home supporters ended with an unexpected moment as manager Carlos Mendes chose to substitute the forward during the second half of a tightly contested league match.
The hosts had fallen behind shortly after halftime when visiting midfielder Diego Herrera finished off a swift counterattack. The home side responded midway through the second period, drawing level through a penalty converted by captain Marco Silva.
Yet the defining image of the evening came with just under 20 minutes remaining. Mendes elected to replace star forward Javier Ortega, a decision that appeared to catch both the player and many supporters by surprise.
As Ortega walked toward the touchline, television cameras captured a brief exchange between the player and his coach. While there was no obvious confrontation, the forward's body language suggested frustration as he took his seat on the bench.
The substitution quickly became a major talking point among fans and pundits, particularly given Ortega's reputation as one of the game's most influential attacking players. Many expected him to remain on the field as the team searched for a winning goal.
Those questions became less pressing moments later when the hosts secured all three points. In stoppage time, striker Lucas Ferreira headed home from a cross delivered by winger Rafael Costa, sending the crowd into celebration.
Speaking after the match, Mendes defended his decision and emphasized that team considerations must come before individual preferences.
"When you have a squad full of talented players, difficult choices are part of the job," Mendes told reporters. "Every decision is made with the objective of helping the team. Sometimes players agree with those decisions, and sometimes they don't."
He added that he had briefly spoken with Ortega after the match and described the conversation as routine.
The forward showed flashes of his quality throughout the game. He tested the goalkeeper with a curling effort from distance and came close to scoring from a free kick that struck the crossbar. Nevertheless, he appeared to be working toward full sharpness after arriving at the club earlier in the season.
Supporters have eagerly anticipated the development of the team's high-profile attacking trio, and while signs of chemistry were visible, the partnership remains a work in progress.
The victory extended the club's strong start to the campaign, but discussion afterward centered less on the result than on the rare sight of a global superstar being withdrawn before the final whistle.

(CNN) Home supporters arrived hoping to watch their marquee signing take center stage. Instead, the evening's most memorable moment came when the star forward was unexpectedly substituted during the second half of a hard-fought league match.
The game itself remained finely balanced for much of the night. The visitors struck first shortly after the break through Diego Herrera, whose finish capped a quick attacking move. The hosts responded not long afterward, with captain Marco Silva converting from the penalty spot to level the score.
Attention shifted dramatically in the 72nd minute. Manager Carlos Mendes decided to take off Javier Ortega, one of the most recognizable players in world football, despite the match still hanging in the balance.
Television footage showed a short interaction between the two as Ortega left the field. There was no public argument, but the player's reaction suggested he was less than thrilled with the decision.
The move immediately sparked debate. Fans questioned whether removing such an influential attacker was the right call when the team still needed a goal to secure victory.
In the end, the coach's gamble paid off. Deep into stoppage time, Lucas Ferreira rose to meet a cross from Rafael Costa and headed home the winner, prompting celebrations throughout the stadium.
Afterward, Mendes stood by his choice.
"Managing a squad with this much talent means making difficult decisions," he said. "My responsibility is to think about what gives the team the best chance to win. Not every player will always be happy, but that's part of football."
He also downplayed the exchange with Ortega, saying they briefly spoke after the game and that there was no issue between them.
Although he did not find the net, Ortega produced several promising moments. A dangerous free kick rattled the crossbar, and he forced a strong save with a shot from outside the area. Still, he looked like a player who is continuing to adapt to a new environment and build match fitness.
The club's highly anticipated attacking partnership remains one of the most intriguing storylines of the season. There were encouraging signs throughout the match, even if the understanding between the forwards is still developing.
The result ultimately gave the home side another important win. Yet long after the final whistle, conversation revolved around the substitution rather than the scoreline—a reminder that when a superstar is involved, even routine coaching decisions can become headline news.

(CNN)As the United Kingdom celebrates giving at least one dose of the coronavirus vaccine to 15 million people and the EU surpasses 23 million doses distributed, several other European countries have not yet managed to put a single shot in arms.Kosovo, Montenegro, and Bosnia and Herzegovina are still waiting to receive their first vaccine shipments, while rollouts in Albania and Northern Macedonia have so far been limited to a few hundred people.The Western Balkan countries are key allies and possible future members of the European Union, but they have been left out of the bloc's immediate vaccine supply plans. The EU has secured more than 2.3 billion doses of various coronavirus vaccines and said it expects to share some of those with others. It also set aside €70 million ($85 million) for the Western Balkan region to purchase some of these doses in the future, but since its own rollout has been slow and delayed, those countries are still waiting. And as relatively wealthy countries -- at least in the global context -- they are also not a top priority for programs designed to help the world's poorest countries access vaccines. Read MoreThey have joined the COVAX program, which is aiming to make access to vaccines more equitable across the world, but the scheme's limited supply means its primary focus is on the 92 low and middle income countries that can't afford vaccines without funding and the Western Balkan countries are not among those. As self-financing COVAX members, they are set to receive 850,000 doses of a combination of coronavirus vaccines -- but when these might arrive is unclear. Why COVAX could become the most important acronym of 2021"The constant tragedy of the Western Balkans is that they are on the fringe," said Allison Carragher, a visiting scholar at Carnegie Europe, a global policy think tank. "They're all aspirational EU members, so they looked at the EU program first, but that has been mangled and delayed by supply chain issues." Many see the EU's omission of Western Balkan countries as a missed opportunity. "This is a small region in terms of population, which means that with a small investment in vaccines, the EU would have gained a lot in terms of soft power and influence in the region," said Alba Cela, the executive director of the Albanian Institute for International Studies. The fact that the EU didn't do this is "allowing for other actors to play a role," she added. Engjellushe Morina, senior policy fellow at the European Council on Foreign Relation said that this feeling of being left behind could have serious implications for the region's security. "Europe has really neglected the region for such a long time and it makes the region vulnerable to other external actors," she said, "This is where Russia comes in. This is where China comes in. This is where Turkey comes in, and they have filled in the void in different aspects."China steps inFaced with the possibility of a long wait, Serbia, the largest of the six Western Balkan countries, took matters into its own hands and looked for vaccines elsewhere. China and Russia were ready to step in.The Serbian government said China has so far supplied it with 1.5 million doses of the Sinopharm vaccine, catapulting the country of 7 million to the top ranks of the global vaccination race. Around 850,000 people have been vaccinated against Covid-19 in Serbia as of Monday, according to the government. The bulk of them received the Chinese vaccine, although the government said it has also received 90,000 doses of the Russian Sputnik V vaccine, and 40,950 of the Pfizer/BioNtech vaccine. The price Serbia paid for the vaccines has not been disclosed. Russia's Sputnik V vaccine hasn't yet been approved for use in the European Union. Adnan Cerimagic, senior analyst at ESI, a policy and research institute, said that without the Chinese vaccine, Serbia would be in a similar position to the rest of the region. "The entire success of the vaccination program relies on China and I think it has worked for China too, using Serbia as a place where they can have an impact in Europe ... the media reports say that thanks to the Chinese vaccine, Serbia is doing better than Germany and many other countries," he said. Carragher said the deal has been a win-win situation. "There's definitely a clear benefit for Serbia, not only reputationally, by being the top vaccinator on the continent of Europe, but also a legitimization of the government, which has been backsliding democratically," she said. "But here, you know, whenever you're the first in Europe, it looks like you're doing something good."China has a lot to gain too. "President Xi has said that his goal is to make this region the first region in the world that's fully covered by the Belt and Road initiative and from that perspective, alliances like this can be leveraged for other diplomatic priorities," Carragher said. The Belt and Road Initiative is China's signature global infrastructure policy which seeks to create new trade corridors linking China to Asia, Africa and Europe."It's also about the underlying principle behind the vaccine program, that's what they're also selling, legitimizing a lack of transparency and vaccines [being] widely deployed before actually being declared safe," Carragher added, pointing to the fact that Russia started its vaccination program before finalizing clinical trials, while China has not published their data in a peer-reviewed publication.China has struck back following criticism over its lack of transparency around vaccines, launching attacks on shots manufactured by other companies and countries. Russia was criticized for its early rollout of its then-unproved vaccine. But earlier this month, peer-reviewed results of Sputnik V's Phase 3 trial showed it has very high efficacy rates — providing something of a vindication for the country.China keeps promising its African allies that coronavirus vaccines for the continent are a priority. But where are they?The vaccines are not China's first venture into the Western Balkans either -- in the past decade, it has invested heavily in Serbia, Montenegro, Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina and Macedonia, financing large infrastructure and resource projects, including highways in Bosnia and Herzegovina and mines and factories in Serbia. It has also opened Confucius Institutes and university sinology departments across the region.Serbia's rollout has been such a success that it has allowed the government to engage in its own round of regional vaccine diplomacy. Last week, it donated 4,688 of its Pfizer/BioNTech doses to Northern Macedonia, which is still waiting for any of the more than 100,000 doses it secured through COVAX. The donation was touted as a major sign of cooperation, with the Serbian President Aleksandar Vucic and the Prime Minister of Northern Macedonia Zoran Zaev taking part in a handover ceremony at the border. On Wednesday, Serbia announced it would donate 4,000 doses of the Russian vaccine to Montenegro, which is also still waiting for its other deliveries.The donations are a symbolic gestures. But in this historically volatile, vulnerable region, symbols like this matter. When the Serbian government announced it would provide vaccines for Serbs living in Kosovo, the government of Kosovo accused it of playing politics with vaccines and "smuggling uncertified pharmaceutical products" into the country. Kosovo declared independence from Serbia in 2008 and most western countries, including the United States, recognize it as an independent state. Serbia, however, does not recognize Kosovo's independence.Kosovo has ordered over 100,000 doses of the vaccine from COVAX and is expecting deliveries from the EU as part of the €70 million scheme.The Serbian government also said it donated some of its doses of the Russian Sputnik V vaccine to the Republika Srpska -- one of the two highly autonomous entities that form Bosnia and Herzegovina. Vaccination of the first 1,000 health care workers started there last week, according to the website of the Republika Srpska's Health Ministry. The region has also ordered 400,000 doses of the Russian vaccine. The government of the country's Bosniak-Croat entity, Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina, said it ordered 800,000 doses through the EU scheme. Separately, the Presidency of Bosnia and Herzegovina, the body that governs the whole country, has ordered 1.2 million doses through the COVAX program.Albania has secured 500,000 doses of the Pfizer vaccine via a direct contract with the company, although according to the government, less than 10,000 have been delivered so far -- not enough to vaccinate the country's 23,000 health care workers. On top of its Pfizer deal, Albania has also ordered about 1.1 million doses through COVAX, ensuring it will have enough doses for its entire population.'The EU could have done more'While the EU has promised to help the region purchase Covid-19 vaccines, there's no indication yet of how this will work. But from a strategic and security point of view, the region is crucial for the EU. It is geographically entirely surrounded by EU member states, but remains vulnerable. Albania, Montenegro, North Macedonia and Serbia are all officially EU candidate countries, while Bosnia and Herzegovina and Kosovo are seen as potential future candidates.COVAX announces plan to distribute more than 330 million Covid-19 vaccine doses to developing nationsCela said this proximity to the EU is why this is not just about showing solidarity with poorer neighbors. "There's a lack of strategic vision. If this region is to be integrated -- and it is de facto integrated in the EU already, because we have free movement and we have a lot of people traveling back and forth, in practical terms -- it makes no sense for the EU not to provide the region with vaccines," she said, highlighting that having low vaccination rates in neighboring countries would represent a risk to the EU."It's a very hot topic here right now," Cela said, noting that the overwhelming feeling in the region is that the European Union could have done more for the Western Balkans.

As vaccination campaigns accelerated across much of North America and Western Europe, several countries in Central Asia found themselves still waiting for large-scale deliveries of Covid-19 vaccines.
While wealthier nations had already secured millions of doses through advance purchase agreements, countries such as Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan faced slower rollouts and uncertainty over future supplies. Many governments in the region joined international initiatives designed to improve vaccine access, but delivery schedules remained unpredictable.
Health officials warned that delays could leave vulnerable populations exposed for longer periods and slow economic recovery efforts. Several countries had planned vaccination campaigns around expected shipments that had yet to arrive, forcing authorities to revise their timelines.
Analysts said the situation highlighted broader inequalities in global vaccine distribution. Nations with greater financial resources were often able to negotiate direct deals with pharmaceutical companies, while smaller economies relied heavily on international partnerships and donation programs.
As shortages persisted, other powers moved to expand their influence in the region. Russia supplied doses of its Sputnik V vaccine to several neighboring countries, while China increased exports of its domestically produced vaccines. These deliveries were welcomed by governments eager to begin immunization programs and reduce pressure on healthcare systems.
In some cases, vaccine agreements carried diplomatic significance beyond public health. Observers noted that successful vaccine deliveries could strengthen political ties and improve perceptions of partner countries at a time when global competition for influence remained intense.
Despite logistical challenges, officials across the region expressed optimism that supplies would gradually increase in the coming months. Additional shipments were expected through international programs as production capacity expanded worldwide.
Public health experts argued that equitable vaccine access was not only a humanitarian concern but also a practical necessity. As long as large populations remained unvaccinated, they warned, the virus would continue to circulate and create risks for neighboring countries.
For governments still awaiting substantial deliveries, the hope was that international cooperation and increased production would eventually narrow the gap between countries that had secured vaccines early and those left waiting on the sidelines.

By the time vaccination campaigns were gathering pace across much of Europe and North America, several countries in Central Asia were still struggling to secure enough doses to launch widespread immunization efforts.
Governments in Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan had signed up for international vaccine-sharing initiatives and negotiated supply agreements where possible. Even so, deliveries often arrived later than expected, making it difficult to plan national rollouts with any certainty.
The contrast with wealthier countries was hard to ignore. While some nations had already vaccinated significant portions of their populations, others were still waiting for the first large shipments to reach clinics and hospitals.
The shortage created opportunities for countries seeking greater influence abroad. Russia and China stepped in with vaccine exports, providing doses that allowed several governments to begin protecting healthcare workers and other high-risk groups. Those deliveries were viewed not only as public health assistance but also as a form of diplomacy.
Regional experts pointed out that vaccines had become an important foreign-policy tool. When governments were unable to obtain supplies through traditional channels, partnerships built around vaccine access often carried political benefits as well.
Officials throughout the region remained hopeful that the situation would improve as global production increased. More doses were expected through international distribution programs, although exact timelines remained uncertain.
Health specialists continued to stress that unequal access posed a problem far beyond individual countries. A pandemic, they argued, cannot be fully controlled if large sections of the world's population remain unprotected.
For many governments, the challenge was not a lack of willingness to vaccinate their citizens but simply getting hold of enough doses. Until supplies became more widely available, they would remain dependent on a combination of international assistance, bilateral agreements, and patience.

Story highlightsA reader asks: Did a record drought trigger the crisis in Syria?Secretary of State John Kerry and climate researchers see a link, and a study says climate change worsened the droughtBut there were other droughts in countries such as Turkey and Iran, which did not see the same mass migrations and social unrestOn Fridays, CNN answers one of our audience's climate change questions as part of the Two Degrees series. Ask your question by filling out this online form. And sign up for the Two Degrees newsletter to learn more.  (CNN)The war in Syria has claimed the lives of more than 250,000 people.From Syrian President Bashar al-Assad to ISIS, many of the culprits are well known.But has climate change contributed to the bloodshed as well?That's a question CNN reader Robert Goldschmidt, from University Park, Florida, asked recently as part of our network's Two Degrees series on climate change.Specifically, he wanted to know if a record drought had "triggered" the crisis. Read MoreThere are high-profile backers of this view, among them U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry. "It's not a coincidence that immediately prior to the civil war in Syria, the country experienced its worst drought on record. As many as 1.5 million people migrated from Syria's farms to its cities, intensifying the political unrest that was just beginning to roil and boil in the region," Kerry said this week in a speech at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia. While drought is not the only cause of the Syrian conflict, the idea is that it has helped drive up social unrest. It increased unemployment, exacerbated famine and water scarcity, and forced farmers from their homes and into cities, where violence began.There's scientific evidence to support the case. In a 2014 study published in the journal Weather, Climate and Society, climate expert Peter Gleick wrote that "water and climatic conditions have played a direct role in the deterioration of Syria's economic conditions."Furthermore, the idea that the drought, which was the worst ever recorded in the region, was worsened by climate change in the region was strengthened in another study published this year. Researchers at the University of California, Santa Barbara, and Columbia University say the multiyear drought that helped drive the conflict was made "two to three times more likely" by man-made global warming. According to the latest report from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, the Middle East overall is expected to trend hotter and drier, which will make severe, multiyear droughts such as what occurred in Syria more likely to occur.  The drought started in 2006, years before violence broke out in Syria. By 2009, yields of wheat and barley fell by about one-half and two-thirds, respectively, and 800,000 people lost their basic food support. By 2011, the year violence erupted after a popular uprising against Assad, the situation had worsened and more than 1 million Syrians were forced into food insecurity. With rising political tensions, and families no longer able to ensure their futures on rural agricultural land, more than 1.5 million people migrated to cities, including Aleppo, Damascus and Homs, where many deaths occurred. While there is evidence to support the climate-violence link, not everyone agrees Syria's drought contributed to its civil war. One fact that casts doubt: Other droughts occurred in other countries such as Turkey and Iran, which did not see the same mass migrations and social unrest. Others simply say the link between climate change and violence is prone to overstatement. And researchers stress the importance of acknowledging that war often emerges from many factors.The U.S. Department of Defense takes the link seriously, though, calling climate change a "threat multiplier." That means its effect is greatest in areas that are already environmentally and socially unstable.  Kerry and other officials stress the connection is real. "It would be better for all of us if I was exaggerating the urgency of this threat, but the science tells us unequivocally that those who continue to make climate change a political fight put us all at risk," Kerry said.Those comments -- and this discussion -- come at a critical time. Negotiators from 195 countries will gather in Paris on November 30 to try to work toward an agreement to curb the rise in global temperatures. Perhaps that agreement, if successful, also could help reduce climate change's contribution to drought, and potentially violence, around the world. Story highlights
	•	A reader asks whether extreme heat contributed to recent migration pressures in Central America
	•	Researchers say climate change has increased the likelihood of prolonged droughts in the region
	•	Some experts argue environmental stress alone cannot explain migration and political instability
	•	Governments and international organizations increasingly describe climate change as a risk multiplier
(CNN) Can climate change help explain why large numbers of people leave their homes and migrate elsewhere?
That's the question submitted by a CNN reader as part of our ongoing climate series. Specifically, the reader asked whether worsening drought conditions in Central America have played a role in recent waves of migration toward the United States.
A growing number of policymakers and researchers believe the answer is at least partly yes.
Several international organizations have pointed to climate-related pressures, including prolonged droughts, crop failures and water shortages, as factors that can intensify existing social and economic problems. While these environmental stresses are rarely viewed as the sole cause of migration, experts say they can make already difficult circumstances much worse.
Supporters of this view often point to the so-called "Dry Corridor," a region stretching across parts of Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador and Nicaragua. In recent years, farmers there have experienced repeated harvest failures linked to irregular rainfall patterns and unusually high temperatures.
According to reports from humanitarian organizations, many rural families depend heavily on small-scale agriculture for food and income. When crops fail repeatedly, households may struggle to meet basic needs, prompting some residents to move to urban areas or seek opportunities abroad.
Scientific research has strengthened concerns about the role of climate change in these developments. Several studies have found that rising global temperatures are increasing the likelihood and severity of drought conditions in parts of Central America. Climate models also suggest that some regions may experience more frequent periods of extreme heat and water stress in the future.
The argument is not that climate change directly causes migration. Rather, researchers describe a chain of effects. Reduced agricultural productivity can contribute to economic hardship, which may increase food insecurity, debt and displacement. In communities already facing poverty, crime or weak institutions, those pressures can become particularly significant.
Still, not all experts agree on the strength of the connection.
Critics note that migration decisions are influenced by many factors, including economic opportunities, family networks, public safety and government policies. They argue that focusing too heavily on climate risks can oversimplify complex social realities.
Others point out that droughts occur in many regions around the world without producing comparable migration patterns. In their view, environmental conditions are only one piece of a much larger puzzle.
Many researchers acknowledge those concerns. They emphasize that migration rarely has a single cause and that climate-related factors usually interact with political, economic and demographic conditions already present in a society.
Even so, governments and international agencies increasingly view climate change as a force that can amplify existing vulnerabilities. Security analysts often describe it as a "threat multiplier," meaning it can worsen tensions and challenges that already exist rather than create them from scratch.
The discussion comes as countries continue debating how to address global warming and prepare for its consequences. Beyond rising temperatures and sea levels, policymakers are paying growing attention to how environmental change may affect food systems, economic stability and human migration.
Whether climate change is a primary driver or a contributing factor, many experts agree that understanding its role will become increasingly important as the planet continues to warm.

Story highlights
	•	A reader asks whether climate change is contributing to migration from Central America
	•	Research links rising temperatures and drought to agricultural losses in the region
	•	Experts caution that migration is driven by multiple factors, not environmental conditions alone
	•	Climate change is increasingly viewed as a factor that can worsen existing social pressures
(CNN) When people leave their homes in search of better opportunities or greater security, how much of that decision can be traced back to climate change?
That question came from a CNN reader who wanted to know whether worsening droughts in Central America have contributed to the migration flows seen in recent years.
Many scientists and policy experts believe environmental conditions are playing a role, although the relationship is far from straightforward.
One region frequently mentioned in this discussion is Central America's "Dry Corridor," where repeated droughts and unpredictable rainfall have affected farming communities across several countries. For families that rely on agriculture, poor harvests can mean lost income, food shortages and growing financial uncertainty.
As those challenges accumulate, some people relocate within their own countries, while others consider moving abroad. Humanitarian organizations have documented cases in which crop failures and water scarcity contributed to decisions to migrate.
Research has added weight to these concerns. A number of studies suggest that climate change is increasing the likelihood of extreme heat and prolonged drought in parts of the region. Scientists warn that these trends may continue as global temperatures rise.
Most researchers, however, stop short of claiming that climate change directly causes migration. Instead, they describe it as one factor among many. Environmental stress can make life more difficult, but economic conditions, public safety, employment opportunities and family connections also influence whether people decide to move.
That distinction is important because migration patterns are rarely explained by a single cause. Communities facing climate-related hardships often confront other challenges at the same time, making it difficult to separate one influence from another.
Some experts remain skeptical of efforts to draw a strong connection between climate change and migration. They note that droughts occur in many places without leading to large-scale population movements. From this perspective, local political and economic conditions may be just as important as environmental factors.
Even researchers who see a climate link generally agree that the issue is complex. Climate change may not initiate a crisis on its own, but it can increase pressure on societies that are already struggling with poverty, weak institutions or limited resources.
That is why governments and international organizations increasingly refer to climate change as a "threat multiplier." The term reflects the idea that environmental changes can intensify existing problems rather than create entirely new ones.
As global leaders continue debating climate policy, questions about migration are becoming a larger part of the conversation. Rising temperatures affect more than weather patterns; they can influence livelihoods, food production and the long-term stability of communities.
The exact role climate change plays in migration remains the subject of ongoing research. What is becoming clearer, however, is that environmental pressures are increasingly intertwined with broader social and economic challenges, making them difficult to ignore.
